Sparkloop · /spark_script · per-idea slideshow

Pick one voice per produced idea

Each produced idea from /spark_produce becomes a slide. Inside the slide: 10 voice variants (Hormozi PAS ×5 + Gary Vee ×5, each with brand-polish iteration showing surgical strikethrough + insertion), Claude rating, Heat metric, and severity-tagged skeptic comments. Pick one variant per slide. Last pick wraps Sparkloop.

Idea 1 of 24 P1-A11-1 3 places where your CNY can become USD
Card P1-A11Pillar P1Short-form 60s · bilingualTopic 01Mechanism-led
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 131 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. You hit send on a CNY payment to your factory. By the time it lands, your CNY has been converted three different times. Once. Twice. Three times. Each conversion takes a 1% spread. Compound that. On a 50,000 dollar order, you just lost 1,500 dollars to invisible currency hops you didn't know existed. Do it twice a month. That's 36,000 a year. Over five years, your bank quietly took 180,000 from your business through conversion points you never saw. That's not a fee. That's the spread on three separate exchange rate hops. Sender bank. Correspondent. Beneficiary bank. Each one takes a bite. Listen. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. No intermediary conversion. No double conversion at the supplier's end. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Your supplier gets exactly what you sent. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. You hit send on a CNY payment to your factory. By the time it lands, your CNY has been converted three different times. Once. Twice. Three times. Each conversion takes a 1% spread. Compound that. On a 50,000 dollar order, you just lost 1,500 dollars to invisible currency hops you didn't know existed. Do it twice a month. That's 36,000 a year. Over five years, your bank quietly took 180,000 from your business through conversion points you never saw. That's not a fee. That's the spread on three separate exchange rate hops. Sender bank. Correspondent. Beneficiary bank. Each one takes a bite. Listen. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. No intermediary conversion. No double conversion at the supplier's end. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Your supplier gets exactly what you sent. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The compounding math (1% three times = 1,500 on 50K, then 36K/year, 180K/5yr) is cooked. Spreads at each hop are variable and not always cumulative because some hops don't apply on every wire. The 180K-over-5-years number is hypothetical extrapolation presented as fact, which is the biggest trust risk. Mechanism is real but quantification overreaches.

  • 180k in 5 years? Show me proof la, not just calculator. My business not so big also.
  • Every conversion 1%? My bank told me only one conversion happen, not three. Confusing.
  • Got license in Malaysia or not? Wise can blocked me last time, dont want repeat.
  • Math too perfect lah, real world got weekend, public holiday, all kinds of delay.
  • Sound smart but I scroll TikTok at 11pm not got time to verify. Just give me one comparison screenshot.
#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 123 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatLow

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk for every business owner paying suppliers in China. You sent CNY last week. Your supplier called and said they got USD. You're confused. You called your bank. They said it was the correspondent. You called the correspondent. They said it was the receiving bank. The receiving bank said the sender bank should have sent CNY. Nobody can tell you where the conversion happened. Because it could have happened at any of three places. Sender bank converts CNY to USD upfront. Or correspondent never had CNY. Or receiving bank converted on arrival. Three possible conversion points. Three spreads. Zero transparency. I see this every single week. Stop guessing where your money got converted. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. Same-day. One rate. No phone-tag with three different banks. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Real talk for everyFor every business owner paying suppliers in China. You sent CNY last week. Your supplier called and said they got USD. You're confused. You called your bank. They said it was the correspondent. You called the correspondent. They said it was the receiving bank. The receiving bank said the sender bank should have sent CNY. Nobody can tell you where the conversion happened. Because it could have happened at any of three places. Sender bank converts CNY to USD upfront. Or correspondent never had CNY. Or receiving bank converted on arrival. Three possible conversion points. Three spreads. Zero transparency. I see thisThis happens every single week. Stop guessing where your money got converted. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. Same-day. One rate. No phone-tag with three different banks. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

This is the lowest-risk script in the set. It describes a real scenario (banks blaming each other over routing-related FX surprises) which any SMB business owner can relate to without needing to verify hard numbers. No specific dollar figures, no compounded math claims. 'This happens every single week' is the only mild overstatement. CTA is appropriately direct without overclaiming.

  • Eh this one I felt before, called my bank they really blame the other party. Relatable.
  • Who is WorldFirst tho. Never see them in Malaysia. Got physical office?
  • Every single week got people facing this? Sounds exaggerated but maybe.
  • Same-day one rate, ok but my bank also got same-day option. Difference how big?
  • No phone-tag part real lah. I waste 2 hours last month on this exact thing.
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 127 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. If you're a business owner sending CNY to your Chinese supplier, there are three places your CNY can quietly turn into USD before it ever lands. Place one. Your sender bank converts CNY to USD upfront because USD is cheaper for them to route. Place two. The correspondent bank in the middle holds only USD, so it converts again. Place three. Your supplier's receiving bank gets USD and converts back to CNY at their bank's rate. That's three conversion points. Three spreads. Three margins your bank never tells you about. Now your supplier is short. They're calling you asking why they got 47,000 dollars when you sent the equivalent of 50,000. Stop. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. Zero double conversion. Same-day. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. If you're a business owner sending CNY to your Chinese supplier, there are three places your CNY can quietly turn into USD before it ever lands. Place one. Your sender bank converts CNY to USD upfront because USD is cheaper for them to route. Place two. The correspondent bank in the middle holds only USD, so it converts again. Place three. Your supplier's receiving bank gets USD and converts back to CNY at their bank's rate. That's three conversion points. Three spreads. Three margins your bank never tells you about. Now your supplier is short. They're calling you asking why they got 47,000 dollars when you sent the equivalent of 50,000. Stop. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. Zero double conversion. Same-day. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core mechanism (three possible conversion points: sender, correspondent, beneficiary) is technically accurate and well-supported by SWIFT routing reality. The '47,000 instead of 50,000' figure is illustrative rather than a verified universal number, and 'WorldFirst sends CNY direct' is broadly true via CNAPS rails but glosses over that not every CNY corridor is the same. 'One rate. Zero double conversion.' is slightly absolute for the polished version.

  • 47k from 50k? Where this number come from lah. My bank not so bad.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never see this brand in Malaysia. Got license here or not?
  • Sound like ad la. Where the proof of zero double conversion?
  • My supplier never complain about getting USD. Maybe this only happen to small businesses?
  • Same day? Maybank also say same day but always next day. So how?
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 146 words

Accusation opener + brutal specificity

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

Your bank stole 1,500 dollars from your last CNY payment to China and you have no idea where it went. Here's how. You sent CNY. Or so you thought. Your sender bank converted it to USD first because USD is what their pipes carry. That's one conversion. Spread one. Then it hit a correspondent bank in Hong Kong or New York. They only hold USD. They held it as USD. Sent it forward as USD. Then your supplier's bank in Shenzhen received USD. They converted it back to CNY at their rate. That's spread three. Three rate margins on one wire. Your supplier got 47,000 dollars worth of CNY instead of 50,000. They're short. They're asking questions. You eat the loss or you wire the difference. Stop signing off on this. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. One conversion. Or none, if you're holding CNY already. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Your bank stolepocketed 1,500 dollars from your last CNY payment to China and you have no idea where it went. Here's how. You sent CNY. Or so you thought. Your sender bank converted it to USD first because USD is what their pipes carry. That's one conversion. Spread one. Then it hit a correspondent bank in Hong Kong or New York. They only hold USD. They held it as USD. Sent it forward as USD. Then your supplier's bank in Shenzhen received USD. They converted it back to CNY at their rate. That's spread three. Three rate margins on one wire. Your supplier got 47,000 dollars worth of CNY instead of 50,000. They're short. They're asking questions. You eat the loss or you wire the difference. Stop signing off on this. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. One conversion. Or none, if you're holding CNY already. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'Your bank pocketed 1,500 dollars' is presented as fact but it's a hypothetical figure. The specific city naming (Hong Kong, New York, Shenzhen) makes it feel concrete but it's also presumptive about routing. Saying 'WorldFirst sends CNY direct. Or none, if you're holding CNY already' is mostly true via multi-currency wallet logic but compresses several different product behaviours.

  • Pocketed 1500? My last wire only short 200 ringgit. Number very different leh.
  • Hong Kong correspondent? My bank go via Singapore. So which is correct?
  • Never heard WorldFirst. Try Wise also got blocked, scared to try new one.
  • Spread three? My bank only show one rate on the slip. Where the other two come from?
  • Asking questions also okay lah, my supplier and me always settle in next order.
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 145 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you wire money to China, you need to hear this. Real talk. There are three places your CNY can become USD and you're paying a spread at every single one of them. One. Your sender bank converts to USD before sending because their rails carry USD, not CNY. You eat that spread. Two. The intermediary correspondent bank only holds USD. So if any CNY got through, it gets converted again here. You eat that spread. Three. Your supplier's bank receives USD and converts to CNY locally at their rate, not yours. You eat that spread too. Three spreads. One wire. Your supplier gets less than you sent. Or worse, they get USD and you find out later they paid their factory short. Stop sending payments through banks that don't carry CNY. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. If you're paying suppliers in China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

If you wire money to China, you need to hear this. Real talk. There are three places your CNY can become USD and you're paying a spread at every single one of them. One. Your sender bank converts to USD before sending because their rails carry USD, not CNY. You eat that spread. Two. The intermediary correspondent bank only holds USD. So if any CNY got through, it gets converted again here. You eat that spread. Three. Your supplier's bank receives USD and converts to CNY locally at their rate, not yours. You eat that spread too. Three spreads. One wire. Your supplier gets less than you sent. Or worse, they get USD and you find out later they paid their factory short. Stop sending payments through banks that don't carry CNY. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. If you're paying suppliers in China, you need WorldFirst. Period. WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Three-spread framing is consistent with reality. 'Worse, they get USD and you find out later they paid their factory short' is plausible but presented as common when it's a worst-case scenario. CTA softening 'WorldFirst is worth a look' is appropriately reserved. The script doesn't claim a specific dollar amount which reduces trust risk compared to PH2/PH3.

  • Supplier paid factory short? Got real example or just scary story?
  • Three spreads concept okay but who is WorldFirst, never see in MY market.
  • I send small amount usually, like 5k USD. Worth using new platform for that?
  • If WF really this good why my forwarder never recommend? They use what?
  • Worth a look soft sell better. At least not pushing me to download right away.
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 126 words

I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. There's no such thing as a CNY wire from most banks. You think you're sending CNY. You're sending USD that gets converted to CNY somewhere down the line. And there are three places that can happen. Your sender bank. The correspondent. The supplier's bank. Each conversion point. Each spread. 1% here, 1% there, 0.5% at the end. That's 2.5% on every payment. On 50,000 dollars that's 1,250 gone. Per wire. Multiply that across a year. 30,000 dollars vanished into currency conversion you didn't know was happening. That's a hire. That's a container. That's your margin. WorldFirst carries CNY natively. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. One conversion. Sometimes zero if you're holding CNY in the account already. The difference goes back into your business. Where it belongs. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

I'll say it. There's no such thing as a CNY wire from most banks. You think you're sending CNY. You're sending USD that gets converted to CNY somewhere down the line. And there are three places that can happen. Your sender bank. The correspondent. The supplier's bank. Each conversion point. Each spread. 1% here, 1% there, 0.5% at the end. That's 2.5% on every payment. On 50,000 dollars that's 1,250 gone. Per wire. Multiply that across a year. 30,000 dollars vanished into currency conversion you didn't know was happening. That's a hire. That's a container. That's your margin. WorldFirst carries CNY natively. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. One conversion. Sometimes zero if you're holding CNY in the account already. The difference goes back into your business. Where it belongs. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 'no such thing as a CNY wire from most banks' opener is a strong overstatement (some banks do offer CNH direct corridors). The compound math 1+1+0.5=2.5%, then 1,250/wire, then 30K/year is presented as universal when it's a stacked hypothetical. 'Zero conversion if you're holding CNY already' is product-accurate but conflates wallet hold with wire send.

  • No such thing as CNY wire from most banks? But OCBC give me CNH rate quote leh. Confused.
  • 30k a year? My total payments not even 30k. Number not relate to my size.
  • WorldFirst carries CNY natively means what? They got China license?
  • Sound like ad copy lah. Where the real customer screenshot or testimonial?
  • Same complaint about bank but solution not clear. Just search WorldFirst? Then what?
#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 140 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's the problem most business owners paying Chinese suppliers don't understand. You think you sent CNY. You didn't. Here's what actually happened. Step one. Your sender bank doesn't have a direct CNY rail. They convert your money to USD on day one. Step two. The correspondent bank in the middle, the one routing the wire, only carries USD. So the USD stays USD. Step three. Your supplier's bank receives USD and has to convert it to CNY locally because your supplier holds a CNY account. Three conversion points. Each one with a spread. Each one taking 0.5 to 1.5% silently. On 50,000 dollars, that's 1,500 dollars in invisible spread. Your supplier ends up holding USD they didn't ask for, or shorter CNY than expected. Stop. WorldFirst routes CNY directly to Chinese accounts. One rate. No middleman conversion. Same-day delivery. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the problem most business owners paying Chinese suppliers don't understand. You think you sent CNY. You didn't. Here's what actually happened. Step one. Your sender bank doesn't have a direct CNY rail. They convert your money to USD on day one. Step two. The correspondent bank in the middle, the one routing the wire, only carries USD. So the USD stays USD. Step three. Your supplier's bank receives USD and has to convert it to CNY locally because your supplier holds a CNY account. Three conversion points. Each one with a spread. Each one taking 0.5 to 1.5% silently. On 50,000 dollars, that's 1,500 dollars in invisible spread. Your supplier ends up holding USD they didn't ask for, or shorter CNY than expected. Stop. WorldFirst routes CNY directly to Chinese accounts. One rate. No middleman conversion. Same-day delivery. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Mechanism explanation is the most technically defensible of the set (sender bank lacking CNY rails, correspondent carrying only USD, beneficiary bank converting locally). '0.5 to 1.5%' is presented as a range which is more honest than a single fake number. 'WorldFirst routes CNY directly' is generally accurate. The weakest beat is the absolute 'no middleman conversion' which is mostly but not always true depending on the destination bank.

  • Range better lah, 0.5 to 1.5%. But still where the proof?
  • Sender bank no CNY rail? Maybank can send CNY ah, I thought.
  • Who regulate WorldFirst? Important question. Wise got license here, WF got or not?
  • Cobi or who is on this video also dont know, no face also weird.
  • Okay this one make sense. But still want to see one screenshot comparison before I download.

Classic Stop / Real-talk opener

Lost some of the original Gary Vee punch after polish. Still works but feels closer to baseline.

Stop. Real talk for every business owner sending CNY to China. You don't actually send CNY. You think you do. You don't. There are three places your money gets converted to USD on the way over. Your sender bank. The correspondent bank in the middle. Your supplier's receiving bank. Each one takes a spread. Each one eats your margin. #9 PG3 Gary Vee Opinion-led 122 words

Stop using your bank / right-tool reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Pure original

Stop using your bank to send CNY. I'm serious. Your bank doesn't carry CNY rails. It carries USD rails. So when you ask it to send CNY, it doesn't send CNY. It sends USD and hopes a conversion happens somewhere down the line. Three somewheres, actually. Sender side conversion. Correspondent bank conversion. Beneficiary bank conversion. Each one a spread. Each one a cost. This is the wrong tool. You wouldn't use a fax machine to send a video. So stop using a USD-rail bank to send CNY. WorldFirst is built on CNY-direct rails. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. Your supplier receives CNY. Not USD. Not double-converted. Use the right tool for the job. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop using your bank to send CNY. I'm seriousYour bank isn't the right tool for sending CNY. Your bank doesn't carry CNY rails. It carries USD rails. So when you ask it to send CNY, it doesn't send CNY. It sends USD and hopes a conversion happens somewhere down the line. Three somewheres, actually. Sender side conversion. Correspondent bank conversion. Beneficiary bank conversion. Each one a spread. Each one a cost. This is the wrong tool. You wouldn't use a fax machine to send a video. So stop using aa USD-rail bank to send CNYisn't the right tool for sending CNY. WorldFirst is built on CNY-direct rails. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. Your supplier receives CNY. Not USD. Not double-converted. Use the right tool for the job. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 'right tool' analogy is rhetorical not fact-based, so lower fact-risk. 'Your bank doesn't carry CNY rails. It carries USD rails' is broadly true for many SEA banks but not universal (Maybank, OCBC, DBS do have CNH corridors). 'Built on CNY-direct rails' for WorldFirst is product-accurate. Weakest spot is the binary 'USD-rail vs CNY-rail' framing which is too clean for reality.

  • Fax machine vs video, ok funny analogy. But my bank Maybank got CNH lah.
  • USD-rail bank, CNY-rail platform, sound technical. Where I verify which one my bank is?
  • Who is WorldFirst? Three years I scroll TikTok, never see. Suddenly now? Sus.
  • Use right tool ok but I want price comparison not analogy. Show me one wire side by side.
  • Margin depends on it true lah but how big is the difference really? Need real number.
#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 130 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about international wires to China. The conversion doesn't happen where you think it happens. You assume your bank converts CNY at the front and sends CNY across. Wrong. Three different banks can convert your money at three different points. Sender bank. Correspondent bank in the middle. Beneficiary bank at the end. Each one books its own rate. Each one takes its own spread. You'll never see this on a statement. You'll just notice your supplier got less than expected. Or worse, your supplier received USD and had to convert again at their own bank. Now there's a fourth conversion. Four spreads on one wire. This is how it actually works. Every single time. WorldFirst doesn't play this game. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Same-day. One rate. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling you about international wires to China. The conversion doesn't happen where you think it happens. You assume your bank converts CNY at the front and sends CNY across. Wrong. Three different banks can convert your money at three different points. Sender bank. Correspondent bank in the middle. Beneficiary bank at the end. Each one books its own rate. Each one takes its own spread. You'll never see this on a statement. You'll just notice your supplier got less than expected. Or worse, your supplier received USD and had to convert again at their own bank. Now there's a fourth conversion. Four spreads on one wire. This is how it actually works. Every single time. WorldFirst doesn't play this gameis built differently. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Same-day. One rate. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'Three different banks can convert at three different points' is accurate. The 'fourth conversion' scenario (supplier receiving USD then converting again locally) is a real worst-case but framed as universal. 'You'll never see this on a statement' is mostly true for the FX margin component. 'WorldFirst is built differently. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts' is product-accurate. Overall the conspiratorial frame is the main trust risk because it makes the script sound like an ad.

  • Fourth conversion? Now four already? Earlier video say three. Make up your mind.
  • Sound like ad with the 'every single time' line. Real talk got nuance la.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Got Malaysia license or not, that's the main question.
  • Conspiratorial tone make me suspicious. If real, where the news article reporting this?
  • One rate is good but I want see the rate vs my Maybank rate. Just show la.
Card P1-A13Pillar P1Short-form 30s · bilingualTopic 02Tutorial
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 147 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. You wire 10K to your Chinese supplier. The receipt says fee waived. You smile. You shouldn't. The rate on that wire was 3 percent off Google's mid-market rate. That's 300 dollars your bank just took. Not as a fee. As a margin. Buried inside the exchange rate. Now do that twice a month. 600 a month. 7,200 a year. Run a business that wires 30K a month for five years and your bank quietly pocketed 54,000 dollars from you. That's a forklift. That's a year of warehouse rent. That's a hire. And the kicker. 35 percent of business owners surveyed already call this their top frustration, and most still don't know it's 300 bucks per 10K. Listen. WorldFirst sends the same wire at near mid-market rates. No hidden margin baked into the rate. The 300 stays with you. Every wire. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. You wire 10K to your Chinese supplier. The receipt says fee waived. You smile. You shouldn't. The rate on that wire was 3 percent off Google's mid-market rate. That's 300 dollars your bank just took. Not as a fee. As a margin. Buried inside the exchange rate. Now do that twice a month. 600 a month. 7,200 a year. Run a business that wires 30K a month for five years and your bank quietly pocketedtook 54,000 dollars from you. That's a forklift. That's a year of warehouse rent. That's a hire. And the kicker. 35 percent of business owners surveyed already call this their top frustration, and most still don't know it's 300 bucks per 10K. Listen. WorldFirst sends the same wire at near mid-market rates. No hidden margin baked into the rate. The 300 stays with you. Every wire. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 5 year compounding extrapolation (54,000 over five years on 30K monthly volume) is the highest-risk claim in the set. It assumes the 3 percent margin holds constant on every wire for 60 straight months, which any SMB will see as theoretical. 'Forklift / warehouse rent / hire' are emotive anchors layered on top of an unverifiable extrapolation. The 35 percent Airwallex stat is real and well-sourced, but pairing it with the compound number makes the whole piece feel like a sales pitch dressed as a stat.

  • 54K over 5 years? You assume bank never lower rate, never negotiate. Not reality bro.
  • Wah forklift warehouse rent. Add more drama can or not.
  • Twice a month 10K? Some month I send 50K, some month I send nothing. Math wrong.
  • If 35% complain why my supplier never complain, only this video complain.
  • Where the Airwallex survey link? Numbers without source = ad.
#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 118 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk for every business owner sending money to China. Your bank is not your friend on this one. They send you a wire confirmation that says fee waived. You think they're doing you a favour. They're not. The 3 percent margin baked into the FX rate already covered the fee three times over. On a 10K wire that's 300 dollars. You call the bank. They show you the receipt. Fee waived. They're right. The money came out of the rate. They never have to call it a fee. Every wire. Same trick. I see it every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. Near mid-market rates. The rate Google shows. Same supplier. Same 10K. The 300 stays in your business. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Real talk for everyFor every business owner sending money to China. Your bank is not your friend on this one. They send you a wire confirmation that says fee waived. You think they're doing you a favour. They're not. The 3 percent margin baked into the FX rate already covered the fee three times over. On a 10K wire that's 300 dollars. You call the bank. They show you the receipt. Fee waived. They're right. The money came out of the rate. They never have to call it a fee. Every wire. Same trickpattern. I see itThis happens every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. Near mid-market rates. The rate Google shows. Same supplier. Same 10K. The 300 stays in your business. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Frame is 'bank is not your friend' which is opinion but well-anchored to the mechanism. The repeated 'every wire, same pattern' is fair characterisation since this is industry standard practice, not a one-off. 'I see this every week' is replaced with 'this happens every week' in the brand version, which is sensible because the creator isn't actually a banker. WF claim is well-bounded. Decent piece overall, just needs the 3 percent caveat.

  • Bank not friend la, of course. But WorldFirst is friend? Also business.
  • Margin covered fee 3 times over. Where got such clean number bro.
  • Every week every single week. Dramatic. Just say it happens often, calm down.
  • Ok if I switch to WorldFirst, what about supplier on Alipay only, can transfer?
  • I check rate sometimes. Bank give negotiated rate when wire big. So 300 not always.
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 149 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. If you're a business owner wiring money to Chinese suppliers, your bank has two ways to charge you. One you see. One you don't. Here's the deal. The wire fee is visible. 25 bucks. 40 bucks. Whatever your bank charges. That's the number you stare at. The rate margin is invisible. 1 to 3 percent of the wire amount. Buried inside the FX rate itself. On a 10K wire at 3 percent, that's 300 dollars gone. No line item. No receipt. Nobody itemises it because they don't have to. Your confirmation literally says fee waived while the rate is 3 percent off Google's mid-market. Both facts coexist legally. Stop trusting the fee line. Start checking the rate. WorldFirst gives you near mid-market rates. Same rate Google shows you. If you're sending money to China and you're still reading the fee line, you're missing where the cost actually lives. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. If you're a business owner wiring money to Chinese suppliers, your bank has two ways to charge you. One you see. One you don't. Here's the deal. The wire fee is visible. 25 bucks. 40 bucks. Whatever your bank charges. That's the number you stare at. The rate margin is invisible. 1 to 3 percent of the wire amount. Buried inside the FX rate itself. On a 10K wire at 3 percent, that's 300 dollars gone. No line item. No receipt. Nobody itemises it because they don't have to. Your confirmation literally says fee waived while the rate is 3 percent off Google's mid-market. Both facts coexist legally. Stop trusting the fee line. Start checking the rateWorth checking the rate, not just the fee line. WorldFirst gives you near mid-market rates. Same rate Google shows you. If you're sending money to China and you're still reading the fee line, you're missing where the cost actually lives. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core mechanism (fee column zero while FX rate carries a 1 to 3 percent margin) is accurate and matches published industry data including the Airwallex 35 percent figure and the Bancoli 3 percent / $300 per 10K case. The phrase 'both facts coexist legally' is true, but the implied universality of 3 percent is risky. Some banks run 1 to 1.5 percent on USD CNY for higher-volume SME clients, so 300 dollars is a worst-case rather than typical. 'Near mid-market rates' on the WF side is also softer than the implication that the full 300 always stays with you.

  • 3 percent ah? My Maybank give me 1.2 only la, where you get 3 from?
  • Bro every video say 300 dollars 300 dollars. Show me actual receipt comparison la.
  • WorldFirst who? Never see them before, got Bank Negara license or not?
  • Google mid market also not the rate I get bro, even your WorldFirst sure got margin.
  • Ok so I save 300. Then customer support, app crash, transfer fail how?
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 133 words

Accusation opener

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

Your bank charged you 300 dollars on your last wire to China and the confirmation said fee waived. Here's how. You wired 10K. The fee column said zero. You felt clever. You missed it. The rate they applied was 3 percent off the real one. The one Google shows. 300 dollars pulled straight out of the rate, never landed as a line item, never refunded. Your bank gets to advertise no transfer fee and keep the money. Legal. Standard. Every business owner who only checks the fee column gets the same treatment. I'll say it. This isn't a glitch. This is the product. WorldFirst is different. Near mid-market rates. The same number Google shows when you search USD CNY. The 300 stays in your account. Stop reading the fee column. Read the rate. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Your bank charged you 300 dollars on your last wire to China and the confirmation said fee waived. Here's how. You wired 10K. The fee column said zero. You felt clever. You missed it. The rate they applied was 3 percent off the real one. The one Google shows. 300 dollars pulled straight out of the rate, never landed as a line item, never refunded. Your bank gets to advertise no transfer fee and keep the money. Legal. Standard. Every business owner who only checks the fee column gets the same treatment. I'll say it. This isn't a glitch. This is the product. WorldFirst is different. Near mid-market rates. The same number Google shows when you search USD CNY. The 300 stays in your account. Stop reading the fee column. ReadWorth reading the rate, not just the fee column. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'Charged you 300 dollars' is borderline because it's technically true on a 10K wire at 3 percent margin, but the casual reader may interpret 'charged' as a fee, which it legally isn't (it's a rate spread). 'Legal. Standard.' is fair framing. 'This isn't a glitch, this is the product' is opinion presented as fact and is defensible but punchy. WF claim 'the 300 stays in your account' is overconfident because WF still carries its own margin, just smaller.

  • Accuse my bank like this got proof or not, kena sue you know.
  • Fee waived = no fee la, where got problem. You also can choose another bank.
  • Sound very angry. Why ah? You work for WorldFirst is it.
  • Aiyo my CIMB I check rate every time. Not every operator is dumb la.
  • 300 dollar on 10K. What about smaller wire like 2K 3K, also 300 ah?

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you wire money to China, you need to hear this. Real talk. Last time you sent 10K to a supplier, two things happened and you only checked one of them. One. Your bank waived the wire fee. You saw fee zero on the confirmation. Win. Two. Your bank quoted you a rate 3 percent off Google's mid-market rate and pocketed 300 dollars in the spread. You didn't see that anywhere. No line item. No receipt. The wire confirmation literally says fee waived next to a rate that just cost you 300 bucks. Both facts are legal. Both facts are normal. 35 percent of business owners say hidden fees are their top frustration, and most still only check the fee line. Stop checking the fee line. Start checking the rate. WorldFirst gives you near mid-market rates. The exact number Google shows. Same supplier. Same 10K. The 300 stays with you. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. Your bank's most profitable product is the rate, not the fee. Watch the math. You wire 10K to your supplier in Shenzhen. The fee column says zero. The rate is 3 percent off Google's mid-market rate. That's 300 dollars they kept and never told you. One wire. Now run it. Two wires a month is 600. 7,200 a year. Over five years that's 36,000 dollars sitting in your bank's pocket. From one business. Pulled out of the FX rate. The fee line is the magic show. The rate is the trick. WorldFirst uses near mid-market rates. Same number Google shows. The 300 stays with you. Where it should be. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

I'll say it. Your bank's most profitable product is the rate, not the fee. Watch the math. You wire 10K to your supplier in Shenzhen. The fee column says zero. The rate is 3 percent off Google's mid-market rate. That's 300 dollars they kept and never told you. One wire. Now run it. Two wires a month is 600. 7,200 a year. Over five years that's 36,000 dollars sitting in your bank's pocket. From one business. Pulled out of the FX rate. The fee line is the magic show. The rate is the trick. WorldFirst uses near mid-market rates. Same number Google shows. The 300 stays with you. Where it should be. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Same compounding-math risk as PH2. 36K over 5 years on 10K twice a month is extrapolation that assumes the margin stays constant for 60 months. 'Magic show / trick' framing is strong opinion presented as fact, fair for Gary Vee voice but lands as bias to a skeptical viewer. Stat-free claims like 'pulled out of the FX rate' are technically correct but need supporting visual for credibility.

#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 146 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's the problem most business owners don't realise when they pay Chinese suppliers. The fee is not the cost. You see fee waived on the wire confirmation. You think the transfer was free. Wrong. Banks have two charging mechanisms running in parallel. Mechanism one is the visible wire fee. 25 to 40 bucks. Sometimes waived as a promo. Mechanism two is the FX rate margin. 1 to 3 percent of the wire amount, embedded in the exchange rate. Not a fee. A margin. Same money. Different label. On a 10K wire at 3 percent margin, that's 300 dollars. Same outcome as a 300 dollar fee. No itemisation. No disclosure. The two facts coexist legally. The bank waived the fee. The bank also kept 300 in the rate. Stop. WorldFirst skips the second mechanism. Near mid-market rates. The rate Google shows when you check USD CNY. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the problem most business owners don't realise when they pay Chinese suppliers. The fee is not the cost. You see fee waived on the wire confirmation. You think the transfer was free. Wrong. Banks have two charging mechanisms running in parallel. Mechanism one is the visible wire fee. 25 to 40 bucks. Sometimes waived as a promo. Mechanism two is the FX rate margin. 1 to 3 percent of the wire amount, embedded in the exchange rate. Not a fee. A margin. Same money. Different label. On a 10K wire at 3 percent margin, that's 300 dollars. Same outcome as a 300 dollar fee. No itemisation. No disclosure. The two facts coexist legally. The bank waived the fee. The bank also kept 300 in the rate. Stop. WorldFirst skips the second mechanism. Near mid-market rates. The rate Google shows when you check USD CNY. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

This is the cleanest of the five. Mechanism-led explanation, no compounding extrapolation, no accusation tone. 'Same money. Different label.' is accurate. 'Two facts coexist legally' is well-supported. The 1 to 3 percent range is correctly stated as a range rather than a fixed 3 percent. The WF claim 'skips the second mechanism' is slightly strong because WF still has a margin, but framing 'near mid-market' is correct.

  • Ok the mechanism explanation actually clear leh. But still ad.
  • Maybank also got promo no fee for first 3 wires. So what makes WF different.
  • Near mid market = also got margin. Just smaller. Say la, don't hide.
  • Why I never see this 'two mechanisms' on Maybank app. Show evidence.
  • If both facts legal then WorldFirst also can do this when they grow big. Right?

Stop reading the fee column on your wire confirmation. I'm serious. The fee column is theatre. Banks waive the wire fee as a promo because the real money is somewhere else. The real money is in the FX rate. You send 10K to China. Confirmation says fee waived. You feel like you won. The rate they applied is 3 percent off Google's mid-market. #10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 132 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about cross-border wires. The fee line is decoration. The rate is the business. Your bank can advertise no transfer fee on the front of the brochure and quietly mark up the FX rate by 3 percent on the back end. Both facts are legal. Both facts coexist on the same confirmation. On a 10K wire, that 3 percent is 300 dollars. No line item. No notification. The wire receipt literally says fee waived next to a rate that just cost you 300 bucks. 35 percent of business owners say this is their top frustration. Most still don't know it's 300 per 10K. WorldFirst doesn't play this game. Near mid-market rates. The rate Google shows when you search USD CNY. Same wire. The 300 stays with you. That's the difference. Period.

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling you about cross-border wires. The fee line is decoration. The rate is the business. Your bank can advertise no transfer fee on the front of the brochure and quietly mark up the FX rate by 3 percent on the back end. Both facts are legal. Both facts coexist on the same confirmation. On a 10K wire, that 3 percent is 300 dollars. No line item. No notification. The wire receipt literally says fee waived next to a rate that just cost you 300 bucks. 35 percent of business owners say this is their top frustration. Most still don't know it's 300 per 10K. WorldFirst doesn't play this gameis built differently. Near mid-market rates. The rate Google shows when you search USD CNY. Same wire. The 300 stays with you. That's the difference. Period.

Risk is the 'both facts legal, both facts coexist on the same confirmation' framing might lead a skeptic to ask for receipt evidence on-screen. Brand-polished version softens to 'something nobody's telling you' which dials it down sensibly.

  • If bank really keep 300 silently MAS or BNM sure already pull license la.

Card P1-A14Pillar P1Short-form 60sTopic 02Comparison
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 142 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. Three business owners sent 50K to the same Chinese supplier on the same day. Supplier received three different amounts. 49,250. 49,700. 49,850. A 600 dollar spread on identical wires. Now compound it. 12 wires a year at 50K each. 600 thousand dollars in sourcing volume. The business owner who used the bank lost 9,000 dollars. The business owner who used the specialist lost 1,800. That's a 7,200 dollar gap in one year. On the same business. Run it across three years. 21,600 dollars. That's an employee. That's a container of inventory. That's the next product line. And it's not a fee. It's the exchange rate margin. Banks bake 1 to 2% into the rate so you never see the line item. Listen. WorldFirst sits at 0.3% on the same wires. Same day to Chinese bank accounts. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. Three business owners sent 50K to the same Chinese supplier on the same day. Supplier received three different amounts. 49,250. 49,700. 49,850. A 600 dollar spread on identical wires. Now compound it. 12 wires a year at 50K each. 600 thousand dollars in sourcing volume. The business owner who used the bank lost around 9,000 dollars. The business owner who used the specialist lost around 1,800. That's a 7,200 dollar gap in one year. On the same business. Run it across three years. 21,600 dollars. That's an employee. That's a container of inventory. That's the next product line. And it's not a fee. It's the exchange rate margin. Banks bake 1 to 2% into the rate so you never see the line item. Listen. WorldFirst sits closer to 0.3% on the same wires. Same day to Chinese bank accounts. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Compound math from 600 spread to 7,200 a year assumes consistent margin on every wire of the year, which is plausible but not guaranteed (rates fluctuate, some wires hit better correspondent paths). '21,600 over 3 years' is illustrative and frames it as a typical case. The supplier-end amounts are grounded in the card's findings but the 'employee / inventory / product line' translation is rhetorical. WF '0.3%' figure should ideally say 'around 0.3%' since it varies by pair.

  • Compound math sound nice but rate change every day, where got always 1.5%.
  • 21,600 over 3 years is best case for the seller side of this story.
  • If WorldFirst really 0.3 percent, why bank can't match? Got hidden somewhere.
  • I pay 50K once a year not 12 times, this not apply to small shop like me.
  • Container of inventory? Got receipt no? Show la.

Three different amounts. 49,250. 49,700. 49,850. The one who got the worst rate is paying for the bank's margin and doesn't even know it. The receipt says no fee. The wire went through. #3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 134 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. Three business owners. Same day. Same Chinese supplier. Same 50K wire. Three different amounts arrived. Here's the deal. Business owner A used a major bank. Supplier got 49,250. Business owner B used a regional bank. Supplier got 49,700. Business owner C used a specialist provider. Supplier got 49,850. Same wire. 600 dollar spread. And all three of them paid the same line item. No fee. Stop. The wire fee is not the cost. The exchange rate is the cost. Banks hide a 1.5% margin in the rate so you never see the line. On 50K that's 750 dollars. Run that across 12 wires a year. You just gave your bank 9,000 dollars for nothing. Period. WorldFirst sits at 0.3% margin. Same wire. Supplier gets the full amount. Same day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. Three business owners. Same day. Same Chinese supplier. Same 50K wire. Three different amounts arrived. Here's the deal. Business owner A used a major bank. Supplier got 49,250. Business owner B used a regional bank. Supplier got 49,700. Business owner C used a specialist provider. Supplier got 49,850. Same wire. 600 dollar spread. And all three of them paid the same line item. No fee. Stop. The wire fee is not the cost. The exchange rate is the cost. Banks hidebake a 1.5% margin ininto the rate so you never see the line. On 50K that's 750 dollars. Run that across 12 wires a year. You just gave your bankThat's 9,000 dollars for nothing. Period.sitting in your bank's pocket. WorldFirst sits closer to 0.3% margin. Same wire. Supplier gets close to the full amount. Same day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The three-supplier comparison anchored in the findings (49,250 / 49,700 / 49,850 on a 50K wire) is defensible because it's the card's own evidence. '1.5% margin = 750 on 50K' aligns with the findings range of 0.6 to 1.5% across providers. The '12 wires a year' compound is a generic average, not a verified scenario, and the WF '0.3% margin' / 'full amount lands' framing is a typical-case figure presented as universal. CN-side receiving bank fees can still apply and are not addressed.

  • Where this 1.5% number from? My RHB never break it down like this.
  • 12 wires a year? I send 3 a year only, this math don't apply to me.
  • WorldFirst is what? Sound like crypto thing. Got license in Malaysia or not?
  • If three guys really same wire same day, who showed you the receipts? Where's the proof.
  • Bank still better lah, at least I can walk in branch when problem.
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 133 words

Accusation opener

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

Your bank stole 600 dollars from you on your last wire to China and you didn't even notice. Here's the proof. Three business owners. Same 50K wire. Same day. Same supplier. Three different amounts landed. The one who used the major bank got the worst rate. 49,250. The specialist provider customer got 49,850. Same wire. Same supplier. 600 dollar difference. All three of them looked at the receipt and saw the same thing. No fee. Wire fee waived. So they thought the wire was free. It wasn't. Your bank packs a 1.5% margin into the rate. You'll never see it on a statement. No receipt. No notification. No refund. I'll say it. This is how banks make their margin on every single cross-border wire to China. WorldFirst doesn't do this. 0.3% margin. Same day. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Your bank stolequietly took 600 dollars from you on your last wire to China and you didn't even notice. Here's the proof. Three business owners. Same 50K wire. Same day. Same supplier. Three different amounts landed. The one who used the major bank got the worst rate. 49,250. The specialist provider customer got 49,850. Same wire. Same supplier. 600 dollar difference. All three of them looked at the receipt and saw the same thing. No fee. Wire fee waived. So they thought the wire was free. It wasn't. Your bank packs a 1.5% margin into the rate. You'll never see it on a statement. No receipt. No notification. No refund. I'll say it. This is how banks makeearn their margin on every single cross-border wirecross-border wires to China. WorldFirst doesn't do thisis built differently. Around 0.3% margin. Same day. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'Your bank stole / quietly took' is loaded language even after the iteration soften. Banks legally disclose FX margins in T&Cs, so 'stole' is contestable. The 600 dollar number and the three-band comparison are grounded in the findings. The 'no receipt, no notification, no refund' framing is accurate for most bank wire confirmations but stated absolutely. WF '0.3% margin' is presented as a static fact rather than a typical-case range.

  • Stole is heavy word la, my Maybank statement got rate clearly stated.
  • If bank really stealing, where's the regulator? They got license one.
  • Quietly took also got the rate on the receipt, you just never look.
  • WorldFirst is which country company? Got MAS license or not?
  • 600 dollar on 50K wire is 1.2%, not 600 dollar on every wire la.
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 138 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you wire money to Chinese suppliers, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three business owners ran the same wire last week and got three different outcomes. One. 50K wired through a major bank. Supplier got 49,250. Wire fee on the receipt: zero. Two. 50K wired through a regional bank. Supplier got 49,700. Wire fee on the receipt: zero. Three. 50K wired through a specialist provider. Supplier got 49,850. Wire fee on the receipt: zero. Three identical wires. 600 dollar spread. All paid no fee. Stop comparing wire fees. The wire fee is not the cost. The exchange rate is the cost. Your bank is paid by the rate margin you don't see. WorldFirst sits at 0.3% on USD to CNY. Same wire, full amount lands, same day to Chinese accounts. If you ship from China, you need to know this. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

If you wire money to Chinese suppliers, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three business owners ran the same wire last week and got three different outcomes. One. 50K wired through a major bank. Supplier got 49,250. Wire fee on the receipt: zero. Two. 50K wired through a regional bank. Supplier got 49,700. Wire fee on the receipt: zero. Three. 50K wired through a specialist provider. Supplier got 49,850. Wire fee on the receipt: zero. Three identical wires. 600 dollar spread. All paid no fee. Stop comparing wire fees. The wire fee is not the cost. The exchange rate is the costWire fees are not the full cost. The exchange rate is. Your bank is paid byearns through the rate margin you don't see. WorldFirst sits closer to 0.3% on USD to CNY. Same wire, close to the full amount lands, same day to Chinese accounts. If you ship from China, you need to know this. Periodthis is worth knowing. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Problem stack is well-grounded (three identical wires, three results, all receipts say zero fee). 'Wire fees are not the full cost' is accurate. 'Your bank earns through the rate margin you don't see' is true. WF 'closer to 0.3% margin' is honest. The 'Same wire, close to the full amount lands' line is iterated to acknowledge minor receiving-side variance. Mainly a structural script, low factual risk but compresses three real cases as a single narrative without sourcing.

  • All three got zero fee on receipt but rate was different, where do I see the rate then?
  • Where these three business owners from? VN? MY? SG? Pair matters.
  • Close to 0.3% means what, 0.5 or 0.3? Just say the number.
  • If you ship from China this is worth knowing, but I source from Vietnam, so irrelevant.
  • WorldFirst what license they got in my country?
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 113 words

I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. Your bank is the most expensive part of your China sourcing and nobody's putting it on a spreadsheet. Three business owners. Same 50K wire. Same supplier. Same day. The first got 49,250 landed. The third got 49,850. 600 dollar gap. Now compound it. 12 wires a year. That's 7,200 dollars gone to your bank's rate margin. Run a business that does 600K in sourcing and that 7,200 is just one factory. Add two more, you're at 20K a year. That's an employee you didn't hire because your bank silently took it. WorldFirst sits at 0.3% margin. Same wire, same supplier, same day to Chinese accounts. The difference shows up on your P&L. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

I'll say it. Your bank is the most expensive part of your China sourcing and nobody's putting it on a spreadsheet. Three business owners. Same 50K wire. Same supplier. Same day. The first got 49,250 landed. The third got 49,850. 600 dollar gap. Now compound it. 12 wires a year. That's 7,200 dollars gonegoing to your bank's rate margin. Run a business that does 600K in sourcing and that 7,200 is just one factory. Add two more, you're at 20K a year. That's an employee you didn't hire because your bank silentlyquietly took it. WorldFirst sits closer to 0.3% margin. Same wire, same supplier, same day to Chinese accounts. The difference shows up on your P&L. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Compound math (600 x 12 = 7,200) is arithmetically clean but assumes 12 wires a year per business owner, which is a typical mid-size importer scenario not all SEA SMEs. 'Add two more factories you're at 20K' is rhetorical and presented as universal. The factual core (bank rate margin, supplier-end gap, WF closer to 0.3%) is grounded. The 'employee you didn't hire' line is rhetorical translation, fine for the format.

  • 12 wires a year is for big shop, I do 4 a year. Maths different.
  • If WorldFirst really 0.3 then everyone should switch why nobody talking about it?
  • 20K a year employee, in which country bro. Not in Singapore.
  • Where does the rate margin number come from, source pls.
  • Same supplier same day three different amount, very dramatic. Got proof?
#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 132 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's the problem most business owners don't realize. Two wires to the same supplier on the same day can land at different amounts. Why? The exchange rate. Your bank picks the rate. They don't show you what they picked relative to the real market rate. They just show you the wire fee. Zero. Waived. Free. So you think the wire was free. Three business owners proved this last week. All sent 50K to the same Chinese factory. The bank customer's supplier got 49,250. The regional bank got 49,700. The specialist got 49,850. 600 dollar spread on identical wires. Same line item. Different reality. Stop assuming the fee line tells you the cost. The rate is the cost. WorldFirst sits at 0.3% margin on USD to CNY. Same day. Full amount lands. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the problempart most business owners don't realize. Two wires to the same supplier on the same day can land at different amounts. Why? The exchange rate. Your bank picks the rate. They don't show you what they picked relative to the real market rate. They just show you the wire fee. Zero. Waived. Free. So you think the wire was free. Three business owners proved this last week. All sent 50K to the same Chinese factory. The bank customer's supplier got 49,250. The regional bank got 49,700. The specialist got 49,850. 600 dollar spread on identical wires. Same line item. Different reality. Stop assuming the fee line tells you the costThe fee line is not the cost. The rate is the cost. WorldFirst sits closer to 0.3% margin on USD to CNY. Same day . Full amount lands. Period.to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

This variant is the most defensible. Mechanism reveal (rate not fee is the cost) is fundamentally accurate. The three-amount comparison is the card's own evidence. The iterated VO removes 'Stop' and 'Period' and downgrades absolutist phrasing. 'Closer to 0.3% margin' is a more honest framing of WF's rate. Only soft flag is 'two wires same day can land at different amounts' is true but the three-business comparison is illustrative, not a documented case study.

  • Okay this make sense, but is this an actual story or made up example?
  • If rate is the real cost, where I see the real rate to compare?
  • 0.3% on USD to CNY, who confirm? Got screenshot?
  • Three business owner case study very convenient ah.
  • WorldFirst is who? Never heard, then need verify lor.

Classic Stop / Real-talk opener

Soc4.0 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Lost some of the original Gary Vee punch after polish. Still works but feels closer to baseline.

Pure original

Stop. Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. Three of you sent 50K wires this morning. Same supplier. Same hour. Different amounts landed. One got 49,250. One got 49,700. One got 49,850. A 600 dollar gap on the same wire. Why? The rate. Your bank packs 1 to 2% margin into the exchange rate. The wire fee on your receipt says zero, so you think it cost you nothing. Wrong. The cost is the rate. Listen. WorldFirst sits at 0.3% margin on the same wire. Same day to Chinese bank accounts. The supplier gets the full amount. The receipt still says no fee. The difference is the 600 dollars stays in your business. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Stop. Real talk for everyFor every business owner sourcing from China. Three of you sent 50K wires this morning. Same supplier. Same hour. Different amounts landed. One got 49,250. One got 49,700. One got 49,850. A 600 dollar gap on the same wire. Why? The rate. Your bank packs 1 to 2% margin into the exchange rate. The wire fee on your receipt says zero, so you think it cost you nothing. WrongWorth a second look. The cost is the rate. Listen. WorldFirst sits closer to 0.3% margin on the same wire. Same day to Chinese bank accounts. The supplier gets close to the full amount. Stop using your bank / right-tool reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Pure original

Stop using your bank for China supplier payments. I'm serious. Watch what happened last week. Three business owners. Same supplier. Same day. 50K each. The major bank customer's supplier got 49,250. The specialist customer's supplier got 49,850. 600 dollars on one wire. Same product. Same factory. Same currency. Different tool. Your domestic bank was built for domestic payments. Cross-border CNY is not its job. You wouldn't use a screwdriver to drive a nail. So why are you using a bank to wire to China? WorldFirst was built for this exact wire. 0.3% rate margin. Same day to Chinese bank accounts. Use the right tool. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Stop usingRethink using your bank for China supplier payments. I'm serious. Watch what happened last week. Three business owners. Same supplier. Same day. 50K each. The major bank customer's supplier got 49,250. The specialist customer's supplier got 49,850. 600 dollars on one wire. Same product. Same factory. Same currency. Different tool. Soft flag is 'built for this exact wire' overstates WF as a single purpose-built tool when it's a broader multi-currency service.

#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 112 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about international wires. Three business owners sent 50K each to the same Chinese supplier yesterday. Three different amounts arrived. 49,250. 49,700. 49,850. Why? Banks don't make their money on the wire fee. They make it on the rate. They pick a rate. You can't see what they picked. You can't compare. You just see the receipt that says no fee, wire complete. Meanwhile they pocketed 1 to 2% of the wire in the rate margin. Silently. Every single wire. WorldFirst doesn't do that. 0.3% margin. The rate you see is close to the real rate. Same day to Chinese accounts. That's the difference. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the truth nobody's telling yousomething most people don't realise about international wires. Three business owners sent 50K each to the same Chinese supplier yesterday. Three different amounts arrived. 49,250. 49,700. 49,850. Why? Banks don't makeearn most of their money on the wire fee. They makeearn it on the rate. They pick a rate. You can't see what they picked. You can't compare. You just see the receipt that says no fee, wire complete. Meanwhile they pocketedtook 1 to 2% of the wire in the rate margin. SilentlyQuietly. Every single wire. WorldFirst doesn't do thatis built differently. Around 0.3% margin. The rate you see is close to the real rate. Same day to Chinese accounts. That's the difference. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The mechanism explanation (banks earn through rate margin, not wire fee) is accurate. The three-amount comparison is grounded. 'Quietly took 1 to 2%' is hedged from 'pocketed' and aligns with findings. WF 'around 0.3%' is honest. The conspiratorial frame is softened in the iteration. Soft flag is the implication that banks deliberately hide the rate. They disclose it, just not next to a mid-market reference.

  • Banks earn through rate margin, true, but they also got compliance cost. Not pure profit.
  • Most people don't realise this, but it's literally on the wire confirmation.
  • Quietly took, the rate is on the receipt bro just compare it.
  • Three different amount on same wire same supplier same day, source?
  • Built differently, like every fintech say. Need MAS or BNM license la.
Card P1-A2Pillar P1Short-form 60s · bilingualTopic 01Curiosity-led
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 126 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. You wire CNY to your supplier on Monday. They see nothing until Friday. That's four working days where production isn't moving. Now do that twelve times a year. That's forty-eight working days a year, almost ten weeks, gone. Why? Because your wire is on path one. SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five days, every single time. There are two other paths most business owners have never heard of. CIPS, one to two days. And a dedicated CNY rail, same-day. CIPS alone moved over twenty-four trillion in 2024, up forty-two percent in one year. This is real infrastructure. Most banks just don't tell you about it. Listen. The path you're on costs you weeks every year. Switch the path, get the weeks back. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. You wire CNY to your supplier on Monday. They see nothing until Friday. That's four working days where production isn't moving. Now do that twelve times a year. That's forty-eight working days a year, almost ten weeks, gone. Why? Because your wire is on path one. SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five days, every single time. There are two other paths most business owners have never heard of. CIPS, one to two days. And a dedicated CNY rail, same-day. CIPS alone moved over twenty-four trillion in 2024, up forty-two percent in one year. This is real infrastructure. Most banks just don't tell you about it. Listen. The path you're on costs you weeks every year. Switch the path, get the weeks back. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The '48 working days a year' and '10 weeks gone' extrapolation is the highest-risk claim. It assumes worst-case 4-day SWIFT delay on every single wire, which is statistically unlikely for any one operator. CIPS 24.47T 2024 figure is technically accurate per public China data but presented without source, which a numbers-savvy skeptic will challenge. 'Real infrastructure' phrasing is fine but the implied scale relevance to one SMB's wire is overstretched.

  • 48 days lost a year? Assume my wire always 4 days. Most of mine 2 days lah.
  • 24 trillion? Where this number from? Show source bro.
  • Even if CIPS exist, my bank still use SWIFT. So what to do?
  • Sound like ad. Show me real screenshot of same-day arrival.
  • WorldFirst regulated where? First time hearing the name.
#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 106 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You wire on Monday. Supplier says nothing's arrived. Tuesday, nothing. Wednesday, nothing. Thursday, you call your bank. They say it's with the correspondent. You call the correspondent. They say it's with the next correspondent. By Friday it lands. Production starts Monday. You just lost a week. Why? Because your wire is on the longest path. SWIFT through three correspondent banks. There are two faster paths. CIPS, one to two days. A dedicated CNY rail, same-day. Same money. Same destination. Different rails. Stop letting your bank pick the slowest one. WorldFirst routes you on the fastest available rail. Same-day. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Real talk for everyFor every business owner sourcing from China. You wire on Monday. Supplier says nothing's arrived. Tuesday, nothing. Wednesday, nothing. Thursday, you call your bank. They say it's with the correspondent. You call the correspondent. They say it's with the next correspondent. By Friday it lands. Production starts Monday. You just lost a week. Why? Because your wire is on the longest path. SWIFT through three correspondent banks. There are two faster paths. CIPS, one to two days. A dedicated CNY rail, same-day. Same money. Same destination. Different rails. Stop letting your bank pick the slowest oneWorth knowing which one your bank picked. WorldFirst routes you on the fastest available rail. Same-day. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The Monday-to-Friday wire chase scenario is realistic and resonant with anyone who's actually wired to China. Three-correspondent-bank chain is upper-range but plausible. 'Production starts Monday, you just lost a week' is dramatic but defensible for time-sensitive shipments. Main risk is universal framing of the bad-experience as everyone's experience. 'Fastest available rail, same-day' on WorldFirst side depends on supplier bank, not always achievable.

  • Monday to Friday chase, yes happened to me. Real talk.
  • Three correspondent banks? My wires usually 1 or 2 max.
  • Production starts Monday, lost a week. Sometimes I just deposit early lah.
  • WorldFirst same-day, but what if my supplier on tier 3 city bank? Still fast?
  • Need to see actual SC Malaysia license number before I trust.
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 132 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers, your CNY can take three different paths to get there and only one is same-day. Here's the deal. Path one. SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five business days. Multiple banks in the chain. Each one takes a fee, each one takes its time. Path two. CIPS, China's own clearing system. One to two days. Cleaner, but most banks abroad don't route through it directly. Path three. A dedicated CNY rail straight into the supplier's account. Same-day when set up right. Most business owners don't know which one their bank is using. So you wire on Monday and your supplier sees nothing until Friday. Production never starts. You eat the delay. Stop. Your wire confirmation tells you which path you're on. Read it. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers, your CNY can take three different paths to get there and only one is same-day. Here's the deal. Path one. SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five business days. Multiple banks in the chain. Each one takes a fee, each one takes its time. Path two. CIPS, China's own clearing system. One to two days. Cleaner, but most banks abroad don't route through it directly. Path three. A dedicated CNY rail straight into the supplier's account. Same-day when set up right. Most business owners don't know which one their bank is using. So you wire on Monday and your supplier sees nothing until Friday. Production never starts. You eat the delay. Stop. Your wire confirmation tells you which path you're on. Read it. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core mechanism (three distinct routing paths with different speeds) is real and defensible. SWIFT 3-5 day and CIPS 1-2 day timing windows align with published norms. 'Dedicated CNY rail same-day' is true conceptually but vague enough to feel like marketing without naming the specific rail. 'Monday to Friday' framing assumes worst-case SWIFT delay as universal which a skeptic with a fast bank can challenge. 'Production never starts' is also situational, framed as inevitable.

  • My Maybank wire to China take 2 days only, not 5 lah. Don't generalise.
  • What is this 'dedicated CNY rail'? Got name or not? Sound vague.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never heard. Got SC license in Malaysia?
  • My supplier got cash flow, don't need full amount land before production.
  • Read wire confirmation? Most banks don't show routing detail one.
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 118 words

Accusation opener

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

Your bank picked the slowest path on purpose. Here's how I know. There are three ways your CNY can reach a Chinese supplier. SWIFT, three to five days. CIPS, one to two days. A dedicated CNY rail, same-day. Your bank is using SWIFT. Always. Because every other bank in the SWIFT chain takes a cut and your bank is one of them. Same-day routing doesn't have a chain, so there's nothing to skim. You think your bank just doesn't know better. They know. They picked it. So your supplier waits four days. Production stalls. You miss the delivery window. You eat the loss. I'll say it. The path is a choice. Your bank made the wrong one for you on purpose. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Your bank picked the slowest path on purpose. Here's how I know. There are three ways your CNY can reach a Chinese supplier. SWIFT, three to five days. CIPS, one to two days. A dedicated CNY rail, same-day. Your bank is using SWIFT. Always. Because every other bank in the SWIFT chain takes a cut and your bank is one of them. Same-day routing doesn't have a chain, so there's nothing to skim. You think your bank just doesn't know better. They know. They picked it. So your supplier waits four days. Production stalls. You miss the delivery window. You eat the loss. I'll say it. The path is a choice. Your bank made the wrong one for you on purpose. Periodone. You can make a different one. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'Your bank picked the slowest path on purpose' is an intent-attribution claim that's hard to defend. SWIFT is usually the default because of infrastructure relationships, not malice. 'Same-day routing doesn't have a chain' is broadly true but oversimplified, dedicated rails still have settlement steps. 'They picked it' frames bank behaviour as deliberate rather than systemic. A skeptic with banking background will tear this apart. Iterated version softened to 'picked one' which lowers the risk.

  • On purpose? You really think bank sit down and choose slow path to skim me?
  • Conspiracy vibes. Banks just slow because old system, not because they evil.
  • Same-day routing also got cost what. Not free.
  • If WorldFirst so good why my friends in import biz never mention?
  • Got proof on the chain fees? My wire fee always disclosed.
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 123 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you pay suppliers in China, you need to hear this. Real talk. Your CNY can take three paths. Most business owners only know one. Path one. SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five days. Multiple hops. Multiple fees. The default at almost every bank. Path two. CIPS direct clearing. One to two days. Lower cost. Most banks abroad don't connect to it directly. Path three. A dedicated CNY rail. Same-day. Money lands the same business day. Almost no one uses it because almost no one knows it exists. So here's what happens. You wire Monday. Supplier sees nothing until Friday. Production stalls. Lead time blown. Customer waiting. Margin bleeding. All because you defaulted to path one. Stop. Pick the path. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.
