If you pay suppliers in China, you need to hear this. Real talk. Your CNY can take three paths. Most business owners only know one. Path one. SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five days. Multiple hops. Multiple fees. The default at almost every bank. Path two. CIPS direct clearing. One to two days. Lower cost. Most banks abroad don't connect to it directly. Path three. A dedicated CNY rail. Same-day. Money lands the same business day. Almost no one uses it because almost no one knows it exists. So here's what happens. You wire Monday. Supplier sees nothing until Friday. Production stalls. Lead time blown. Customer waiting. Margin bleeding. All because you defaulted to path one. Stop. Pick the path. PeriodPick the path yourself next time. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Problem-stack format is clean. The 3-path teaching is consistent with grounding research. 'Customer waiting. Margin bleeding.' cascade is mildly dramatic for a one-wire delay but defensible for time-sensitive shipments. 'Almost no one uses it because almost no one knows it exists' is hyperbolic, plenty of corporate treasury teams know about CIPS. Most SMBs do default to path one, so the core point holds, just the absolutism is a soft spot.

  • Margin bleeding from one delayed wire? Bit dramatic la.
  • Almost no one knows? Big corp finance team all know CIPS lah.
  • Ok the 3 paths thing useful info. But how I pick path 3 specifically?
  • WorldFirst Singapore right? They cover Malaysia accounts also?
  • Show real timeline screenshot, then I believe.
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 117 words

I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. The path your CNY takes to China is the most overlooked decision in your supply chain. You send a wire on Monday. Supplier sees it Friday. That's four days of dead time, on every single payment. Now compound it. Twelve payments a year, that's forty-eight working days lost. Two payments a month, that's a hundred days. A hundred days of production stalled, every year. Why? Because nobody told you there are three paths. SWIFT three to five days. CIPS one to two days. A dedicated CNY rail, same-day. CIPS alone settled over twenty-four trillion in 2024. Real infrastructure. Your bank just isn't routing you on it. WorldFirst does. The day you send is the day they receive. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

I'll say it. The path your CNY takes to China is the most overlooked decision in your supply chain. You send a wire on Monday. Supplier sees it Friday. That's four days of dead time, on every single payment. Now compound it. Twelve payments a year, that's forty-eight working days lost. Two payments a month, that's a hundred days. A hundred days of production stalled, every year. Why? Because nobody told you there are three paths. SWIFT three to five days. CIPS one to two days. A dedicated CNY rail, same-day. CIPS alone settled over twenty-four trillion in 2024. Real infrastructure. Your bank just isn't routing you on it. WorldFirst does. The day you send is the day they receive. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 100-days-lost-per-year extrapolation is the highest-risk claim. Two payments a month at 4-day delay each gives 96 days, not 100, and assumes worst-case delay on every payment, which won't hold for a fast bank. The 24 trillion CIPS figure is technically accurate but presented without citation. 'Most overlooked decision in your supply chain' is opinion-framed which is fair Gary Vee voice. 'Your bank isn't routing you on it' is broadly true.

  • 100 days? You assume every wire 4 days delay. Mine usually 2.
  • 24 trillion in 2024, source please. Otherwise sound made up.
  • Most overlooked decision? I always check fees, that's a decision la.
  • If CIPS so big why WorldFirst not a bank? How they access it?
  • Day you send is day they receive. Even at 11pm Friday? Sure ah?
#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 124 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's the part most business owners don't realize when they pay China. There isn't one wire. There are three. Three different mechanisms, three different speeds, three different costs. Mechanism one. SWIFT. A messaging network. Your money hops bank to bank to bank. Three to five days, every hop takes a fee. Mechanism two. CIPS. China's own clearing system. Direct settlement, one to two days, fewer hops, lower cost. Mechanism three. A dedicated CNY rail built into the receiving bank. Same-day. The money lands in the supplier's account the day you send it. Most banks default you to mechanism one because it's their default. They don't pick the fastest one for you. They pick the easiest one for them. Stop accepting the default. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the part most business owners don't realize when they pay China. There isn't one wire. There are three. Three different mechanisms, three different speeds, three different costs. Mechanism one. SWIFT. A messaging network. Your money hops bank to bank to bank. Three to five days, every hop takes a fee. Mechanism two. CIPS. China's own clearing system. Direct settlement, one to two days, fewer hops, lower cost. Mechanism three. A dedicated CNY rail built into the receiving bank. Same-day. The money lands in the supplier's account the day you send it. Most banks default you to mechanism one because it's their default. They don't pick the fastest one for you. They pick the easiest one for them. Stop accepting the default. PeriodWorth knowing the difference. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Cleanest of the Hormozi set. Teaching frame ('three mechanisms') is accurate and properly hedged ('most banks default'). No universal percentage claim, no extrapolated multi-year math, no intent attribution to banks. The 'easiest for them' line is borderline but framed as opinion. Same-day claim on path three is the only soft spot but it's stated conditionally enough to hold up. Most likely to pass a skeptic's first scan.

  • Ok this one make sense. Actually didn't know about CIPS before.
  • Same-day land really? Or land in WorldFirst account first then takes another day?
  • Easiest for them, fine. But how WorldFirst make money then?
  • Who regulate WorldFirst here? Got Malaysia license?
  • Need to see actual rate and fee comparison vs my bank, not just speed.

Classic Stop / Real-talk opener

Soc4.0 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

Lost some of the original Gary Vee punch after polish. Still works but feels closer to baseline.

Pure original

Stop. Real talk for every business owner paying Chinese suppliers. You think there's one way to send CNY to China. There's three. Path one. SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five days. Path two. CIPS. China's own clearing system. One to two days. Path three. A dedicated CNY rail straight into the supplier's account. Same-day. Most banks abroad only use path one. They don't tell you the other two exist. So you wait. Your supplier waits. Your production waits. WorldFirst routes you onto the fastest available path. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The money lands the day you send it. Stop accepting the slow default. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop. Real talk for everyFor every business owner paying Chinese suppliers. You think there's one way to send CNY to China. There's three. Path one. SWIFT through correspondent banks. Three to five days. Path two. CIPS. China's own clearing system. One to two days. Path three. A dedicated CNY rail straight into the supplier's account. Same-day. Most banks abroad only use path one. They don't tell you the other two exist. So you wait. Your supplier waits. Your production waits. WorldFirst routes you onto the fastest available path. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The money lands the day you send it. Stop accepting the slow defaultWorth knowing your options. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Time windows (3-5, 1-2, same-day) are all reasonable. Main soft spot is 'they don't tell you the other two exist' which is a sweeping claim about bank behaviour, but framed as opinion. Defensible overall.

  • My bank actually mention SWIFT vs faster route before, just expensive.
  • WorldFirst routes me on fastest? What if no fast route exist for my supplier's bank?
  • Who is WorldFirst? Got license to operate Malaysia?
  • Same-day mean same calendar day or business day?

#9 PG3 Gary Vee Opinion-led 118 words

Stop using your bank / right-tool reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Pure original

Stop sending CNY through SWIFT. I'm serious. SWIFT is a tool. It's the wrong tool for China. SWIFT was built to move money between banks that don't have a direct relationship. So your money hops three, four banks before it lands. Three to five days. Multiple fees. That's not a payment, that's a relay race. China has its own clearing system called CIPS. One to two days, direct settlement. And there's a third path, a dedicated CNY rail, that lands same-day. You wouldn't drive cross-country when there's a flight. So why are you wiring CNY on the slowest route there is? WorldFirst picks the fast route for you. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Use the right tool. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop sending CNY through SWIFT. I'm seriousSWIFT isn't the right tool for China. SWIFT is a tool. It's the wrong tool for China. SWIFT was built to move money between banks that don't have a direct relationship. So your money hops three, four banks before it lands. Three to five days. Multiple fees. That's not a payment, that's a relay race. China has its own clearing system called CIPS. One to two days, direct settlement. And there's a third path, a dedicated CNY rail, that lands same-day. You wouldn't drive cross-country when there's a flight. So why are you wiring CNY on the slowest route there is? WorldFirst picks the fast route for you. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Use the right tool. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Right-tool reframe is clean and educational. 'SWIFT built to move money between banks without direct relationship' is technically accurate. 'Hops three, four banks' is upper-range but defensible for US-to-CN routes. The flight-vs-drive analogy lands. Main soft spot is 'WorldFirst picks the fast route for you' which is a product claim that depends on supplier's receiving bank being on the right rail, not always the case.

  • Cross-country vs flight analogy nice. But flight got security check also lah.
  • What if my supplier's bank not on the fast rail? Still SWIFT?
  • WorldFirst pick the fast route, but I still pay fee right? How much?
  • First time hearing WorldFirst, Got SC Malaysia license?
  • SWIFT also got faster options now, gpi all that. Not always 5 days.
#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 116 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatHigh

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about paying China. There isn't one path. There's three. And your bank only uses the slowest one. Path one is SWIFT. Three to five days. Multiple banks in the chain, each one taking a fee. This is what your bank defaults you to. Path two is CIPS, China's own clearing system. One to two days. Cleaner. Cheaper. But it requires your bank to plug into China's rails, and most don't. Path three is a dedicated CNY rail straight into the supplier's bank. Same-day. The money lands the day you send it. The reason nobody talks about path two and three? They aren't the bank's default, and the default makes them money. WorldFirst is different. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling you about paying China. There isn't one path. There's three. And your bank only uses the slowest one. Path one is SWIFT. Three to five days. Multiple banks in the chain, each one taking a fee. This is what your bank defaults you to. Path two is CIPS, China's own clearing system. One to two days. Cleaner. Cheaper. But it requires your bank to plug into China's rails, and most don't. Path three is a dedicated CNY rail straight into the supplier's bank. Same-day. The money lands the day you send it. The reason nobody talks about path two and three? They aren't the bank's default, and the default makes them moneythe default. WorldFirst is different. Periodbuilt around the faster ones.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Conspiratorial framing ('truth nobody's telling you', 'the default makes them money') is high-risk because it attributes intent to banks. Bank fee structures are real but framing them as deliberate suppression of alternatives is hard to evidence. CIPS connection statement ('most banks don't connect to it directly') is broadly true for retail/SMB banks abroad but not for major commercial banks. Iterated version softens conspiracy tone, which lowers the score in the polished line.

  • Conspiracy vibe too strong. Banks just lazy, not evil.
  • Path 2 path 3 not the default because mostly retail wires don't qualify, not coz of bank greed.
  • If WorldFirst plug into CIPS directly, where the source? Show me press release.
  • Sound like ad with grudge against banks. Where my proof?
  • Who is WorldFirst la. License? Backing? Don't want to send 100K to startup.
Card P1-A4Pillar P1Short-form 60s · bilingualTopic 06Pain-led
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 142 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. You wire $50,000 to your Chinese supplier. They get $49,800. You just lost $200 and your bank gave you zero warning. Now do that twice a month. That's $400 a month. $4,800 a year. Over five years, three middle banks quietly took $24,000 from your business. That's a hire. That's a trade show. That's three months of inventory. And here's the part that makes it worse. The supplier won't ship until the full amount lands. So now you wire another $200. Plus another wire fee. Plus another week of delay. Plus your customer is waiting. The cause is one default setting on your SWIFT MT103. Field 71A. Set to SHA. Stop. WorldFirst routes payments through MYbank straight to Chinese accounts. No SWIFT chain. No middle banks. No deductions. Same-day. The full amount lands. Every wire. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. You wire $50,000 to your Chinese supplier. They get $49,800. You just lost $200 and your bank gave you zero warning. Now do that twice a month. That's $400 a month. $4,800 a year. Over five years, three middle banks quietly took $24,000 from your business. That's a hire. That's a trade show. That's three months of inventory. And here's the part that makes it worse. The supplier won't ship until the full amount lands. So now you wire another $200. Plus another wire fee. Plus another week of delay. Plus your customer is waiting. The cause is one default setting on your SWIFT MT103. Field 71A. Set to SHA. Stop. WorldFirst routes payments through MYbank straight to Chinese accounts. No SWIFT chain. No middle banks. No deductions. Same-day. The full amount lands. Every wire. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The compounding math ($24K over 5 years) assumes $200 lost on every wire, twice a month, for 60 straight months. That's an extrapolation a skeptical SMB will dismiss. Real correspondent deductions vary by route, by amount, by bank, sometimes they're $0. The SWIFT 71A SHA reference is technically accurate. 'Three middle banks' is also an upper-bound. 'Three months of inventory' emotion stack is fine in isolation but compounds the credibility risk when sitting on top of an averaged-up loss number.

  • $24K over 5 years from $200? Sure or not. You assume every wire loss is $200, that's the max case.
  • I don't wire $50K twice a month bro. Math don't apply to me.
  • Three months of inventory? Mine $24K is more like one week of inventory. Numbers feel cherry-picked.
  • Field 71A SHA? You expect me to log into SWIFT myself? My bank handle this lah.
  • Got example from real importer or just math? Show me proof.
#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 131 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You think your bank is moving your money. They're not. They're just sending a message through SWIFT. Real correspondent banks in the middle are moving it. 1, 2, 3 of them. Each one takes a $15 to $50 cut. You wire $50,000, supplier sees $49,800. You call the bank. They blame the intermediary. The intermediary blames the receiving bank in China. Nobody takes responsibility. Meanwhile your supplier won't ship until the gap is topped up. You're losing days. Your customer is waiting. Your margin is bleeding. And you're on the phone with a teller who's never heard the word correspondent before. I see this every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. Direct rail to Chinese accounts. Same-day. Full $50,000 lands. Supplier ships. You sleep. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Real talk for everyFor every business owner sourcing from China. You think your bank is moving your money. They're not. They're just sending a message through SWIFT. Real correspondent banks in the middle are moving it. 1, 2, 3 of them. Each one takes a $15 to $50 cut. You wire $50,000, supplier sees $49,800. You call the bank. They blame the intermediary. The intermediary blames the receiving bank in China. Nobody takes responsibility. Meanwhile your supplier won't ship until the gap is topped up. You're losing days. Your customer is waiting. Your margin is bleeding. And you're on the phone with a teller who's never heard the word correspondent before. I see thisThis happens every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. Direct rail to Chinese accounts. Same-day. Full $50,000 lands. Supplier ships. You sleep. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Strongest narrative of the GaryVee set. The 'call the bank, they blame intermediary, intermediary blames CN bank, nobody takes responsibility' beat is real and resonates with anyone who's been through it. 'Teller has never heard the word correspondent' is honest. Correspondent $15-50 deduction range is accurate. Soft spots: 'this happens every week' is anecdotal claim without source, and 'full $50,000 lands' on WF side is the standard overreach. The personal-energy story carries the script even if individual claims are challengeable.

  • Call the bank, they blame intermediary, intermediary blame CN bank. Story of my life bro.
  • Teller never heard of correspondent? Maybe try the priority banking line lah.
  • Direct rail to Chinese accounts. Including state banks like ICBC and Bank of China?
  • Full $50,000 lands. Including covering the CN bank receiving fees too or not?
  • Sleep at night? Sure. But also need to do KYC with WorldFirst first, that's also effort.
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 145 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. If you wire money to Chinese suppliers, your bank is robbing you in plain sight. Here's how. You send $50,000. Supplier WhatsApps you saying $49,800 landed. Where did the $200 go? Not a scam. Not a math error. Correspondent banks. SWIFT wires hop through 1 to 3 middle banks before they hit China. Each bank shaves $15 to $50 directly off the wire. Your bank never tells you. Supplier just sees a smaller number. Now they refuse to ship until you top up. You eat the $200. You eat the delay. You eat the next order too. Stop. The default code on your wire is SHA. SHA means the supplier eats every middle-bank cut. Change it to OUR and the supplier gets the full amount. Or skip SWIFT entirely. WorldFirst routes direct to China through MYbank. No middle banks. No deductions. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. If you wire money to Chinese suppliers, your bank is robbing you in plain sightmay be quietly taking from you. Here's how. You send $50,000. Supplier WhatsApps you saying $49,800 landed. Where did the $200 go? Not a scam. Not a math error. Correspondent banks. SWIFT wires hop through 1 to 3 middle banks before they hit China. Each bank shaves $15 to $50 directly off the wire. Your bank never tells you. Supplier just sees a smaller number. Now they refuse to ship until you top up. You eat the $200. You eat the delay. You eat the next order too. Stop. The default code on your wire is SHA. SHA means the supplier eats every middle-bank cut. Change it to OUR and the supplier gets the full amount. Or skip SWIFT entirely. WorldFirst routes direct to China through MYbank. No middle banks. No deductions. Period. Worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core mechanism is real and defensible: correspondent banks do deduct $15-50 directly from SWIFT wires under the SHA default, and 1-3 hops is typical for US-China corridors. The 'supplier refuses to ship' beat is a real but situational scenario, framed as inevitable. The MYbank claim is accurate for WorldFirst's 1688 World Pay rail. Main soft spots: 'robbing you in plain sight' is strong language for what is actually a disclosed (if buried) industry default, and 'no deductions' on the WF side ignores any CN-side bank receiving fees that can still trim small amounts.

  • $50K wire one time only? Bro I never send that big lah. My orders maybe $5K.
  • SHA, OUR, what code? My bank app got no such thing meh.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never hear before. Got Bank Negara license or not?
  • My supplier always confirm full amount one. Maybe got better banker?
  • MYbank? That's Alipay one right? How they connect to my Maybank account?
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 150 words

Accusation opener

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

Three banks you've never heard of just stole $200 from your last wire to China. Here's how. You wired $50,000. Your supplier received $49,800. You assumed the gap was a fee. It wasn't. SWIFT moves your money through a chain of correspondent banks. 1, 2, sometimes 3 of them sit between your bank and the supplier's bank. Each one is allowed to deduct $15 to $50 directly from the wire amount before it moves to the next hop. Your bank never disclosed it because the default SWIFT code on your wire, SHA, says the supplier eats those deductions. Not you. So your supplier got short, refused to start production, and you lost a week. I'll say it. Banks have been running this default for decades. WorldFirst is different. Direct rail through MYbank into Chinese accounts. No correspondent chain. No silent deductions. The number you send is the number that lands. Stop signing off on the SHA default. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Three banks you've never heard of just stoletook $200 fromout of your last wire to China. Here's how. You wired $50,000. Your supplier received $49,800. You assumed the gap was a fee. It wasn't. SWIFT moves your money through a chain of correspondent banks. 1, 2, sometimes 3 of them sit between your bank and the supplier's bank. Each one is allowed to deduct $15 to $50 directly from the wire amount before it moves to the next hop. Your bank never disclosed it because the default SWIFT code on your wire, SHA, says the supplier eats those deductions. Not you. So your supplier got short, refused to start production, and you lost a week. I'll say it. Banks have been running this default for decades. WorldFirst is different. Direct rail through MYbank into Chinese accounts. No correspondent chain. No silent deductions. The number you send is the number that lands. Stop signing off on the SHA default. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'Took $200 out of your wire' is softer than 'stole' but still implies wrongdoing or concealment, when in reality correspondent bank deductions are disclosed in T&Cs and standard SWIFT practice. 'Banks have been running this default for decades' is sweeping but technically accurate. 'The number you send is the number that lands' on WF side overstates because Chinese receiving banks can still apply small inbound fees on some account types. 'Three banks you've never heard of' is good narrative but a skeptic with banking literacy will challenge the framing.

  • Took? Banks disclose in fine print what. Not theft if I tick the box.
  • Number you send equals number that lands? Sure or not? Chinese bank also got receiving fee meh.
  • Banks been doing decades, WorldFirst only how many years? Why I trust new player?
  • Three banks I never heard of? Show me the wire trace then. Don't just claim.
  • Ok so SHA bad. Why my bank not just default to OUR if it's better?
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 147 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you wire money to China, you need to hear this. Real talk. The last time you sent $50,000 to a supplier, four things happened and you saw zero of them. One. Your bank charged you a wire fee. Maybe $30. That you saw on the statement. Two. Correspondent bank A took $50 off the wire. You didn't see that. Three. Correspondent bank B took another $35. You didn't see that either. Four. Correspondent bank C took $80. Still invisible. Supplier received $49,835 instead of $50,000. WhatsApp ping. 'Short $165, please top up.' Now you wire another $200. Another fee. Another week. Your customer is waiting. Your supplier is annoyed. Your margin is bleeding. Stop wiring through SWIFT for China payments. WorldFirst routes direct to Chinese accounts through MYbank. No correspondent chain. Same-day. The number you send is the number that lands. If you're a business owner paying China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

If you wire money to China, you need to hear this. Real talk. The last time you sent $50,000 to a supplier, four things happened and you saw zero of them. One. Your bank charged you a wire fee. Maybe $30. That you saw on the statement. Two. Correspondent bank A took $50 off the wire. You didn't see that. Three. Correspondent bank B took another $35. You didn't see that either. Four. Correspondent bank C took $80. Still invisible. Supplier received $49,835 instead of $50,000. WhatsApp ping. 'Short $165, please top up.' Now you wire another $200. Another fee. Another week. Your customer is waiting. Your supplier is annoyed. Your margin is bleeding. Stop wiring through SWIFT for China payments. WorldFirst routes direct to Chinese accounts through MYbank. No correspondent chain. Same-day. The number you send is the number that lands. If you're a business owner paying China, you need WorldFirst. Period. WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The breakdown ($30 wire fee + $50 + $35 + $80 = $195 short) is plausible and well-constructed. Specific numbers per correspondent ($50, $35, $80) earn the $165 supplier WhatsApp gap, which makes the story believable. Risks: the supplier WhatsApp script is staged, the 'three correspondents' is an upper bound (most wires hit 1-2), and 'the number you send is the number that lands' is the standard WF overreach. 'Your supplier is annoyed' is honest framing of real importer pain, which lands well.

  • $50 plus $35 plus $80? You picked the worst case. Most of my wires the short amount is like $20-30.
  • WorldFirst, MYbank, why I never hear before? My fellow importer also never mention.
  • Direct to Chinese accounts? Including my factory's CCB account? Or only some banks?
  • Same-day with no chain? What about SAFE compliance check on the China side?
  • I just send extra $200 on every wire and call it done. Less hassle than switching providers.
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 113 words

I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. The most expensive part of your supply chain is the banks in the middle that you've never heard of. You wire $50,000 to your Chinese supplier. They get $49,800. That's $200 gone. One wire. Now run the math. 12 wires a year. That's $2,400. 24 wires. $4,800. Five years of that. $24,000. That's not a fee. That's correspondent banks deducting from the wire on the way through. SWIFT lets them. Your bank's default is SHA, which means the supplier eats every cut. So they short the order. You eat the gap. You eat the delay too. WorldFirst doesn't route through the SWIFT chain. Direct to Chinese accounts via MYbank. Same-day. Full amount lands. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

I'll say it. The most expensive part of your supply chain is the banks in the middle that you've never heard of. You wire $50,000 to your Chinese supplier. They get $49,800. That's $200 gone. One wire. Now run the math. 12 wires a year. That's $2,400. 24 wires. $4,800. Five years of that. $24,000. That's not a fee. That's correspondent banks deducting from the wire on the way through. SWIFT lets them. Your bank's default is SHA, which means the supplier eats every cut. So they short the order. You eat the gap. You eat the delay too. WorldFirst doesn't route through the SWIFT chain. Direct to Chinese accounts via MYbank. Same-day. Full amount lands. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The compound math ($200 x 24 wires x 5 years = $24K) sits on the same fragile assumption: every wire loses exactly $200. Real correspondent deductions vary, sometimes are $0 on common routes, sometimes higher on exotic ones. 'The most expensive part of your supply chain' is hyperbole, since freight, duty, and FX margin can all dwarf correspondent fees on a typical lane. SHA + 71A reference is accurate. Mechanism teaching is sound but the headline math overreaches.

  • Most expensive part of supply chain? Bro freight is more lah. By far.
  • $200 every wire compound to $24K? Only if you assume worst case 24 wires a year of exactly $200 short. Cherry-picked.
  • SHA default = supplier eats. Ok this part I learn something new actually.
  • MYbank direct, no SWIFT chain. Need to see Bank Negara approval before I switch.
  • WorldFirst sound like Wise. Same thing or different?
#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 143 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's the problem most business owners don't realize when they wire money to China. SWIFT is not a payment network. It's a messaging network. SWIFT doesn't move your money. Real banks do. Real banks called correspondents sit between your bank and your supplier's bank. A US to China wire usually hits 1 to 3 of them. Each correspondent is allowed to deduct its own processing fee, usually $15 to $50, directly from the wire amount. Your bank never warns you because the default field on your SWIFT message, 71A, is set to SHA. SHA means the supplier eats the chain. So you send $50,000. Supplier gets $49,800. They refuse to ship. You eat a week. This is how the SWIFT default works. Stop. WorldFirst doesn't use the SWIFT chain for China payments. Direct MYbank routing. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Full amount lands. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the problem most business owners don't realize when they wire money to China. SWIFT is not a payment network. It's a messaging network. SWIFT doesn't move your money. Real banks do. Real banks called correspondents sit between your bank and your supplier's bank. A US to China wire usually hits 1 to 3 of them. Each correspondent is allowed to deduct its own processing fee, usually $15 to $50, directly from the wire amount. Your bank never warns you because the default field on your SWIFT message, 71A, is set to SHA. SHA means the supplier eats the chain. So you send $50,000. Supplier gets $49,800. They refuse to ship. You eat a week. This is how the SWIFT default works. Stop. WorldFirst doesn't use the SWIFT chain for China payments. Direct MYbank routing. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Full amount lands. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Cleanest of the Hormozi set. The 'SWIFT is messaging not payment' distinction is technically accurate and underappreciated. Mechanism explanation (correspondent banks, $15-50 deductions, SHA default) checks out. Soft spots: 'A US to China wire usually hits 1 to 3' is true but the deduction amount varies wildly, and 'full amount lands' on WF side glosses over CN-side processing. Most skeptics will accept the teaching since the script earns its conclusion through mechanism rather than emotion.

  • Ok the SWIFT-is-messaging thing actually makes sense. But always 1 to 3? My direct wires to HSBC HK only one hop sometimes.
  • MYbank direct routing, is it legal for Malaysian SMB to use? Got AML clearance?
  • Sounds like the SHA default is the real problem. Can I just call my bank and switch to OUR?
  • Same-day to China? Even my Wise transfer take 1 day sometimes lah.
  • Looks like ad but the mechanism part is solid. Need pricing comparison to be convinced.
#8 PG1 Gary Vee Opinion-led 125 words

Classic Stop / Real-talk opener

Soc4.0 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Lost some of the original Gary Vee punch after polish. Still works but feels closer to baseline.

Pure original

Stop. Real talk for every business owner paying Chinese suppliers. You wired $50,000. Supplier WhatsApps you saying $49,800 landed. You think the supplier is lying. You think your bank made a mistake. Neither. SWIFT wires hop through 1 to 3 correspondent banks before they reach China. Each one is allowed to deduct $15 to $50 directly off the wire. No notification. No line item. It's built into the default. Listen. Your supplier now refuses to ship until you top up. You lose a week. You lose the delivery. You lose the customer. WorldFirst skips the SWIFT chain entirely. Direct rail through MYbank to Chinese accounts. Same-day. The number you send is the number that lands. Stop letting middle banks take a slice of every payment. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop. Real talk for everyFor every business owner paying Chinese suppliers. You wired $50,000. Supplier WhatsApps you saying $49,800 landed. You think the supplier is lying. You think your bank made a mistake. Neither. SWIFT wires hop through 1 to 3 correspondent banks before they reach China. Each one is allowed to deduct $15 to $50 directly off the wire. No notification. No line item. It's built into the default. Listen. Your supplier now refuses to ship until you top up. You lose a week. You lose the delivery. You lose the customer. WorldFirst skips the SWIFT chain entirely. Direct rail through MYbank to Chinese accounts. Same-day. The number you send is the number that lands. Stop lettingWorth knowing if middle banks takeare taking a slice of every payment. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Solid mechanism explanation. The 'supplier is lying or bank made a mistake' setup is true to how importers actually feel when this happens, which earns credibility. SHA default reference is technically accurate. Soft spots: 'no notification, no line item' is technically true but overstated since correspondent fees are disclosed in cross-border wire T&Cs even if not in real-time notifications. 'The number you send is the number that lands' on WF side ignores possible CN bank receiving fees. The mechanism beat is the script's strongest argument.

  • Yea I had this exact issue last month. Thought my supplier cheating me.
  • SHA default, OUR option, why my Maybank never tell me about these?
  • WorldFirst skips SWIFT entirely? But for compliance reporting how?
  • MYbank to Chinese accounts. So only works if supplier is on Alipay side?

#9 PG3 Gary Vee Opinion-led 108 words

Stop using your bank / right-tool reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Pure original

Stop using SWIFT for your China supplier payments. I'm serious. SWIFT is a tool. It's the wrong tool. You wouldn't use a fax machine to send a contract. So why are you using a 50-year-old messaging chain to send money to your factory? Here's what happens. You wire $50,000. Three correspondent banks each take $15 to $80 off the top. Supplier gets $49,800. Refuses to ship until the gap is topped up. You lose a week of lead time. WorldFirst is built for this. Direct MYbank rail. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Full $50,000 lands. Supplier starts that day. Use the right tool. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop using SWIFT for your China supplier payments. I'm seriousSWIFT is not the right tool for paying Chinese suppliers. SWIFT is a tool. It's the wrong tool. You wouldn't use a fax machine to send a contract. So why are you using a 50-year-old messaging chain to send money to your factory? Here's what happens. You wire $50,000. Three correspondent banks each take $15 to $80 off the top. Supplier gets $49,800. Refuses to ship until the gap is toppedWon't ship until the gap is topped up. You lose a week of lead time. WorldFirst is built for this. Direct MYbank rail. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Full $50,000 lands. Supplier starts that day. Use the right tool. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Fax machine analogy is fair and clarifies the SWIFT messaging-vs-rail distinction well. '50-year-old messaging chain' is accurate, SWIFT launched 1973. Correspondent $15-80 deduction range is in the realistic upper band. 'Full $50,000 lands' on WF side ignores CN-side receiving fees on certain account types. Overall the analogy carries the script and the mechanism is defensible.

  • Fax vs SWIFT analogy is actually clean. Helps me understand.
  • $15 to $80? My last wire short only $35. So this range fits but worst case is rare la.
  • WorldFirst direct MYbank rail. Got documentation? White paper or what?
  • Same-day to Chinese accounts. Including weekends? Or just biz days?
  • Use the right tool, sure. But switching providers also got KYC headache. Effort vs payoff?
#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 127 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about international wires to China. SWIFT is not one bank moving your money. SWIFT is a chain of 2, 3, sometimes 4 banks passing the message along. Each one in the chain is allowed to take a cut off the wire amount before passing it on. Your bank's default setting, the SHA code on field 71A, says the supplier eats those cuts. Not you. So you send $50,000. Three middle banks take a slice. Supplier sees $49,800. Refuses to start production. You're now scrambling. The bank won't help because technically nothing went wrong. This is how it works. Every single time. WorldFirst doesn't play this game. Direct rail to Chinese accounts through MYbank. No correspondent chain. The rate you see, the amount you send, is what lands. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling you about international wires to China. SWIFT is not one bank moving your money. SWIFT is a chain of 2, 3, sometimes 4 banks passing the message along. Each one in the chain is allowed to take a cut off the wire amount before passing it on. Your bank's default setting, the SHA code on field 71A, says the supplier eats those cuts. Not you. So you send $50,000. Three middle banks take a slice. Supplier sees $49,800. Refuses toWon't start production. You're now scrambling. The bank won't helpcan't reverse it because technically nothing went wrong. This is how it works. Every single time. WorldFirst doesn't play this gameis built differently. Direct rail to Chinese accounts through MYbank. No correspondent chain. The rate you see, the amount you send, is what lands. PeriodThe amount you send is what lands.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 'SWIFT is a chain of 2-4 banks' breakdown is accurate. Field 71A SHA reference is technically correct. 'Bank can't reverse it because technically nothing went wrong' is honest framing of how dispute resolution actually goes. Risks: 'the amount you send is what lands' on WF side overstates because CN-side bank fees can still apply, and 'three middle banks' is an upper bound. Mechanism is the strongest part of the script.

  • 2 to 4 banks passing the message? So SWIFT is just a chat group basically lol.
  • Bank can't reverse because nothing went wrong. That part rings true, happened to my cousin.
  • Amount you send is what lands. Are you saying CN receiving bank don't charge at all?
  • WorldFirst is built differently. How? Different license? Different network? Be specific.
  • Field 71A SHA, I need to call my bank to switch to OUR or it's in the app?

#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 128 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. Your wire to China was supposed to clear in 2 days. It's day 5. Your supplier hasn't seen a dollar and your factory slot is gone. Here's why. Three correspondent banks in the SWIFT chain. Each one takes a business day. That's 3 days right there. Each one shaves 15 to 50 dollars. That's 150 dollars off a 50K wire, invisible, no line item. Now do this twice a month. That's 300 dollars a month. 3,600 a year. Over 5 years your bank's middlemen quietly took 18,000 dollars from your supplier payments and burned 60 days of factory time on top. That's a vacation. That's inventory. That's a hire. WorldFirst skips the SWIFT chain. CIPS rails direct to Chinese accounts. Same-day landing. Full amount. Stop. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. Your wire to China was supposed to clear in 2 days. It's day 5. Your supplier hasn't seen a dollar and your factory slot is gone. Here's why. Three correspondent banks in the SWIFT chain. Each one takes a business day. That's 3 days right there. Each one shaves 15 to 50 dollars. That's 150 dollars off a 50K wire, invisible, no line item. Now do this twice a month. That's 300 dollars a month. 3,600 a year. Over 5 years your bank's middlemen quietly took 18,000 dollars from your supplier payments and burned 60 days of factory time on top. That's a vacation. That's inventory. That's a hire. WorldFirst skips the SWIFT chain. CIPS rails direct to Chinese accounts. Same-day landing. Full amount. Stop. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The compounding math (18K over 5 years, 60 days of factory time) is the highest-risk claim. It assumes every wire hits 3 correspondents and 50-dollar full deductions, plus assumes the 'factory time' lost stacks linearly, which it does not. Most SMBs reschedule production once and absorb the delay rather than losing 3 full days per wire. 'A vacation, inventory, a hire' is emotive framing on top of an extrapolation. Skeptics will pick the math apart.

  • 60 days factory time over 5 years? Bro you double counting lah.
  • I don't wire 50K twice a month for 5 years straight. Numbers cooked.
  • Where the 50 dollar per hop come from? Got source?
  • Same day to China sounds too good. Bank also got cutoff time.
  • Vacation hire inventory... feel like ad script.
#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 123 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You think your bank moves the money. They don't. They hand it to another bank. That bank hands it to another bank. By the time it reaches Shenzhen, three middlemen each took a day and 50 bucks. You call the bank to chase. They blame the correspondent. The correspondent blames the receiving bank. The receiving bank blames compliance. Compliance says the beneficiary name was missing Co Ltd. Nobody owns it. Meanwhile your supplier is texting you every day at 2am asking where the money is. Your factory slot is gone. Your customer is asking why. I see this every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. CIPS direct to Chinese accounts. Same-day. Full amount. One platform, one accountability line. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Real talk for everyFor every business owner sourcing from China. You think your bank moves the money. They don't. They hand it to another bank. That bank hands it to another bank. By the time it reaches Shenzhen, three middlemen each took a day and 50 bucks. You call the bank to chase. They blame the correspondent. The correspondent blames the receiving bank. The receiving bank blames compliance. Compliance says the beneficiary name was missing Co Ltd. Nobody owns it. Meanwhile your supplier is texting you every day at 2am asking where the money is. Your factory slot is gone. Your customer is asking why. I see thisThis happens every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. CIPS direct to Chinese accounts. Same-day. Full amount. One platform, one accountability line. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The blame-shuffle pattern (bank to correspondent to receiving to compliance) is a very real SMB experience and lands as authentic. 'Supplier texting you every day at 2am' is emotive but plausible for Asia corridor. Main risk: 'one platform, one accountability line' is a structural claim that's mostly true for WF but glosses over the fact that WF still has CN local banking partners who can be a point of failure. 'Factory slot is gone' assumes worst case.

  • Factory slot gone from one delay? Some maybe lah, most negotiable.
  • 2am supplier text feeling real. That part hit.
  • One accountability line means what, you guys also got partners right.
  • This happens every week, ok then where the testimonial?
  • Got Malaysia regulator approval? I check first before move my money.
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 160 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. If you're a business owner wiring money to Chinese suppliers, your wire is sitting in a queue right now and you don't even know it. Here's the deal. Your bank quoted 2 days. It's day 5. Your supplier is still chasing you. Two things are happening. One. Correspondent banks. A US to China SWIFT wire hops through 1 to 3 of them. Each one takes a full business day to process. Each one shaves 15 to 50 dollars in fees before passing the money on. Two. Compliance review. Chinese banks check the beneficiary name letter for letter. If your wire says Shenzhen ABC Trading but the registered name is Shenzhen ABC Trading Co Ltd, the wire pauses for 3 to 10 days. Sometimes it gets returned. So on a 50K wire your supplier sees 49,850 land a week late. Stop using SWIFT for China. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. If you're a business owner wiring money to Chinese suppliers, your wire is sitting in a queue right now and you don't even know it. Here's the dealHere's how it works. Your bank quoted 2 days. It's day 5. Your supplier is still chasing you. Two things are happening. One. Correspondent banks. A US to China SWIFT wire hops through 1 to 3 of them. Each one takes a full business day to process. Each one shaves 15 to 50 dollars in fees before passing the money on. Two. Compliance review. Chinese banks check the beneficiary name letter for letter. If your wire says Shenzhen ABC Trading but the registered name is Shenzhen ABC Trading Co Ltd, the wire pauses for 3 to 10 days. Sometimes it gets returned. So on a 50K wire your supplier sees 49,850 land a week late. Stop using SWIFT for China. Period. WorldFirst routes outside the SWIFT chain via CIPS and MYbank rails. Most payments clear same-day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core mechanism (correspondent bank hops adding days, SHA fee deductions, name-mismatch compliance pauses) is accurate and matches industry data. The 49,850 figure is fair given the 15 to 50 dollar per hop range. Main risks: 'most wires same-day' on WorldFirst side is true for MYbank-routed CNY payments but not for all WF flows, and 'sometimes returned' is presented as routine when full returns are the minority case (most pause for review then clear).

  • 3 correspondent banks every time? My Maybank wire to China usually 2 days only.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never heard. Got license in Malaysia?
  • CIPS rails what is that, sound very technical.
  • Same day to China? My bank also say same day for SWIFT one.
  • Name mismatch case ok fair, that one I see before.
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 134 words

Accusation opener

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

Your bank lied to you about the 2-day wire and you let them. Here's how. They quoted 2 business days to Shenzhen. They didn't tell you the money goes through 3 correspondent banks first. Each one sits on it for a business day. That's day 2, day 3, day 4 gone before the money even touches a Chinese bank. Then Chinese bank compliance checks the beneficiary name. Yours said Shenzhen ABC Trading. Registered name has Co Ltd on the end. Wire paused for review. Day 5 your supplier finally sees the money. 150 bucks short from correspondent fees. Factory slot already given to someone else. I'll say it. SWIFT is built for banks, not for business owners paying China. WorldFirst uses CIPS and MYbank rails. Direct to Chinese accounts. Most wires same-day. Full amount. Stop pretending SWIFT is your only option. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Your bank lied to you about the 2-day wire and you let themquoted you 2 days for the wire and that's not what you got. Here's howHere's why. They quoted 2 business days to Shenzhen. They didn't tell youmention the money goes through 3 correspondent banks first. Each one sits on it for a business day. That's day 2, day 3, day 4 gone before the money even touches a Chinese bank. Then Chinese bank compliance checks the beneficiary name. Yours said Shenzhen ABC Trading. Registered name has Co Ltd on the end. Wire paused for review. Day 5 your supplier finally sees the money. 150 bucks short from correspondent fees. Factory slot already given to someone else. I'll say it. SWIFT is built for banks, not for business owners paying China. WorldFirst uses CIPS and MYbank rails. Direct to Chinese accounts. Most wires same-day. Full amount. Stop pretending SWIFT is your only option. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'Quoted you 2 days for the wire and that's not what you got' is softer than the original 'lied' framing but still places fault entirely on the bank. The 2-day quote is technically what banks advertise but it's a 'business days, subject to' caveat banks do disclose. 'Full amount' on WF side overstates if CN receiving bank charges apply. The Shenzhen ABC Trading example is illustrative but presented as if it always happens; name-mismatch holds are a real ~5-10% case, not universal.

  • Bank already say business days plus subject to compliance. Not lying.
  • Full amount land? Sure or not? My BOC charge me also.
  • Co Ltd missing thing real but not every wire fail this way.
  • WorldFirst how long got? New company I scared.
  • Factory slot already given to someone? Bit dramatic la.
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 141 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you wire money to China, listen. Real talk. Last week you sent 50K to a Shenzhen supplier. Four things went wrong and you only blamed one of them. One. The wire took 5 days instead of 2. You blamed your bank. Two. The amount landed 150 short. You blamed your supplier for miscounting. Three. The factory pushed back your production date 5 days. You blamed shipping. Four. Your customer asked why their delivery slipped. You blamed customs. All four trace back to one thing. SWIFT correspondent banks. 3 hops, 3 days, 150 dollars in fees deducted invisibly. Then compliance review because the beneficiary name had Trading instead of Trading Co Ltd. Stop using SWIFT for China. WorldFirst uses CIPS and MYbank rails. No middlemen. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Full amount. If you're a business owner paying China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

If you wire money to China, listen. Real talkthis is worth knowing. Last week you sent 50K to a Shenzhen supplier. Four things went wrong and you only blamed one of them. One. The wire took 5 days instead of 2. You blamed your bank. Two. The amount landed 150 short. You blamed your supplier for miscounting. Three. The factory pushed back your production date 5 days. You blamed shipping. Four. Your customer asked why their delivery slipped. You blamed customs. All four trace back to one thing. SWIFT correspondent banks. 3 hops, 3 days, 150 dollars in fees deducted invisibly. Then compliance review because the beneficiary name had Trading instead of Trading Co Ltd. Stop using SWIFT for China. WorldFirst uses CIPS and MYbank rails. No middlemen. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Full amount. If you're a business owner paying China, you need WorldFirst. Period. WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The four-things-stack is educational and the SWIFT root cause traceback is technically sound. Main red flag is attributing all four downstream consequences (customer delay, factory pushback, etc.) to one wire's delay; in reality factories have buffer slots and customers tolerate some slip. The 'last week you sent 50K to Shenzhen' framing assumes a recent specific incident the viewer hasn't actually had. 'Full amount' on WF side has the same slight overreach issue.

  • Customer cancel because of one wire delay? My customer not so strict lah.
  • Factory pushback 5 days for 5 day wire delay 1 to 1? Reality not so clean.
  • Where the 150 number come from? Show actual SWIFT receipt.
  • WorldFirst Bank Negara approved or not? Got check before I move money.
  • Co Ltd thing happen to me once. Annoying but learn already.
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 114 words

I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. SWIFT was built for banks talking to each other, not for business owners paying Chinese factories. Your wire goes through 3 correspondent banks. 3 business days. 150 dollars deducted invisibly. Now stack it. 24 wires a year. That's 72 days of delay across the year and 3,600 dollars eaten by middlemen. Across 5 years. 18,000 dollars. A full quarter of factory time lost in transit. And here's the kicker. Half of China-bound wires also get held at compliance because the beneficiary name has a missing Co Ltd or wrong character. Another week of delay. Another supplier waiting. WorldFirst uses CIPS and MYbank rails. No correspondent chain. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

I'll say it. SWIFT was built for banks talking to each other, not for business owners paying Chinese factories. Your wire goes through 3 correspondent banks. 3 business days. 150 dollars deducted invisibly. Now stack it. 24 wires a year. That's 72 days of delay across the year and 3,600 dollars eaten by middlemen. Across 5 years. 18,000 dollars. A full quarter of factory time lost in transit. And here's the kickerpart most business owners miss. Half of China-bound wires also get held at compliance because the beneficiary name has a missing Co Ltd or wrong character. Another week of delay. Another supplier waiting. WorldFirst uses CIPS and MYbank rails. No correspondent chain. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 72 days of delay per year is the riskiest claim. It assumes every wire is delayed 3 days and the delays don't overlap or get absorbed by buffer. In reality only ~8-15% of wires have material delay so the real annualized delay is closer to 6-10 days, not 72. 18K over 5 years extrapolation also assumes 24 wires/year at exactly 150 deduction every time. 'No correspondent chain' on WF side is correct for CIPS routing but not universal.

  • 72 days delay a year? You assume every wire late which is BS.
  • 18K over 5 years, where the math? Show calculator.
  • Half of China wires held at compliance? Where data come from?
  • Most business owners miss this part... ok fair, I didn't know that part actually.
  • Same day to China is the hook but no proof in the post.
#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 155 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's the part most business owners never see when they wire money to China. SWIFT is not a bank. It's a messaging network. The actual money moves through correspondent banks in the middle, real banks, real people, real processing time. A US to China wire usually hops through 1 to 3 of them. Each hop is a business day. Each hop deducts 15 to 50 dollars from the amount in flight. Nobody tells you. The receipt shows what you sent. The supplier sees what landed. The gap goes to banks in the middle. Then Chinese bank compliance reviews the beneficiary name against the company registration. If anything is off by a single character, the wire pauses for days. So on a 50K wire your supplier gets 49,850, three days late. Stop. WorldFirst routes through CIPS and MYbank rails, no SWIFT correspondent chain. Direct to the Chinese bank account. Same-day on most wires. Full amount. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the part most business owners never see when they wire money to China. SWIFT is not a bank. It's a messaging network. The actual money moves through correspondent banks in the middle, real banks, real people, real processing time. A US to China wire usually hops through 1 to 3 of them. Each hop is a business day. Each hop deducts 15 to 50 dollars from the amount in flight. Nobody tells you. The receipt shows what you sent. The supplier sees what landed. The gap goes to banks in the middle. Then Chinese bank compliance reviews the beneficiary name against the company registration. If anything is off by a single character, the wire pauses for days. So on a 50K wire your supplier gets 49,850, three days late. Stop. WorldFirst routes through CIPS and MYbank rails, no SWIFT correspondent chain. Direct to the Chinese bank account. Same-day on most wires. Full amount. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Cleanest of the Hormozi set. The SWIFT-is-messaging-not-money distinction is accurate and underappreciated. 1 to 3 correspondent hops with 15-50 dollar deductions matches industry data. Name compliance review framing is honest. 'Same-day on most wires' is correctly hedged with 'most'. Main residual risk is 'no SWIFT correspondent chain' on WF side which is true for CNY via MYbank but not for all WF currency pairs. Most skeptics will accept this teaching.

  • Ok the SWIFT is just messaging part actually useful. Didn't know that.
  • Most wires same day, ok at least you hedge. Better than other ads.
  • MYbank rails what level of license they got with PBOC?
  • How WorldFirst make money if no chain fees? Margin somewhere right?
  • Got actual rate vs bank screenshot anywhere?
#8 PG1 Gary Vee Opinion-led 118 words

Classic Stop / Real-talk opener

Soc4.0 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Lost some of the original Gary Vee punch after polish. Still works but feels closer to baseline.

Pure original

Stop. Real talk for every business owner paying suppliers in China. Your wire was quoted 2 days. It's day 5. Your supplier is chasing you. Here's why. Three correspondent banks in the SWIFT chain. Each one is a full business day. Each one shaves 15 to 50 dollars. On a 50K wire your supplier sees 49,850 land 3 days late. And it's not over. Chinese bank compliance also reviews the beneficiary name. Miss the Co Ltd on the end and your wire pauses another week. Listen. WorldFirst doesn't use SWIFT correspondent banks for China. CIPS and MYbank rails. Direct to the Chinese account. Most wires clear same-day. Full amount lands. Stop letting SWIFT eat your supplier relationships. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop. Real talk for everyFor every business owner paying suppliers in China. Your wire was quoted 2 days. It's day 5. Your supplier is chasing you. Here's why. Three correspondent banks in the SWIFT chain. Each one is a full business day. Each one shaves 15 to 50 dollars. On a 50K wire your supplier sees 49,850 land 3 days late. And it's not over. Chinese bank compliance also reviews the beneficiary name. Miss the Co Ltd on the end and your wire pauses another week. Listen. WorldFirst doesn't use SWIFT correspondent banks for China. CIPS and MYbank rails. Direct to the Chinese account. Most wires clear same-day. Full amount lands. Stop letting SWIFT eat your supplier relationshipsWorth knowing if SWIFT delays are costing you supplier trust. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Mechanism is solid. The escalation from 'wire late' to 'compliance pauses another week' is technically possible but stacks two worst-case scenarios on the same wire when in practice most wires hit one or neither. 'Most wires clear same-day' is correctly hedged. 'Worth knowing if SWIFT delays are costing you supplier trust' is fair soft CTA. Main residual risk is the assumption SWIFT is universally slow when many EU and SG corridors are faster.

  • Two things in one wire happen same time? Rare la.
  • My SWIFT to China usually fine, only one time delay.
  • Worth knowing... ok at least not pushy. But still don't know who you are.
  • CIPS what is that, China only right? My bank no CIPS access.
  • MYbank means Ant Group right? Got data privacy concern.
#9 PG3 Gary Vee Opinion-led 116 words

Stop using SWIFT / right-tool reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Pure original

Stop using SWIFT to pay Chinese suppliers. I'm serious. SWIFT is a tool. It's the wrong tool for this job. You wouldn't fax a contract in 2026. So why are you running your supplier payments on a network designed in 1973? Here's what happens. You wire 50K. Three correspondent banks. Three business days lost. 150 dollars deducted invisibly. Then Chinese bank compliance pauses your wire because the beneficiary name was Trading instead of Trading Co Ltd. Day 7 your supplier sees the money. Day 8 production starts. Your customer already cancelled. WorldFirst is built for this job. CIPS and MYbank rails. Direct to the Chinese account. Same-day. Full amount. Use the right tool. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop using SWIFT to pay Chinese suppliers. I'm seriousSWIFT is not the right tool for paying Chinese suppliers. SWIFT is a tool. It's the wrong tool for this job. You wouldn't fax a contract in 2026. So why are you running your supplier payments on a network designed in 1973? Here's what happens. You wire 50K. Three correspondent banks. Three business days lost. 150 dollars deducted invisibly. Then Chinese bank compliance pauses your wire because the beneficiary name was Trading instead of Trading Co Ltd. Day 7 your supplier sees the money. Day 8 production starts. Your customer already cancelled. WorldFirst is built for this job. CIPS and MYbank rails. Direct to the Chinese account. Same-day. Full amount. Use the right tool. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 'SWIFT designed in 1973' framing is historically accurate (SWIFT founded 1973). 'Right tool for the job' positioning is fair B2B framing. Risk: 'Your customer already cancelled' is escalation that stacks worst-case outcomes. The Trading vs Trading Co Ltd example is real but presented as if it always blocks payment. WF/CIPS positioning is largely accurate but 'same-day, full amount' is a clean ideal that has CN-side caveats.

  • SWIFT 1973 fair, but they update the system right? Not the same as fax.
  • Customer cancel from one payment delay? You move in tier 1 only is it.
  • Use the right tool... ok but who regulate WorldFirst?
  • Co Ltd thing real, happen to my friend once.
  • How long WorldFirst been around? New name to me.
#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 127 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about wires to China. The 2-day quote is a fiction. SWIFT isn't a network that moves money. It's just a messaging system that tells banks to move money. Real banks in the middle do the actual work. 1 to 3 of them on every US to China wire. Each one takes a day. Each one quietly takes 15 to 50 dollars off the top before passing it on. You'll never see those fees on a statement because they're not your bank's fees. They're the middlemen's. And the SHA setting your bank picks by default means your supplier eats every one of those deductions. WorldFirst doesn't play this game. CIPS rails. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The amount you send is the amount they receive. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the truth nobody's telling yousomething most business owners don't know about wires to China. The 2-day quote is a fictionoptimistic. SWIFT isn't a network that moves money. It's just a messaging system that tells banks to move money. Real banks in the middle do the actual work. 1 to 3 of them on every US to China wire. Each one takes a day. Each one quietly takes 15 to 50 dollars off the top before passing it on. You'll never see those fees on a statement because they're not your bank's fees. They're the middlemen's. And the SHA setting your bank picks by default means your supplier eatscovers every one of those deductions. WorldFirst doesn't play this gameis built differently. CIPS rails. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The amount you send is the amount they receive. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Cleanest of the Gary Vee set. The SWIFT-as-messaging vs banks-as-movers distinction is accurate and educational. 1-3 hops, 15-50 dollar deductions, SHA-default framing all match the source findings. 'Optimistic' instead of 'fiction' is softer and defensible. 'The amount you send is the amount they receive' on WF side is slightly overstated because CN receiving bank fees can still apply on some accounts, but the polish version drops 'period' which softens the absolute.

  • SWIFT just messaging not actual money, that part useful actually.
  • SHA default thing is true, my bank never explain to me.
  • Amount sent equal amount received? CN bank also charge ah.
  • WorldFirst is built differently... ok how exactly. Tell me the rail.
  • Regulated in MY or only US/UK? Need to check.
Card P1-A6Pillar P1Short-form 60s · bilingualTopic 05Reframe
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 137 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. You pay your Chinese supplier 20,000 dollars in USD because it feels safer. It just cost you 460 dollars extra and you didn't feel a thing. Here's why. Two conversions. Your bank does USD on the way out, 1.5% margin, 300 dollars. Supplier's bank does CNY on the way in, 0.8% margin, 160 dollars. Total leakage: 460 dollars. Now run that across the year. 12 orders. That's 5,500 dollars sitting inside someone else's spread. Run two years. 11,000. Run five. 27,500. That's a hire. That's a container. That's your kid's school fees. And it's not a fee line. It's the rate. Listen. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% total. 60 to 100 dollars on the same 20K order. The other 360 stays in your business. Every order. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. You pay your Chinese supplier 20,000 dollars in USD because it feels safer. It just cost you 460 dollars extra and you didn't feel a thing. Here's why. Two conversions. Your bank does USD on the way out, 1.5% margin, 300 dollars. Supplier's bank does CNY on the way in, 0.8% margin, 160 dollars. Total leakage: 460 dollars. Now run that across the year. 12 orders. That's 5,500 dollars sitting inside someone else's spread. Run two years. 11,000. Run five. 27,500. That's a hire. That's a container. That's your kid's school fees. And it's not a fee line. It's the rate. Listen. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% total. 60 to 100 dollars on the same 20K order. The other 360 stays in your business. Every order. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Compounding math (460 per order x 12 = 5,500/yr) is internally consistent with the per-order figure. Assumes constant order size and frequency, which is a model not a fact. The hire/container/school-fees framing is rhetorical, not load-bearing. The 1.5% sender + 0.8% receiver high-end is real but cherry-picked at the upper bound. WorldFirst CNY-direct cost claim (60-100 on 20K at 0.3-0.5%) aligns with findings.

  • 460 sounds inflated. My BCA rate not that bad.
  • 12 orders a year very different from my volume. I do 4.
  • Container? bro it's a tiktok not a sales call
  • But what if cny rate moves against me? Usd more predictable for me
  • Show me a real receipt
#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 135 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk for every business owner who quotes in USD because it feels professional. The dollar feels safer. It costs you 2% more. Every single order. You wire 20K USD to your Shenzhen factory. Your bank takes 1% on the conversion out. Their bank takes 0.5% on the conversion in. That's 300 dollars gone before the goods touch a container. Then your supplier waits a week for SAFE compliance. They take the currency risk while it sits. They pad the next quote 2 to 5% to cover the wait. You're paying for safety that exists in your head, not in your books. I see this every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst pays the supplier in CNY directly. Same bank account. Same supplier. One conversion at 0.3 to 0.5%. Half the cost. Same-day in 80% of cases. Search WorldFirst. Now.
