#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 150 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers in USD because it feels safer, you're paying 2% extra for that feeling. Here's the math. Your USD wire to a Chinese factory goes through two exchange rate conversions. Your bank takes 0.5 to 1.5% turning your local currency into USD. Your supplier's bank takes another 0.3 to 0.8% turning USD back into CNY. That stack is invisible because it's baked into the rate, not the fee line. On a 20,000 dollar order, you've quietly given up 160 to 460 dollars before anything ships. Then your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for the USD to clear. So next quote, they pad your USD price by 2 to 5% to cover the wait. Stop paying twice for the same feeling. WorldFirst pays your supplier in CNY directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% total. Same payment. Same destination. Half the cost. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers in USD because it feels safer, you're paying 2% extra for that feeling. Here's the math. Your USD wire to a Chinese factory goes through two exchange rate conversions. Your bank takes 0.5 to 1.5% turning your local currency into USD. Your supplier's bank takes another 0.3 to 0.8% turning USD back into CNY. That stack is invisible because it's baked into the rate, not the fee line. On a 20,000 dollar order, you've quietly given up 160 to 460 dollars before anything ships. Then your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for the USD to clear. So next quote, they pad your USD price by 2 to 5% to cover the wait. Stop paying twice for the same feeling. Worth knowing before the next PI lands. WorldFirst pays your supplier in CNY directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% total. Same payment. Same destination. Half the cost. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core mechanism (two stacked FX margins on USD wire to China, supplier wait, padded next quote) is grounded in the card findings. The 0.5 to 1.5% sender-side and 0.3 to 0.8% receiver-side margins are typical-case ranges, defensible. The 'next quote padded 2 to 5%' is a real industry pattern but presented as universal when it's situational. 'Half the cost' is roughly accurate against the 0.8-2.3% USD stack vs 0.3-0.5% CNY route, but supplier-side bank fees on CNY receipt can still apply in edge cases. WorldFirst CNY-direct claim aligns with CIPS/MYbank rails described in findings.

  • Where got 2%? My Maybank quote not so off lah.
  • My supplier always quote in USD only. Never offer CNY price.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never hear before. Got MAS license?
  • Sounds like ad
  • How they get same-day to china? Public holiday how?
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 151 words

Accusation opener

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

You think paying in USD protects you. It doesn't. It protects your bank. Here's the truth. Every USD wire to a Chinese supplier hits two banks taking exchange rate margin. Your bank, 0.5 to 1.5% on the way out. Their bank, 0.3 to 0.8% on the way back into CNY. You don't see either one because they're baked into the rate, not the fee. On a 20,000 dollar order that's up to 460 dollars gone before the goods even ship. Then your supplier waits a week for the USD to clear. Their cash flow tightens. So next PI they quote you 2 to 5% higher to cover the wait. You just paid extra twice. I'll say it. The dollar feels safer because it's familiar. It's not cheaper. It's not faster. It's just familiar. WorldFirst pays your supplier in CNY directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5%. Same supplier. Same bank account. Lower cost. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

You think paying in USD protects you. It doesn't. It protects your bank. Here's the truthsituation. Every USD wire to a Chinese supplier hits two banks taking exchange rate margin. Your bank, 0.5 to 1.5% on the way out. Their bank, 0.3 to 0.8% on the way back into CNY. You don't see either one because they're baked into the rate, not the fee. On a 20,000 dollar order that's up to 460 dollars gone before the goods even ship. Then your supplier waits a week for the USD to clear. Their cash flow tightens. So next PI they quote you 2 to 5% higher to cover the wait. You just paid extra twice. I'll say it. The dollar feels safer because it's familiar. It's not cheaper. It's not faster. It's just familiar. WorldFirst pays your supplier in CNY directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5%. Same supplier. Same bank account. Lower cost. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Accusation framing ('protects your bank, not you') is sharp but defensible because the bank is the structural beneficiary of FX margin. Two-conversion mechanism and the 2-5% padded-next-quote are both in findings. 'You just paid extra twice' is a fair summary. Risk: 'familiar, not cheaper, not faster' is accurate but USD is sometimes faster in practice for SEA banks without strong CNY rails, so 'not faster' is situational.

  • Familiar IS protection when supplier disputes. Try arguing in CNY.
  • ok but invoice in usd is industry standard, can't just switch
  • this is one of those finance influencer ads right
  • My accountant only knows usd. CNY books become a mess.
  • 0.3% sounds too good. What's the catch?
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 158 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you pay Chinese suppliers in USD, you need to hear this. You think you're paying one cost. You're paying four. One. Your bank's USD conversion margin, 0.5 to 1.5%, hidden in the rate. Two. Your supplier's bank CNY conversion margin, 0.3 to 0.8%, hidden in their rate. Three. Their 7 to 10 day wait for the USD to clear, which costs them cash flow. Four. The 2 to 5% padding they bake into the next USD quote to cover that wait. Add it up on a 20,000 dollar order. 460 dollars in spread. 700 dollars in next-quote padding. 1,160 dollars on one purchase order. And the USD price never even moved on paper. Real talk. The dollar feels safer because it's the currency you think in. It is not the cheapest currency to send to China. WorldFirst pays your supplier in CNY directly. One conversion at 0.3 to 0.5%. Same supplier. Same bank account. Half the cost. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

If you pay Chinese suppliers in USD, you need to hear this. You think you're paying one cost. You're paying four. One. Your bank's USD conversion margin, 0.5 to 1.5%, hidden in the rate. Two. Your supplier's bank CNY conversion margin, 0.3 to 0.8%, hidden in their rate. Three. Their 7 to 10 day wait for the USD to clear, which costs them cash flow. Four. The 2 to 5% padding they bake into the next USD quote to cover that wait. Add it up on a 20,000 dollar order. 460 dollars in spread. 700 dollars in next-quote padding. 1,160 dollars on one purchase order. And the USD price never even moved on paper. Real talk. The dollar feels safer because it's the currency you think in. It is not the cheapest currency to send to China. WorldFirst pays your supplier in CNY directly. One conversion at 0.3 to 0.5%. Same supplier. Same bank account. Half the cost. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Four-cost stack (sender margin, receiver margin, supplier wait, padded quote) is grounded in findings. Math (460 spread + 700 padding = 1,160 on 20K) is the upper bound, presented confidently. The 700 padding figure assumes 3.5% next-quote padding, which is the midpoint of 2-5% range. Defensible but additive presentation makes it feel guaranteed when 3 and 4 are next-order effects, not same-order. Closing claim 'half the cost' aligns with findings.

  • 1,160 on 20K is 5.8% — that's insane. Real number?
  • padding 700? my supplier doesn't pad like that
  • Four costs is alot to remember. tldr?
  • Currency I think in matters for tax reporting btw
  • Sounds plausible but I'm scrolling past, will think tmr

I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

Pure original

I'll say it. Paying Chinese suppliers in USD is the most expensive habit in your supply chain and nobody talks about it. You wire 20K in USD. Two banks take a slice of the rate. Yours, 1%. Theirs, 0.5%. That's 300 dollars on one order. Now run the math. 12 orders a year. That's 3,600. Two years. 7,200. Five years. 18,000. That's a salary. That's a container. That's product development. And here's what your bank won't tell you. Both margins are buried in the rate. You'll never see a line item. The USD price feels locked in, but it isn't. WorldFirst pays the supplier in CNY directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% total. Same supplier bank. Same payment. The difference goes back into your business. Period.

#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 148 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's the part most business owners miss about paying suppliers in USD. The USD price on the PI is not locked in. You see 20,000 USD and think that's the cost. Wrong. Two conversions happen. Your bank converts your local currency to USD at their card rate, not the rate on Google. That's 0.5 to 1.5% margin you never see. Then the wire lands in your supplier's Chinese bank. Chinese accounts hold CNY, not USD. So the receiving bank converts USD to CNY at their card rate. Another 0.3 to 0.8% margin. Stacked. Invisible. Baked into the rate, never the fee line. On a 20K order that's 160 to 460 dollars vanished into spread. Plus your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for clearance and SAFE compliance review. Stop. WorldFirst sends CNY direct over CIPS or MYbank rails. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% all-in. 80% land same-day. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Your bank converts your local currency to USD at their card rate, not the rate on Google. That's 0.5 to 1.5% margin you never see. Then the wire lands in your supplier's Chinese bank. Chinese accounts hold CNY, not USD. So the receiving bank converts USD to CNY at their card rate. Another 0.3 to 0.8% margin. Stacked. Invisible. Baked into the rate, never the fee line. On a 20K order that's 160 to 460 dollars vanished into spread. Plus your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for clearance and SAFE compliance review. Stop. WorldFirst sends CNY direct over CIPS or MYbank rails. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5% all-in. 80% land same-day. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Most mechanism-tight script in the pack. Card-rate vs Google-rate framing is accurate. SAFE compliance review on inbound USD to Chinese accounts is real. CIPS/MYbank claim with 80% same-day matches findings exactly. 'Half the cost' is defensible. Main minor stretch: '7 to 10 day wait' is the upper-bound supplier-side delay; many wires clear in 3 to 5 days in practice. Not a fact issue, just a typical-case framing presented as norm.

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Pure original

Stop sending USD to Chinese suppliers. I'm serious. USD is a tool. It's the wrong tool for paying a country that runs on CNY. You wouldn't pay a Vietnam factory in baht. So why are you paying a Shenzhen factory in dollars? Here's what happens. Your bank converts your local currency to USD, 1% margin. Their bank converts USD to CNY, 0.5% margin. Two conversions on one payment. 300 dollars gone on a 20K order. Plus a 7 to 10 day wait for clearance. Plus a padded quote next time. WorldFirst is built for this. Pays the supplier in CNY directly over CIPS or MYbank rails. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5%. Same bank account they already use. Use the currency the country uses. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop sending USD to Chinese suppliers. I'm seriousSending USD to Chinese suppliers is the wrong default. USD is a tool. It's the wrong tool for paying a country that runs on CNY. You wouldn't pay a Vietnam factory in baht. So why are you paying a Shenzhen factory in dollars? Here's what happens. Your bank converts your local currency to USD, 1% margin. Their bank converts USD to CNY, 0.5% margin. Two conversions on one payment. 300 dollars gone on a 20K order. Plus a 7 to 10 day wait for clearance. Plus a padded quote next time. WorldFirst is built for this. Pays the supplier in CNY directly over CIPS or MYbank rails. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5%. Same bank account they already use. Use the currency the country uses. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Vietnam-baht analogy is a strong rhetorical move but technically imperfect because Vietnam factories can and sometimes do accept USD as a stable peg currency, so the parallel isn't perfectly clean. Mechanism numbers (1% + 0.5%, 300 on 20K) are mid-range and defensible. CIPS/MYbank claim and same-bank-account-they-already-use are in findings. 'Wrong tool' framing is sharp.

  • Vietnam factories do take USD actually, weak analogy
  • switching currency in PI means renegotiating contract. Not simple
  • Same bank account? You sure? My supplier got cny only account meh
  • Sounds like an ad
  • 300 saved one time is not worth changing my whole workflow
#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 142 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about paying suppliers in USD. The dollar doesn't protect you. It just hides where the money goes. Your bank quotes USD. You think the price is locked. It isn't. They convert your local currency at their card rate, 1% margin. The USD lands at your supplier's Chinese bank. Chinese accounts hold CNY, not USD. So the receiving bank converts again, 0.5% margin. Two banks. Two spreads. Both invisible. On a 20K order, 300 dollars vanishes inside the rate. Then your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for the USD to clear, takes the currency risk, and pads the next quote to cover both. This is how it works on every USD wire to China. WorldFirst pays in CNY directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5%. The rate you see is the rate the supplier gets. That's the difference. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling you about paying suppliers in USD. The dollar doesn't protect you. It just hides where the money goes. Your bank quotes USD. You think the price is locked. It isn't. They convert your local currency at their card rate, 1% margin. The USD lands at your supplier's Chinese bank. Chinese accounts hold CNY, not USD. So the receiving bank converts again, 0.5% margin. Two banks. Two spreads. Both invisible. On a 20K order, 300 dollars vanishes inside the rate. Then your supplier waits 7 to 10 days for the USD to clear, takes the currency risk, and pads the next quote to cover both. This is how it works on every USD wire to China. WorldFirst pays in CNY directly. One conversion. 0.3 to 0.5%. The rate you see is the rate the supplier gets. That's the difference. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Cleanest mechanism explanation in the GV pack. Card-rate, two conversions, supplier currency risk, padded next quote — all in findings. 'Rate you see is the rate the supplier gets' is roughly accurate for CNY-direct (still has 0.3-0.5% margin, but it's transparent). Risk: 'doesn't protect you, just hides where the money goes' is opinion presented as truth, fine for the GV voice.

  • Card rate vs spot rate — ok this explains a lot
  • But cny price will move on me, who absorbs that risk now?
  • Receiving bank really convert again? Thought usd account also got
  • What is the catch with WorldFirst seriously
  • Tagging my partner to watch
Card P1-A6Pillar P1Short-form 60s · bilingualTopic 05Supplier-perspective
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 139 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. Your last USD quote from your Chinese supplier was padded by 3%. On a 20K order, that's 600 dollars baked in. You never saw the line item. Now do that every order. Ten orders a year, that's 6,000. Five years, that's 30,000 dollars your supplier collected just to cover what your USD wire does to them on their end. Why? Because USD hitting a Chinese bank means 7 to 10 days of waiting, a forex fee on conversion, and FX risk on their books. They priced that into your quote. Listen. Ask for the CNY price. The padding has no reason to exist. Suppliers drop quotes 1 to 3% when you pay in their currency. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese accounts, same-day, 80% land in hours. No padding. No waiting. Cleaner price every order. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. Your last USD quote from your Chinese supplier was padded by 3%. On a 20K order, that's 600 dollars baked in. You never saw the line item. Now do that every order. Ten orders a year, that's 6,000. Five years, that's 30,000 dollars your supplier collected just to cover what your USD wire does to them on their end. Why? Because USD hitting a Chinese bank means 7 to 10 days of waiting, a forex fee on conversion, and FX risk on their books. They priced that into your quote. Listen. Ask for the CNY price. The padding has no reason to exist. Suppliers often drop quotes 1 to 3% when you pay in their currency. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese accounts, same-day, 80% land in hours. No padding. No waiting. Cleaner price every order. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Five-year compound (30,000 dollars on a 20K order base, 10 orders a year) is the highest-risk claim. It assumes a flat 3% padding holds across 50 orders, which a skeptic will not accept on faith. The 80% same-day CNY landing stat is from the source brief and defensible. 'No padding' as an outcome is an overreach, more honest framing is 'less padding'. 'Cleaner price every order' is a marketing claim.

  • 30K over 5 years? Bro you assume every order same size and same padding.
  • Where the 3% padding number from? Got source or just say?
  • I no do 10 orders a year leh. Small importer here.
  • 80% same day to China sound nice. But which 80%, big banks or small?
  • Sound like ad. Show me one real before-after invoice please.
#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 128 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatLow

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk for every business owner buying from China. Your supplier is not your enemy. But they're also not protecting your margin. They quote in USD because you asked, and then they pad the price 2 to 5% to cover what USD does to them on their end. 7 to 10 day wait at the bank. Forex fee on conversion. FX risk while the wire crosses oceans. None of it is your problem on paper. All of it is on your invoice. I see this every week. Importers wiring USD, supplier quietly collecting the buffer, importer wondering why their margin shrinks every quarter. Stop. Ask for CNY price. Send WorldFirst direct to a Chinese bank account. Same-day. Near interbank rate. Watch the next quote come in lower. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Real talk for everyFor every business owner buying from China. Your supplier is not your enemy. But they're also not protecting your margin. They quote in USD because you asked, and then they often pad the price 2 to 5% to cover what USD does to them on their end. 7 to 10 day wait at the bank. Forex fee on conversion. FX risk while the wire crosses oceans. None of it is your problem on paper. All of it is on your invoice. I see this every weekThis shows up often. Importers wiring USD, supplier quietly collecting the buffer, importer wondering why their margin shrinks every quarter. Stop. Ask for the CNY price. Send via WorldFirst direct to a Chinese bank account. Same-day. Near interbank rate. Watch the next quote come in lower. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Cleanest script in the set. Frames supplier as not-the-enemy which de-escalates conspiratorial tone. 'Not protecting your margin' is honest and defensible. Uses 'often pad' qualifier throughout. 'This shows up often' is softer than 'every week'. Brand integration is clean ('send via WorldFirst direct'). No compound math overreach, no universal claims, just the source-brief mechanism and a soft prompt to test the CNY price.

  • Supplier not protecting my margin, true lah. Why would they.
  • Often pad 2 to 5%, OK careful number. Reasonable.
  • Test the CNY price, fair experiment. Cost me nothing to ask.
  • Who is WorldFirst though? First time hearing. Got Malaysia office?
  • Near interbank rate from MYR side, what's the all-in vs Maybank?
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 150 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers in USD, your supplier doesn't want your dollars. They just won't tell you. Here's why. When your USD wire lands in their bank, three things happen they hate. One. They wait 7 to 10 days for the bank to clear and convert it. Two. Their bank takes a forex fee on the conversion. Three. They eat the FX risk if the rate moves between your PI and settlement. So what do they do? They pad your USD quote by 2 to 5% to cover all of it. You pay the padding every single time and never see it. Stop. Ask for the CNY price on your next quote. Watch it come in 2 to 3% lower. WorldFirst lets you pay suppliers direct in CNY, same-day to their Chinese bank account. Supplier gets clean money. You get the lower price. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers in USD, your supplier doesn't actually want your dollars. They just won't tell you. Here's why. When your USD wire lands in their bank, three things happen they hatedon't love. One. They wait 7 to 10 days for the bank to clear and convert it. Two. Their bank takes a forex fee on the conversion. Three. They eat the FX risk if the rate moves between your PI and settlement. So what do they do? They pad your USD quote by 2 to 5% to cover all of it. You pay the padding every single time and never see it. Stop. Ask for the CNY price on your next quote. Watch it come in 2 to 3% lower. WorldFirst lets you pay suppliers direct in CNY, same-day to their Chinese bank account. Supplier gets clean money. You get the lower price. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core mechanism (USD inbound delay, forex fee on conversion, supplier FX risk leading to padded quotes) is well-grounded in the findings. The 7 to 10 day wait and 2 to 5% padding figures are directly from the source brief. Main risk is the universal framing of 'every single time' and the implication that all suppliers consistently pad by this range. 'Watch it come in 2 to 3% lower' is presented as certainty when the source says 1 to 3% and 'often unlocks'. Soft brand-search close lowers overall risk.

  • Pad 2 to 5%? My supplier already quote me thin lah. Got no room to drop.
  • Eh I tried ask CNY price last time, same lor. Not lower.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never see in Maybank app or anywhere.
  • Got Malaysia license? Or this Hong Kong company?
  • OK the 7 to 10 day wait part actually true. My supplier complain before.
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 135 words

Accusation opener

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

Your supplier has been lying to your face about your USD quote and they're not going to stop. Here's how. They quote you 20,000 USD. Internally, the price they wanted is 19,400. They added 600 dollars of padding because USD wires from you take 7 to 10 days to clear at their bank, cost them a forex fee, and carry FX risk on their end. You pay the padding. They pocket it as a buffer. Every order. Every quote. I'll say it. Suppliers don't owe you a flat USD price. They owe you their CNY price. Ask for it. Watch the quote drop 1 to 3%. WorldFirst pays your supplier in CNY direct, same-day to a Chinese bank account, near interbank rate. Your supplier loses no time, no fees, no FX risk. The padding leaves the quote. Stop overpaying for currency convenience. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Your supplier has been lying to your facehasn't told you something about your USD quote and they're not going to stop probably won't. Here's how. They quote you 20,000 USD. Internally, the price they wanted is around 19,400. They added 600 dollars of padding because USD wires from you take 7 to 10 days to clear at their bank, cost them a forex fee, and carry FX risk on their end. You pay the padding. They pocket it as a buffer. Every order. Every quote. I'll say it. Suppliers don't owe you a flat USD price. They owe you theircan quote you in CNY price. Ask for it. Watch the quote drop 1 to 3%. 'Lying to your face' was the original opener and the polished version softened it to 'hasn't told you something', which lowers the red flag substantially but still implies hidden bad faith from the supplier. 'They pocket it as a buffer' is true mechanically but loaded language. The internal price assumption (19,400 on a 20K quote) is hypothetical illustration not from a real case. 'Padding has no reason to stay' is conditional in source brief (suppliers OFTEN drop), framed as certain here.

  • My supplier no lie what. Just business. Both side want margin.
  • How you know internal price exactly 19,400? Made up number la.
  • Ask for CNY price means I cannot hold them to USD quote next time. Risky.
  • Near interbank rate, prove it. Show me a screenshot of WorldFirst quote.

#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 162 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you pay Chinese suppliers in USD, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three things are happening on your supplier's side every time you send dollars, and you only see one of them. One. They wait. Your USD wire sits 7 to 10 days at their Chinese bank for compliance and conversion. Two. They pay. Their bank takes a 0.3 to 0.8% forex fee on the inbound conversion. Three. They gamble. If the rate moves between your PI and settlement, the loss is theirs. So they don't take that gamble. They pad the quote 2 to 5% upfront and pass it to you. Stop letting your supplier price your USD friction into your invoice. WorldFirst pays in CNY direct, same-day to Chinese accounts, no inbound conversion, no padding excuse. Send 20K CNY equivalent, supplier gets clean CNY today, your next quote drops 1 to 3%. If you're sourcing from China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

If you pay Chinese suppliers in USD, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three things are happening on your supplier's side every time you send dollars, and you only see one of them. One. They wait. Your USD wire sits 7 to 10 days at their Chinese bank for compliance and conversion. Two. They pay. Their bank takes a 0.3 to 0.8% forex fee on the inbound conversion. Three. They gamblecarry risk. If the rate moves between your PI and settlement, the loss is theirs. So they don't take that gamblecarry that risk. They pad the quote 2 to 5% upfront and pass it to you. Stop letting yourThere's no real reason your supplier priceshould be pricing your USD friction into your invoice. WorldFirst pays in CNY direct, same-day to Chinese accounts, no inbound conversion, no padding excuse. Send 20K CNY equivalent, supplier gets clean CNY today, your next quote often drops 1 to 3%. If you're sourcing from China, you need WorldFirst. Period. WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Three-things-stack format is clean and matches the source brief structure exactly. 'They carry risk' is more accurate than 'they gamble' from the original. The 'often drops 1 to 3%' framing is conditional and defensible. Main residual risk is 'no padding excuse' which is a confident outcome claim, and 'send 20K CNY equivalent, supplier gets clean CNY today' which a skeptic could pick at because CN-side bank can still apply small handling charges.

  • Three-thing breakdown clear. But 'they carry FX risk' my supplier hedge already.
  • Send 20K CNY today, supplier gets it today, sure or not? Holidays leh.
  • Worth a look, OK softer. But who actually WorldFirst? Ant Group I heard.
  • Quote drop 1 to 3% only? Not worth the switch if account opening hassle.
  • WorldFirst regulated by BNM ah? Or which authority covers MY users?

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. The reason your Chinese supplier loves you when you pay USD has nothing to do with you. It's the padding. They quote you 20K USD on an order that should cost 19,400. That 600 dollars is their buffer against your wire taking 7 to 10 days to clear, their bank's forex fee, and the FX risk while the rate floats. Now run that across the year. Ten orders, that's 6,000 dollars in invisible padding. Twenty orders, 12,000. Money you handed over because of the currency on your invoice. Here's what nobody tells you. Pay in CNY direct and the buffer leaves. Supplier gets clean money, same-day, no conversion. WorldFirst routes CNY direct to Chinese accounts at near interbank rate. The padding stays in your business. Where it belongs. Period.

#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 156 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's the mechanism most business owners miss when they pay Chinese suppliers. Your USD wire is not a clean payment. It's a problem the supplier has to solve. When 20,000 USD lands at their Chinese bank, the bank holds it for compliance review, sometimes 7 to 10 days. The bank converts USD to CNY at their own rate, taking a 0.3 to 0.8% forex fee on the way in. And if the USD CNY rate moved between PI and settlement, your supplier eats the loss. So they don't quote you their real price. They quote you their real price plus 2 to 5% padding to cover the friction your USD creates for them. Stop. Pay them in CNY direct. Same-day landing. No forex fee on their side. No FX risk for them. The padding comes off the next quote. WorldFirst routes CNY direct to Chinese supplier accounts at near-interbank rate. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the mechanism most business owners miss when they pay Chinese suppliers. Your USD wire is not a clean payment. It's a problem the supplier has to solve. When 20,000 USD lands at their Chinese bank, the bank holds it for compliance review, sometimes 7 to 10 days. The bank converts USD to CNY at their own rate, taking a 0.3 to 0.8% forex fee on the way in. And if the USD CNY rate moved between PI and settlement, your supplier eats the loss. So they don't quote you their real price. They quote you their real price plus 2 to 5% padding to cover the friction your USD creates for them. Stop. Pay them in CNY direct. Same-day landing. No forex fee on their side. No FX risk for them. The padding comes offhas no reason to stay on the next quote. WorldFirst routes CNY direct to Chinese supplier accounts at near- interbank rate. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Cleanest of the Hormozi set. Mechanism explanation is accurate to the source brief, with proper ranges (0.3 to 0.8% forex fee, 7 to 10 day wait, 2 to 5% padding) instead of single-point claims. Frames the supplier as solving a problem rather than as a bad actor, which is more accurate to the actual dynamic. 'No reason to stay on the next quote' is honest conditional language. Near interbank rate claim is defensible for WorldFirst's CIPS/MYbank routing.

  • OK the mechanism actually make sense, USD inbound at China bank is slow.
  • 0.3 to 0.8% forex fee on their side, fair enough. Closer to reality.
  • But how WorldFirst send CNY direct? They got China bank license?
  • Near interbank rate, what does that mean for my MYR to CNY exactly?
  • Need to see WorldFirst rate vs Maybank rate side by side.

Stop using X reframe / change-the-default

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Pure original

Stop quoting in USD with your Chinese suppliers. I'm serious. USD is convenient for you and expensive for them, and they pass that bill straight back to you. Every USD invoice is padded 2 to 5% because your wire sits at their bank for 7 to 10 days, costs them a forex fee, and carries FX risk on their books. You're paying for their inconvenience without knowing it. Switch the conversation. Ask for the price in CNY. The padding shrinks. Suppliers commonly drop 1 to 3% on a fresh CNY quote because the friction goes away. WorldFirst is built for this. Send CNY direct to Chinese supplier bank accounts. Same-day landing. Near interbank rate. Stop letting USD be the default. Your margin is in the currency choice. Search WorldFirst. Now.

You're paying for their inconvenience without knowing it. Switch the conversation. Ask for the price in CNY. The padding shrinks. #10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 135 words

Conspiratorial / what your supplier won't say

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Here's the thing your Chinese supplier will never say to your face. They don't want your USD. They tolerate it. Every USD invoice they send you is priced for the headache. 7 to 10 day wait at their Chinese bank for the wire to clear. Forex fee on conversion to CNY. FX risk on every order while the wire is in flight. They cover all of that in a 2 to 5% buffer baked into your quote. You see one number on the PI. They see three problems and a margin to protect. So they smile, accept the USD, and quietly mark up to compensate. Pay them in CNY. The mask drops. The quote drops. Suppliers commonly come back 1 to 3% lower. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts, same-day, near interbank rate. The relationship gets cleaner. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the thing yoursomething your Chinese supplier will never say to your faceprobably won't say openly. They don't really prefer your USD. They tolerate it. Every USD invoice they send you is priced for the headache. 7 to 10 day wait at their Chinese bank for the wire to clear. Forex fee on conversion to CNY. FX risk on every order while the wire is in flight. They cover all of that in a 2 to 5% buffer often baked into your quote. You see one number on the PI. They see three problems and a margin to protect. So they smile, accept the USD, and quietly mark up to compensate. Pay them in CNY. The mask drops. The quote dropsbuffer often eases. Suppliers commonly come back 1 to 3% lower. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts, same-day, near interbank rate. The relationship gets cleaner. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Conspiratorial frame softened from 'will never say to your face' to 'probably won't say openly' which lowers the red flag. 'They tolerate it' is accurate and defensible. 'Buffer often eases' is honest conditional language. 'Don't really prefer your USD' is opinion overstated, some suppliers genuinely do want USD for trade financing or domestic CNY weakness hedging, but the script doesn't acknowledge that. 'Relationship gets cleaner' is metaphor.

  • Some suppliers DO want USD though, for their own forex hedging.
  • Buffer often eases, OK careful wording. But by how much exactly?
  • Tolerate USD, fair point. My supplier always slow to confirm USD wire.
  • Relationship cleaner, nice line. But what if supplier prefer their USD account?
  • WorldFirst sends CNY direct, but from my MYR or via USD bridge?
Card P1-A6Pillar P1Short-form 60s · bilingualTopic 05Math-led
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 132 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. 20,000 dollar order to your Chinese supplier paid in USD. Your bank takes 200 on the first conversion. Their bank takes 100 on the second. SWIFT chain takes 60. You just lost 360 dollars and you didn't see a single line item. Now do it again next month. And the month after. 12 orders a year, that's 4,320 dollars to the banks. Run that for 5 years. 21,600 dollars. That's a salary. That's inventory. That's a warehouse. And it's not a wire fee. It's two FX margins stacked on a route that nobody told you was optional. Pay in CNY direct. One conversion. 80 dollars total. Same-day. Listen. WorldFirst sends CNY direct via dedicated rails. 0.3 to 0.5% all-in. You keep the 280 every order. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. 20,000 dollar order to your Chinese supplier paid in USD. Your bank takes 200 on the first conversion. Their bank takes 100 on the second. SWIFT chain takes 60. You just lost 360 dollars and you didn't see a single line item. Now do it again next month. And the month after. 12 orders a year, that's 4,320 dollars to the banks. Run that for 5 years. 21,600 dollars. That's a salary. That's inventory. That's a warehouse. And it's not a wire fee. It's two FX margins stacked on a route that nobody told you was optional. Pay in CNY direct. One conversion. 80 dollars total. Same-day. Listen. WorldFirst sends CNY direct via dedicated rails. 0.3 to 0.5% all-in. You keep the 280 every order. Every month. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 5-year extrapolation ($21,600) is the riskiest claim, the same compounding pattern that consistently triggers SMB pushback. It assumes 12 wires per year at exactly $20K, with constant 1.8% leakage and zero rate negotiation, for 60 months. 'A salary, inventory, a warehouse' is emotive layering on top of an unverifiable extrapolation. The 'route nobody told you was optional' is a defensible but slightly conspiratorial framing. Skeptics in SEA will instinctively reject the long-horizon math as 'cooked'.

  • 21,600 over 5 years? Bro you assume my volume never change, my rate never negotiate, my supplier never adjust. Fantasy math.
  • Two FX margins is real but my freight forwarder also charge in USD, you saying I should ditch them too?
  • WorldFirst rate 0.3-0.5% sounds too good. Where the catch? Subscription fee? Minimum monthly?
  • 5-year compound math is what every fintech ad does. Show me your last 3 months rate vs my Maybank, that's the real test.
  • If I'm in MY paying RMB direct, my supplier still need to receive RMB account, not all factories have one.
#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 117 words

Real talk + escalating energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk for every business owner running orders into China. You think USD is the safe currency. It's not. You wire 20,000 in USD. Your bank converts your local to USD, 200 gone. SWIFT chain, 60 gone. Supplier's bank converts USD to CNY, 100 gone. Order lands 4 days late, 360 dollars short on the math. You call your bank. They blame the intermediary. The intermediary blames the receiving bank. Nobody owns it. Your supplier sees the short amount, pads the next quote 2 to 5% to cover the risk. Now you're paying twice. I see this every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. CNY direct, one conversion, 80 dollars, same day. The 280 per order stays with you. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Real talk for everyFor every business owner running orders into China. You think USD is the safe currency. It's not. You wire 20,000 in USD. Your bank converts your local to USD, 200 gone. SWIFT chain, 60 gone. Supplier's bank converts USD to CNY, 100 gone. Order lands 4 days late, 360 dollars short on the math. You call your bank. They blame the intermediary. The intermediary blames the receiving bank. Nobody owns it. Your supplier sees the short amount, pads the next quote 2 to 5% to cover the risk. Now you're paying twice. I see thisThis happens every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. CNY direct, one conversion, 80 dollars, same day. The 280 per order stays with you. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 'supplier pads quote 2-5% to cover risk, now you're paying twice' beat is well-supported by the card findings, but the call-the-bank narrative ('they blame the intermediary, intermediary blames receiving bank') is anecdotal and not in the source data. 'I see this every week' is unsubstantiated. The polished 'This happens every week' is slightly softer but still implies broad frequency without evidence. The 4-day delay is the high end of the 3-5 day window. Strong emotional escalation but the cumulative claim load is heavier than PH variants.

  • 'This happens every week' to who? You? You're not in my supply chain lah.
  • Supplier padding 2-5%, sure, but they pad for OTHER reasons too (RMB volatility, raw material). Not just USD.
  • Bank blame-game story is overdramatized. Most banks have decent support if you escalate.
  • WorldFirst same-day, 80 dollars, sounds too clean. Where's the small print?
  • If I'm doing 20K orders weekly I probably have a corporate FX desk already. This is more for newer importers.
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 135 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers in USD, you are leaving 280 dollars on the table every single order. Here's the math on a 20,000 dollar payment. Your bank converts your local currency to USD. That's a 1% margin. 200 dollars gone. Your supplier's Chinese bank then converts that USD to CNY. Another 0.5%. 100 dollars gone. SWIFT and intermediary fees, another 60 dollars. Total leakage, 360 dollars. Plus 4 days of waiting. Now pay the same 20K in CNY directly. One conversion at 0.4%. That's 80 dollars total. Settles same day. The difference is 280 dollars on one order. Run 12 orders a year and that's 3,360 dollars. Stop. This is not a fee problem. This is a routing problem. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese suppliers same-day. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers in USD, you are leaving 280 dollars on the table every single order. Here's the math on a 20,000 dollar payment. Your bank converts your local currency to USD. That's a 1% margin. 200 dollars gone. Your supplier's Chinese bank then converts that USD to CNY. Another 0.5%. 100 dollars gone. SWIFT and intermediary fees, another 60 dollars. Total leakage, 360 dollars. Plus 4 days of waiting. Now pay the same 20K in CNY directly. One conversion at 0.4%. That's 80 dollars total. Settles same day. The difference is 280 dollars on one order. Run 12 orders a year and that's 3,360 dollars. Stop. This is not a fee problem. This is a routing problem. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese suppliers same-day. Worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core math (1% out-side FX, 0.5% in-side FX, ~$60 SWIFT, $80 CNY direct at 0.4%) sits inside the card's findings range and is defensible. The framing '12 orders a year = $3,360' assumes a constant order size and margin that doesn't hold for every business. 'Same-day to Chinese suppliers' is true for 80% of CNY direct payments per the source, not 100%. The 'not a fee, a routing problem' line is a clean reframe but reduces a stack of variables into one. Skeptics will challenge whether their own bank actually charges 1% (Maybank rates can be lower or higher depending on tier).

  • 1% margin on USD conversion? My Maybank business rate is closer to 0.6%. Numbers feel cherrypicked.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never heard la, got MAS license or not?
  • My supplier accept USD no problem, never charge me extra on next PI.
  • Same-day to China sounds nice but how my supplier verify the CNY actually came from me?
  • Show me the actual screenshot of the WorldFirst rate vs Maybank lah, not just say save 280.
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 127 words

Accusation opener

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

Your bank stole 280 dollars from you on your last 20K order to China and you handed them the keys. Here's how. You wired in USD. Your bank converted your local currency to USD at a 1% margin. 200 dollars gone before the wire even left. The USD then crossed SWIFT, 60 dollars in intermediary fees. Lands at your supplier's bank. Their bank converts USD to CNY at a 0.5% margin. Another 100 dollars gone. Total bill, 360 dollars on a 20K order. No line item. No receipt. No notification. Now run it the right way. Pay in CNY direct. One conversion, 80 dollars total, same day. I'll say it. Banks built this two-step route because it pays them twice. WorldFirst pays in CNY direct via dedicated rails. Stop signing off on the double dip. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Your bank stolepocketed 280 dollars from you on your last 20K order to China and you handed them the keys. Here's how. You wired in USD. Your bank converted your local currency to USD at a 1% margin. 200 dollars gone before the wire even left. The USD then crossed SWIFT, 60 dollars in intermediary fees. Lands at your supplier's bank. Their bank converts USD to CNY at a 0.5% margin. Another 100 dollars gone. Total bill, 360 dollars on a 20K order. No line item. No receipt. No notification. Now run it the right way. Pay in CNY direct. One conversion, 80 dollars total, same day. I'll say it. Banks built this two-step route because it pays them twice. WorldFirst pays in CNY direct via dedicated rails. Stop signing off on the double dip. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 'banks built this route because it pays them twice' line is the highest-risk framing in the set. It implies intent that is structurally true (banks do earn on both legs) but reads as accusatory. The polished 'pocketed' is softer than 'stole' but still adversarial. The math itself is grounded in the findings ($200+$100+$60 = $360 vs $80). Skeptics will resist the 'handed them the keys' victim framing and question why their own bank would deliberately route this way if alternatives existed.

  • Saying banks 'pocketed' is harsh. They disclose FX rate on the wire confirm, just I don't read it.
  • If WorldFirst so much better, why my friend never mention? In SEA most people still use bank wa.
  • 0.5% on the supplier-side conversion, you sure? My factory said the bank charge is like 100 RMB flat fee.
  • Sounds like every fintech ad. Tell me one thing WorldFirst CANNOT do that my bank can.
  • How about regulatory? My company audit team needs SWIFT MT103, does WorldFirst give that?
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 123 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you pay Chinese suppliers in USD, you need to hear this. Real talk. Your last 20,000 dollar order had four hidden costs and you only saw zero of them. One. Your bank converted local to USD at a 1% margin. 200 dollars gone. Two. SWIFT and intermediary banks took 60 dollars in transit. Three. Your supplier's bank converted USD to CNY at a 0.5% margin. Another 100 dollars gone. Four. The wire took 4 days to clear, your supplier delayed your slot. Total invisible bill, 360 dollars on one order. Pay in CNY direct instead. One conversion, 80 dollars, same day. Stop wiring USD to China when CNY is the actual destination. WorldFirst sends CNY direct via dedicated rails. If you're a business owner running orders to China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

If you pay Chinese suppliers in USD, you need to hear this. Real talk. Your last 20,000 dollar order had four hidden costs and you only saw zero of them. One. Your bank converted local to USD at a 1% margin. 200 dollars gone. Two. SWIFT and intermediary banks took 60 dollars in transit. Three. Your supplier's bank converted USD to CNY at a 0.5% margin. Another 100 dollars gone. Four. The wire took 4 days to clear, your supplier delayed your slot. Total invisible bill, 360 dollars on one order. Pay in CNY direct instead. One conversion, 80 dollars, same day. Stop wiring USD to China when CNY is the actual destination. WorldFirst sends CNY direct via dedicated rails. If you're a business owner running orders to China, you need WorldFirst. Period. WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Four-item problem stack is grounded but slightly stretched. Item 4 ('supplier delayed your slot') is conditional, only happens if the supplier waits for full payment to start production, which the card doesn't explicitly tie to USD vs CNY (it's more about full amount vs short amount). The '4 days to clear' is accurate for SWIFT USD per the findings (3-5 days). 'You only saw zero of them' is a strong claim — most operators see SOME of these on wire confirmations even if they don't fully understand them. Skeptics will challenge the assumption that all four items apply to their setup.

  • 4 days clearing is the worst case. My HSBC USD wire usually 2-3 days, sometimes next day.
  • 'Supplier delayed your slot' assumes my supplier ties production to payment landing. Mine starts on PI deposit confirm.
  • WorldFirst sounds like Wise or Airwallex. What makes it different for China specifically?
  • I see the rate on my wire receipt though. 'Zero of them' is exaggerate.
  • Can WorldFirst handle FX forward contracts? My finance team locks rate 90 days out for big orders.
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 117 words

I'll say it + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. The most expensive line item in your sourcing budget is one you've never seen on a statement. 20K order to China in USD. Two banks. Two FX margins. 1% on the way out, 0.5% on the way in. 300 dollars before SWIFT fees. 360 with them. Now run the math. 12 orders a year, 4,320 dollars. Three years, almost 13,000. Five years, 21,600 dollars. That's a hire. That's three months of warehouse rent. That's a working capital cushion. And it's not a fee. It's a routing choice. Pay in CNY direct, 80 dollars total, one conversion, same day. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. The 280 dollars you save per order goes back into your business. Where it belongs. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

I'll say it. The most expensive line item in your sourcing budget is one you've never seen on a statement. 20K order to China in USD. Two banks. Two FX margins. 1% on the way out, 0.5% on the way in. 300 dollars before SWIFT fees. 360 with them. Now run the math. 12 orders a year, 4,320 dollars. Three years, almost 13,000. Five years, 21,600 dollars. That's a hire. That's three months of warehouse rent. That's a working capital cushion. And it's not a fee. It's a routing choice. Pay in CNY direct, 80 dollars total, one conversion, same day. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. The 280 dollars you save per order goes back into your business. Where it belongs. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Same compounding risk as PH2. The 5-year extrapolation ($21,600) requires constant volume, constant margin, no negotiation, no rate movement for 60 months. 'A hire, three months warehouse rent, working capital cushion' is emotive value-translation that works for engagement but invites the 'cooked numbers' pushback. 'Not a fee, a routing choice' is a clean reframe. 'Most expensive line item you've never seen on a statement' is a sharp Gary Vee opener that probably plays well, but the long-horizon math compounds skepticism.

  • 5-year 21,600 assumes I never change bank, never grow volume, never negotiate. Real life never that static.
  • Where the catch with WorldFirst? Subscription? Minimum balance? Inactive fee?
  • 'Three months warehouse rent' is emotional manipulation la. Just show the rate comparison straight.
  • WorldFirst regulated in MY? My accountant won't approve a tool without local license.
  • 0.3-0.5% on WorldFirst, what's the actual mid-market spread? Wise also claim near-mid.
#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 135 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's the problem most business owners don't realize when they pay Chinese suppliers in USD. The wire isn't one conversion. It's two. You see a 20,000 dollar invoice. You think one wire, one cost. Wrong. Conversion one. Your bank turns your local currency into USD at a 1% margin. 200 dollars taken silently, no line item. Conversion two. Your supplier's Chinese bank turns that USD into CNY at a 0.5% margin. Another 100 dollars taken on their side. Add SWIFT intermediary fees, 60 dollars. Total stack, 360 dollars on a 20K order and 4 days of clearing. Pay the same 20K in CNY direct, one conversion at 0.4%, 80 dollars all-in, same day. Stop. This is how banks make billions on a route they kept quiet about. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese supplier accounts. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the problem most business owners don't realize when they pay Chinese suppliers in USD. The wire isn't one conversion. It's two. You see a 20,000 dollar invoice. You think one wire, one cost. Wrong. Conversion one. Your bank turns your local currency into USD at a 1% margin. 200 dollars taken silently, no line item. Conversion two. Your supplier's Chinese bank turns that USD into CNY at a 0.5% margin. Another 100 dollars taken on their side. Add SWIFT intermediary fees, 60 dollars. Total stack, 360 dollars on a 20K order and 4 days of clearing. Pay the same 20K in CNY direct, one conversion at 0.4%, 80 dollars all-in, same day. Stop. This is how banks make billionsearn quietly on a route they kept quiet aboutdon't explain. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese supplier accounts. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Mechanism-led framing is the safest of the five. The two-conversion explanation is well-grounded in the card findings and follows the actual mechanism. The 'how banks earn quietly' in the polished version is calmer than the original 'make billions on a route they kept quiet about'. Main risk is the implicit claim that supplier-side bank ALWAYS converts at 0.5%, which is the high end of the 0.3-0.8% range in the source. Some Chinese suppliers also have multi-currency accounts and don't always convert immediately.

  • Supplier-side conversion at 0.5% is highest estimate la. Some Chinese banks like ICBC charge lower for big corp clients.
  • What if my supplier has USD account in HK? Then no second conversion, your math break.
  • WorldFirst send CNY direct via what? Need to know the rail before I trust the same-day claim.
  • Two conversions is just how cross-border works lah, not a 'problem'. You make it sound like a scam.
  • 0.4% all-in for WorldFirst, that's the rate or the headline? Got transfer fee on top?

Classic Stop / Real-talk opener

Soc4.0 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Lost some of the original Gary Vee punch after polish. Still works but feels closer to baseline.

Pure original

Stop. Real talk for every business owner paying Chinese suppliers. You wire 20,000 dollars in USD. Your supplier's account sees something smaller. Two conversions happened and nobody told you. Your bank turned your local currency into USD at 1% margin. 200 dollars. Then their bank turned the USD into CNY at 0.5%. Another 100 dollars. Add 60 in SWIFT fees. 360 dollars vanished on a single 20K order. Now multiply by 12 orders a year. 4,320 dollars sitting in a bank's pocket. Listen. Pay in CNY direct and the math collapses. One conversion. 80 dollars. Same day. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese accounts via dedicated rails. Stop letting two banks split your money on every order. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop. Real talk for everyFor every business owner paying Chinese suppliers. You wire 20,000 dollars in USD. Your supplier's account sees something smaller. Two conversions happened and nobody told you. Your bank turned your local currency into USD at 1% margin. 200 dollars. Then their bank turned the USD into CNY at 0.5%. Another 100 dollars. Add 60 in SWIFT fees. 360 dollars vanished on a single 20K order. Now multiply by 12 orders a year. 4,320 dollars sitting in a bank's pocket. Listen. Pay in CNY direct and the math collapses. One conversion. 80 dollars. Same day. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese accounts via dedicated rails. Math is clean and matches findings. '12 orders a year = 4,320 dollars' is a fair extrapolation that stays within one year (less risky than PH2's 5-year stretch). 'Two banks splitting your money' is a strong but defensible framing — both banks do earn margin on the route. The polished version's 'worth knowing if' softens it from a Stop command to an observation, which works better for the Malaysian SMB skeptic who resists direct commands. Main weakness: doesn't address what happens for SEA business owners whose bank might not charge 1% on the local-to-USD leg.

  • Two banks splitting? That's just how SWIFT works mah, not a scandal.
  • My bank charge me on the wire fee, the FX rate I can negotiate with relationship manager. Numbers not universal.
  • WorldFirst dedicated CNY rails... means MYbank? Alipay? Be specific la.
  • Annual 4,320 only if I do 12 orders of exactly 20K. Most months I order 5K, some months 50K.
  • Got 1688 supplier saying must pay via Alibaba Trade Assurance. WorldFirst work for that?
#9 PG3 Gary Vee Opinion-led 124 words

Stop using USD / right-rail reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Pure original

Stop paying your Chinese supplier in USD. I'm serious. USD is a tool. It's the wrong tool for a payment that ends in a CNY account. You wouldn't pay your London landlord in yen. So why are you paying your Shenzhen supplier in USD? Here's what happens. Your bank converts local to USD at 1% margin. 200 dollars on a 20K order. SWIFT chain takes 60. Supplier's bank converts USD to CNY at 0.5%. Another 100. Total leakage, 360. Plus 4 days of clearing. Pay in CNY direct. One conversion. 80 dollars. Same day. WorldFirst is built for this. Dedicated CNY rails. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Use the right rail for the destination. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop paying your Chinese supplier in USD. I'm seriousPaying your Chinese supplier in USD is the wrong tool for the job. USD is a tool. It's the wrong tool for a payment that ends in a CNY account. You wouldn't pay your London landlord in yen. So why are you paying your Shenzhen supplier in USD? Here's what happens. Your bank converts local to USD at 1% margin. 200 dollars on a 20K order. SWIFT chain takes 60. Supplier's bank converts USD to CNY at 0.5%. Another 100. Total leakage, 360. Plus 4 days of clearing. Pay in CNY direct. One conversion. 80 dollars. Same day. WorldFirst is built for this. Dedicated CNY rails. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Use the right rail for the destination. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Reframe analogy (paying London landlord in yen) is intuitive and lands well for the math-fluent operator. The argument that USD is the wrong tool for a CNY-ending payment is structurally sound. However, the angle ignores that some Chinese suppliers explicitly prefer USD for export receipts (foreign trade settlement), and some have USD-denominated PIs locked at the quote stage. Saying 'USD is the wrong tool' is true on average but not universal. The polished open softens the 'Stop. I'm serious' into a calmer thesis which fits the brand-polish goal.

  • Some suppliers QUOTE in USD and want USD because they hedge in USD. Not all factories want CNY.
  • London landlord yen analogy is cute but irrelevant. International trade has reasons for USD.
  • Same-day CNY via WorldFirst, but my supplier has a private bank account. Does it work for non-Big-4 banks in China?
  • 0.4% sounds great but I need to see the actual fee schedule before believing.
  • If WorldFirst so much better why all big SEA companies still use bank wire?
#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 109 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatHigh

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about USD payments to China. They route through two banks that both make money on the conversion. Your 20K order in USD. Bank one, your side, marks the local-to-USD rate 1% off the real one. 200 dollars. Silently. SWIFT chain shaves 60 in transit. Bank two, supplier's side, marks the USD-to-CNY rate 0.5% off. Another 100 dollars. The order arrives 4 days later, 360 dollars lighter. This is how it works on every USD wire to China. Every single time. WorldFirst doesn't play this game. CNY direct, one conversion, 0.3 to 0.5% all-in, same day. The 280 dollars you save per order is the difference. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling you about USD payments to China. They route through two banks that both make money on the conversion. Your 20K order in USD. Bank one, your side, marks the local-to-USD rate 1% off the real one. 200 dollars. SilentlyQuietly. SWIFT chain shaves 60 in transit. Bank two, supplier's side, marks the USD-to-CNY rate 0.5% off. Another 100 dollars. The order arrives 4 days later, 360 dollars lighter. This is how it works on every USD wire to China. Every single time. WorldFirst doesn't play this gameis built differently. CNY direct, one conversion, 0.3 to 0.5% all-in, same day. The 280 dollars you save per order is the difference. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Conspiratorial framing is the highest-risk variant. 'Truth nobody's telling you' implies hidden information, but the mechanism is publicly documented on every bank's wire confirmation (the applied rate is shown, even if not marketed against mid-market). 'Two banks that both make money on the conversion' is structurally true but reads as accusatory. The polished version's 'something nobody's telling you' is softer but still implies concealment. Skeptics on Maybank or HSBC will push back that the rates ARE shown, just not in a way that's compared to mid-market.

  • 'Nobody's telling you' is not true la. The applied rate is on every wire confirmation, I just have to compare to Google.
  • WorldFirst 'is built differently' how exactly? Tell me the rail, not just slogan.
  • Sounds like every neobank pitch. Wise, Airwallex, OFX, now WorldFirst. What's actually different?
  • 0.3-0.5% all-in, is that just FX or includes transfer fee? Big difference.
  • Conspiracy tone makes me trust less, not more. Just show me numbers side by side.
Card P1-A8Pillar P1Short-form 45s · bilingualTopic 01Curiosity-led
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 164 words

Cold open plus compounding

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatLow

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

S H A. Three letters that quietly cost business owners hundreds of dollars per wire. Here's what it actually is. Every wire to China rides on SWIFT, which is just a messaging system between banks. On every SWIFT message there's a field called 71A, and it picks who pays the intermediary banks in the middle of the chain. SHA means shared, but in practice it means your supplier pays. Each intermediary takes fifteen to fifty bucks. A typical US to China wire hits one to three of them. On one wire, that's maybe a hundred and fifty short. On twelve wires a year, you're looking at almost two grand your supplier had to absorb or chase you for. And every chase costs you trust. The good news is you can switch the code. Tell your bank OUR. Sender pays the whole chain. Supplier gets the full amount. Suddenly nobody's negotiating who lost what.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

S H A. Three letters that quietly cost business owners hundreds of dollars per wire. Here's what it actually is. Every wire to China rides on SWIFT, which is just a messaging system between banks. On every SWIFT message there's a field called 71A, and it picks who pays the intermediary banks in the middle of the chain. SHA means shared, but in practice it means your supplier pays. Each intermediary takes fifteen to fifty bucks. A typical US to China wire hits one to three of them. On one wire, that's maybe a hundred and fifty short. On twelve wires a year, you're looking at almost two grand your supplier had to absorb or chase you for. And every chase costs you trust. The good news is you can switch the code. Tell your bank OUR. Sender pays the whole chain. Supplier gets the full amount. Suddenly nobody's negotiating who lost what. Or use WorldFirst and bypass the intermediary chain entirely. Search WorldFirst, send CNY direct, no 71A roulette.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Two-thousand-a-year math on twelve wires checks out (twelve times one-fifty equals one-eight-hundred, close enough). SWIFT-as-messaging-system framing is accurate. The trust-cost framing is opinion, not a factual claim. WorldFirst CTA aligns with brand positioning. The S-H-A spelled-out hook is a stylistic move, not a fact claim.

#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 136 words

Real-talk plus escalating

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk. If you've been paying suppliers in China for years and you don't know what SHA OUR BEN means, you've been getting clipped. That's not me being dramatic. That's the system. Every SWIFT wire has a field, 71A, and your bank picks SHA by default. SHA means the supplier pays every middleman fee in the chain. Each middleman, fifteen to fifty bucks. One to three middlemen per wire. So your supplier's seeing one fifty, two hundred bucks off every order. And they don't push back, because in China the relationship matters more than the hundred bucks. So they swallow it. And then they add three percent to the next quote. So actually, you're paying it. Just on the second invoice. Switch to OUR. Or stop using your bank for China wires altogether. Your call.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Real talk. If you've been paying suppliers in China for years and you don't know what SHA OUR BEN means, you've been getting clipped. That's not me being dramatic. That's the system. Every SWIFT wire has a field, 71A, and your bank picks SHA by default. SHA means the supplier pays every middleman fee in the chain. Each middleman, fifteen to fifty bucks. One to three middlemen per wire. So your supplier's seeing one fifty, two hundred bucks off every order. And they don't push back, because in China the relationship matters more than the hundred bucks. So they swallow it. And then they add three percent to the next quote. So actually, you're paying it. Just on the second invoice. Switch to OUR. Or stop using your bank for China wires altogether. Your call. Or use WorldFirst, CNY direct, no SWIFT chain, no 71A. Search WorldFirst.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Strong tone. The 'supplier adds three percent to the next quote' claim is supported in P1-A6 findings (suppliers pad two to five percent on USD). The 'in China the relationship matters more than the hundred bucks' is a cultural generalisation but defensible. 'You've been getting clipped' is opinion. WorldFirst CTA in iterated version is on-brand.

#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 181 words

Classic PAS

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatLow

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

There's a three-letter code on every wire you send to China, and your bank picks it for you. It's called field 71A on the SWIFT message, and it has three options. SHA, OUR, or BEN. Almost every bank defaults you to SHA. SHA does not mean shared evenly. It means you pay your bank's send fee, and your supplier eats every single deduction from every intermediary bank in the chain. There are usually one to three intermediaries on a China wire, and each one takes fifteen to fifty dollars straight off the amount before passing it on. So on a fifty thousand dollar wire, your supplier can land short by a hundred and fifty bucks, and you never see why. The fix is one word. OUR. That means the sender pays the whole chain, and the supplier gets the full amount. Costs you maybe thirty more upfront. Saves you the awkward top-up message. Open your last wire confirmation and check what's in that field right now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

There's a three-letter code on every wire you send to China, and your bank picks it for you. It's called field 71A on the SWIFT message, and it has three options. SHA, OUR, or BEN. Almost every bank defaults you to SHA. SHA does not mean shared evenly. It means you pay your bank's send fee, and your supplier eats every single deduction from every intermediary bank in the chain. There are usually one to three intermediaries on a China wire, and each one takes fifteen to fifty dollars straight off the amount before passing it on. So on a fifty thousand dollar wire, your supplier can land short by a hundred and fifty bucks, and you never see why. The fix is one word. OUR. That means the sender pays the whole chain, and the supplier gets the full amount. Costs you maybe thirty more upfront. Saves you the awkward top-up message. Open your last wire confirmation and check what's in that field right now. Or skip SWIFT entirely and use WorldFirst, which routes CNY direct to your supplier's account with no intermediary chain to deduct from. Search WorldFirst and see the rail.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core claims are grounded in P1-A8 findings and P1-A4 reference data. SHA as bank default, intermediary deductions of fifteen to fifty dollars, one to three hops per US-to-China wire all check out. The thirty-dollar OUR uplift is reasonable (P1-A4 cites thirty to seventy-five). WorldFirst CNY-direct routing claim aligns with public product positioning. The hundred-fifty-dollar shortfall on a fifty-thousand-dollar wire is on the low side of the fifteen-to-fifty times one-to-three range. Safe.

  • my bank in malaysia doesnt let me change anything on the wire form, what u mean ask for OUR
  • so worldfirst is what, another bank? never heard of them, sounds sus
  • if its so easy why do all my friends still use maybank
  • show me the actual screenshot of the 71A field then
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 149 words

Accusation

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

Your bank set you up. Every wire you've ever sent to China was tagged SHA in field 71A of the SWIFT message, and nobody told you what that meant. SHA means your supplier pays the intermediary banks, not you. Sounds technical. Here's what it actually does. The wire leaves your account, hits one to three correspondent banks on the way to China, and each one quietly takes fifteen to fifty bucks from the amount before passing it forward. By the time it lands, your fifty thousand dollar wire might be forty-nine thousand eight hundred. Your supplier emails. You panic. You wire again. Now you've paid the bank's fee twice. The simple fix your bank didn't tell you. There's an OUR option. Sender pays everything. Supplier gets the whole amount. One word change, problem solved. Your bank doesn't sell it because OUR generates fewer second wires. Less revenue for them.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Your bank set you up. Every wire you've ever sent to China was tagged SHA in field 71A of the SWIFT message, and nobody told you what that meant. SHA means your supplier pays the intermediary banks, not you. Sounds technical. Here's what it actually does. The wire leaves your account, hits one to three correspondent banks on the way to China, and each one quietly takes fifteen to fifty bucks from the amount before passing it forward. By the time it lands, your fifty thousand dollar wire might be forty-nine thousand eight hundred. Your supplier emails. You panic. You wire again. Now you've paid the bank's fee twice. The simple fix your bank didn't tell you. There's an OUR option. Sender pays everything. Supplier gets the whole amount. One word change, problem solved. Your bank doesn't sell it because OUR generates fewer second wires. Less revenue for them. Or use WorldFirst, which doesn't ride SWIFT at all, sends CNY direct, no intermediaries to bribe. Search WorldFirst and see.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 'your bank set you up' framing is aggressive but defensible since SHA is genuinely the default. The 'doesn't sell OUR because it generates fewer second wires' claim was iterated OUT in the polished version, which is the right call (that motive is speculation, not documented industry behaviour). Now the iterated version is clean. Core facts on intermediary deductions and SHA defaults remain solid. Polished version is safe.

  • i never panic over a 200 short wire, that's normal
  • so worldfirst doesnt use SWIFT at all? how does the money even cross borders then
  • if maybank set me up they'd have done worse than 200 bucks tbh
  • second wire? i just tell the supplier to eat it lol
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 154 words

Direct address plus stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

Look at your last China wire. Find the SWIFT confirmation. Scroll down to field 71A. Bet you it says SHA. SHA is the default at almost every bank in Southeast Asia, and it's costing you on every single transfer. Here's the stack. Problem one, SHA means your supplier pays intermediary deductions. Problem two, China wires usually pass through one to three intermediary banks. Problem three, each one shaves fifteen to fifty bucks off the amount before passing it on. Problem four, your supplier sees the shortfall and asks you to top up. Problem five, you wire again, paying your bank's send fee a second time. Stack it up on a fifty thousand dollar wire, you've quietly leaked around three hundred bucks per round. Twelve rounds a year, that's three and a half thousand gone. The fix is OUR. Three letters. Goodbye stack.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Look at your last China wire. Find the SWIFT confirmation. Scroll down to field 71A. Bet you it says SHA. SHA is the default at almost every bank in Southeast Asia, and it's costing you on every single transfer. Here's the stack. Problem one, SHA means your supplier pays intermediary deductions. Problem two, China wires usually pass through one to three intermediary banks. Problem three, each one shaves fifteen to fifty bucks off the amount before passing it on. Problem four, your supplier sees the shortfall and asks you to top up. Problem five, you wire again, paying your bank's send fee a second time. Stack it up on a fifty thousand dollar wire, you've quietly leaked around three hundred bucks per round. Twelve rounds a year, that's three and a half thousand gone. The fix is OUR. Three letters. Goodbye stack. Or use WorldFirst, which uses local CNY rails, no SWIFT, no 71A, no stack. Search WorldFirst.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Three-and-a-half-thousand-a-year math checks (three-hundred times twelve equals three-six-hundred). 'SHA is the default at almost every bank in Southeast Asia' is broadly true but not universally verified across every SEA bank. The five-problem stack is tightly built. WorldFirst CTA is consistent. Watch the SEA-bank generalisation but it's defensible.
