Your bank checks SHA by default, every time, and SHA means your supplier eats the fees from every middleman bank between you and them. There are usually one to three middlemen. Each one takes fifteen to fifty bucks. Your supplier loses. You don't know. They ask for the missing amount, you wire again, pay another fee, and you think wire fees are just like that. They're not. They're a choice. The bank made the choice for you. Take the choice back.

#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 140 words

Mechanism-first

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's the mechanism nobody explains. When you send a wire to China, your bank generates a SWIFT message called MT103. Inside that message, there's a field labeled 71A. It accepts three values. SHA, OUR, or BEN. SHA tells the chain you'll pay your sending bank's fee, and the supplier covers every other deduction along the route. OUR tells the chain you pay everything top to bottom. BEN tells the chain the supplier pays the entire bill, including your sending fee. Your bank's system defaults to SHA. That's not a coincidence. SHA generates more frequent top-up requests, which generate more wires, which generate more fees. The mechanism is hidden. The choice is yours. Walk to your bank's wire desk, ask for 71A to be OUR on the next China wire, and watch the shortfall problem disappear. One field. Three letters. Hundreds saved per wire.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the mechanism nobody explains. When you send a wire to China, your bank generates a SWIFT message called MT103. Inside that message, there's a field labeled 71A. It accepts three values. SHA, OUR, or BEN. SHA tells the chain you'll pay your sending bank's fee, and the supplier covers every other deduction along the route. OUR tells the chain you pay everything top to bottom. BEN tells the chain the supplier pays the entire bill, including your sending fee. Your bank's system defaults to SHA. That's not a coincidence. SHA generates more frequent top-up requests, which generate more wires, which generate more fees. The mechanism is hidden. The choice is yours. Walk to your bank's wire desk, ask Ask your bank for 71A to be OUR on the next China wire, and watch the shortfall problem disappear. One field. Three letters. Hundreds saved per wire.. Or skip the whole MT103 game with WorldFirst, which routes CNY direct via local rails with no intermediary chain at all. Search WorldFirst and look at the wire path.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Most technically accurate of the five Hormozi variants. MT103 is the correct SWIFT message type for cross-border payments. SHA/OUR/BEN definitions are textbook. The iterated version smartly cut the 'SHA generates more wires therefore profits banks' speculation. WorldFirst direct-CNY-rail framing is accurate. Mechanism-first tone may feel dry for TikTok but content is tight.

Conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Want to know what your bank doesn't want you to ask? Try this. Walk in next week and say, I want to send my next China wire as OUR not SHA. Watch the teller's face. Half of them won't know what you're talking about. The other half will tell you it costs more, but won't say how much. Here's the truth. SHA is the default because it generates short wires. Your supplier complains. You wire again. Bank collects another fee. OUR breaks the loop. You pay the chain upfront, maybe thirty more bucks, and your supplier gets the full amount. One wire. No follow-up. So why is OUR not the default? Think about who profits when wires go short.

#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 135 words

Cold open + compounding stakes

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. Your bank app shows sent. Your supplier's WhatsApp shows nothing. You haven't lost the money. You've lost the day. Then the week. Then the order. Because your supplier won't load raw materials until the CNY hits their account. So every hour the wire sits at an intermediary bank, your delivery date slips. Three intermediaries, two days of conversion windows, one AML hold above a threshold nobody told you about, plus a Friday public holiday in Shenzhen. That's a five-day silence. On a Q4 order, five days is your container. On a launch SKU, five days is your competitor beating you to the shelf. Listen. The wire isn't lost. The visibility is. WorldFirst removes the correspondent chain entirely. CNY straight to Chinese bank accounts, same-day, with a tracking reference your supplier can match. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. Your bank app shows sent. Your supplier's WhatsApp shows nothing. You haven't lost the money. You've lost the day. Then the week. Then the order. Because your supplier won't load raw materials until the CNY hits their account. So every hour the wire sits at an intermediary bank, your delivery date slips. Three intermediaries, two days of conversion windows, one AML hold above a threshold nobody told you about, plus a Friday public holiday in Shenzhen. That's a five-day silence. On a Q4 order, five days is your container. On a launch SKU, five days is your competitor beating you to the shelf. Listen. The wire isn't lost. The visibility is. WorldFirst removes the correspondent chain entirely. CNY straight to Chinese bank accounts, same-day, with a tracking reference your supplier can match. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The cumulative five-day silence (three intermediaries + two-day conversion + AML hold + Friday holiday in Shenzhen) is a worst-case stack presented as routine, which a skeptic will challenge. 'Five days is your container' is dramatic and not universally true. The Q4/launch SKU framing is emotive on top of an extrapolated worst case. The 'tracking reference your supplier can match' is a real WF capability but framing makes it sound unique to WF when SWIFT-based tracking exists across rails, just less visible.

  • Five day silence stacking everything bad together? In real life most wire two to three days only.
  • Five days lose container? You don't book ship that tight, you got buffer.
  • Sound like worst case story used as average. Got data on actual avg delay?
  • Tracking reference, ok. SWIFT got tracking also. What's unique to WorldFirst?
  • Who is WorldFirst? Need licence info before I trust 30K to them.
#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 105 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You wire $30K. Bank app says sent. Supplier says nothing. You call the bank. They blame the correspondent. The correspondent blames the receiving bank. The receiving bank says it's in conversion. Conversion says there's a holiday. Holiday says wait Monday. Monday says AML flagged it. AML says give us a reason. Meanwhile your supplier is asking every two hours. Your delivery slot is gone. Your customer is asking. And the bank app still says sent. I see this every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. CNY direct to Chinese accounts, same-day, one tracking reference. Supplier sees it land. You sleep. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Real talk for everyFor every business owner sourcing from China. You wire $30K. Bank app says sent. Supplier says nothing. You call the bank. They blame the correspondent. The correspondent blames the receiving bank. The receiving bank says it's in conversion. Conversion says there's a holiday. Holiday says wait Monday. Monday says AML flagged it. AML says give us a reason. Meanwhile your supplier is asking every two hours. Your delivery slot is gone. Your customer is asking. And the bank app still says sent. I see thisThis happens every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. CNY direct to Chinese accounts, same-day, one tracking reference. Supplier sees it land. You sleep. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The escalating chase chain (bank to correspondent to receiving bank to conversion to holiday to Monday to AML) is dramatic but each step is plausible. 'Every two hours' from supplier is emotive. 'Your delivery slot is gone' is a single beat treated as universal. 'Supplier sees it land' on WF side is a confident outcome that's defensible for the direct rail but skips cut-off and CN-side caveats. 'You sleep' is emotive close, fine for Gary Vee voice.

  • The blame chain is real, happened to me twice. Last CNY especially.
  • Every two hours supplier asking, bit dramatic. Mine ask once a day.
  • Delivery slot gone in one week? Most ship dates got buffer la.
  • Supplier sees it land same day, every time? What if their bank delay?
  • WorldFirst from where? Need to see Bank Negara before trust 30K.
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 140 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. Your bank says the wire is sent. Your supplier says nothing arrived. Both are telling the truth. Here is how that's possible. When your bank says sent, they mean the wire left their system. They don't mean it landed. Between you and your supplier, there are correspondent banks. Sometimes three. Sometimes five. Each one holds the wire while they queue it, check it, and convert it. A conversion window alone is up to two working days. Add a CN bank holiday or an AML threshold hold and you're at four. So now your supplier is asking where the money is and you're staring at a bank app that says sent. You look like the problem. Stop. Track the wire by SWIFT reference, not by the word sent. WorldFirst pays directly to Chinese accounts in CNY same-day. No correspondent chain. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. Your bank says the wire is sent. Your supplier says nothing arrived. Both are telling the truth. Here is how that's possible. When your bank says sent, they mean the wire left their system. They don't mean it landed. Between you and your supplier, there are correspondent banks. Sometimes three. Sometimes five. Each one holds the wire while they queue it, check it, and convert it. A conversion window alone is up to two working days. Add a CN bank holiday or an AML threshold hold and you're at four. So now your supplier is asking where the money is and you're staring at a bank app that says sent. You look like the problem. Stop. Track the wire by SWIFT reference, not by the word sent. WorldFirst pays directly to Chinese accounts in CNY same-day. No correspondent chain. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core teaching (sent = left bank's system, not arrived; correspondent chain causes silence) is technically accurate and defensible. 'Three to five correspondent banks' is at the high end of realistic ranges, two to three is more common for USD to CNY routes. 'Two working days for conversion' is plausible but presented as a near-universal worst case. 'No correspondent chain' on WF side is broadly correct for the CNY-direct rail but the literal experience can vary, and 'same-day' has caveats around cut-off times.

  • Three to five correspondent banks? My MBB wire usually one hop only lah.
  • SWIFT reference can track? Most bank app don't even show it nicely.
  • WorldFirst who? Never hear before. Got Bank Negara license?
  • Same day to Chinese account? Got cut off time or not?
  • Sound like ad. Show real screenshot of WorldFirst paying same day.
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 136 words

Accusation opener

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

Your bank lied to you and you thanked them for it. They told you the wire was sent. They didn't tell you sent means left our system. Not landed in your supplier's account. Between sent and landed there can be three to five correspondent banks, each one holding your money while it queues for its slot. Add a currency conversion window of up to two working days. Add an AML threshold hold above a number nobody disclosed. Add a Chinese bank holiday you didn't know about. Now you're four days into silence. Your supplier won't start production. Your container ship date is moving. Your customer is asking. I'll say it. Banks call this normal because for them it is. For a business owner running a Q4, it's the difference between hitting your number and missing it. WorldFirst removes the correspondent chain. Direct CNY same-day to Chinese accounts. Stop accepting silence as service. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Your bank lied to you and you thanked them for it. Theytold you the wire was sent. They didn't telled you the wire was sent. They didn't tell you sent means left our system. Not landed in your supplier's account. Between sent and landed there can be three to five correspondent banks, each one holding your money while it queues for its slot. Add a currency conversion window of up to two working days. Add an AML threshold hold above a number nobody disclosed. Add a Chinese bank holiday you didn't know about. Now you're four days into silence. Your supplier won't start production. Your container ship date is moving. Your customer is asking. I'll say it. Banks call this normal because for them it is. For a business owner running a Q4, it's the difference between hitting your number and missing it. WorldFirst removes the correspondent chain. Direct CNY same-day to Chinese accounts. Stop accepting silence as service. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Even with 'lied' removed, 'didn't tell you' carries an implication of withholding that a banking-savvy skeptic will challenge: 'sent' is standard SWIFT terminology, disclosed in T&Cs. 'Three to five correspondent banks' is the high end. 'Banks call this normal because for them it is' is opinion presented as fact. The Q4 / hit your number framing is defensible but stacks consequences. 'Direct CNY same-day' on WF side still has cut-off caveats not mentioned.

  • Didn't tell me? It's literally in the T&C lah. Not bank's fault I don't read.
  • Three to five correspondent on a simple USD to CNY wire? Maybe two only normally.
  • Hit my number, miss my number, ok dramatic. Real impact more like one delivery slip.
  • WorldFirst same day, sure? Cut off time? Public holiday handle how?
  • Who is WorldFirst leh. From which country. Got Bank Negara approval or not.
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 134 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you wire money to China, you need to hear this. Real talk. Last time your supplier said nothing arrived, four things happened and your bank only told you about one. One. The wire left your bank. They called this sent. Two. It hit a correspondent bank in a different timezone and queued for the next clearing cut-off. Three. The currency conversion sat in a window of up to two working days. Four. An AML check above an undisclosed threshold paused it for review. Your supplier sees zero. Your bank says sent. You look like the problem. You lose a week. You lose the order. Stop wiring through chains you can't track. WorldFirst pays direct to Chinese accounts in CNY same-day. One reference. One status. One person to ask. If you're a business owner paying China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

If you wire money to China, you need to hear this. Real talk. Last time your supplier said nothing arrived, four things happened and your bank only told you about one. One. The wire left your bank. They called this sent. Two. It hit a correspondent bank in a different timezone and queued for the next clearing cut-off. Three. The currency conversion sat in a window of up to two working days. Four. An AML check above an undisclosed threshold paused it for review. Your supplier sees zero. Your bank says sent. You look like the problem. You lose a week. You lose the order. Stop wiring through chains you can't track. WorldFirst pays direct to Chinese accounts in CNY same-day. One reference. One status. One person to ask. If you're a business owner paying China, you need WorldFirst. Period. WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The four-things stack is structurally clean and educational. Each factor (correspondent queue, conversion window, AML threshold, bank only reports step one) is real and individually defensible. Main red flag is the implicit guarantee that all four happen on every wire, which is a worst-case framing. 'Undisclosed threshold' on AML is true but bordering on conspiratorial. 'One reference. One status. One person to ask.' on WF side is marketing-strong but defensible for the direct rail.

  • Four things every wire? Mostly only one or two happen lah, not all four.
  • AML threshold undisclosed, ok that's actually a fair point. Mine got held last year.
  • Lose a week, lose the order, bit stacked. Most supplier wait few days.
  • One person to ask, who? Got Malaysia support? English speaker?
  • Worth a look ok but who is WorldFirst leh. Ant Group? Trust them or not?
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 109 words

I'll say it / opinion-as-truth

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. The word sent is the most expensive word your bank uses and nobody warns you about it. You hit send. App says sent. Done, right? Wrong. Sent means the wire left their system. It does not mean it arrived. Between sent and arrived there are correspondent banks, conversion windows of up to two working days, AML holds nobody told you about, and the occasional CN bank holiday. So your supplier is staring at an empty account and you're staring at the word sent and a week disappears. WorldFirst is direct CNY to Chinese bank accounts, same-day, no chain. One status. The word arrived means arrived. That's the difference.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

I'll say it. The word sent is the most expensive word your bank uses and nobody warns you about it. You hit send. App says sent. Done, right? WrongNot quite. Sent means the wire left their system. It does not mean it arrived. Between sent and arrived there are correspondent banks, conversion windows of up to two working days, AML holds nobody told you about, and the occasional CN bank holiday. So your supplier is staring at an empty account and you're staring at the word sent and a week disappears. WorldFirst is direct CNY to Chinese bank accounts, same-day, no chain. One status. The word arrived means arrived. That's the difference.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'The word sent is the most expensive word your bank uses' is opinion-framed which is appropriate for Gary Vee voice and defensible since the sent/arrived gap genuinely costs business owners time and orders. The teaching is accurate. 'Conversion windows of up to two working days' is on the high side but in range. 'The word arrived means arrived' on WF side is product-marketing tight but truthful for the direct CNY rail.

  • Most expensive word, that's clever line lah. But still need data behind it.
  • Conversion two working days? Mostly one day for me. Where you get two?
  • WorldFirst arrived means arrived, ok confident statement. Show me proof.
  • Cut off time how? What if I send Friday 5pm Malaysia time?
  • Who is WorldFirst. First time hearing leh.
#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 136 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here is what most business owners don't understand about international wires. Sent is not a status. Sent is a sentence your bank uses to close the ticket. The actual wire is now somewhere inside a chain of correspondent banks. Two. Three. Sometimes five. Each one holds it while they check sanctions, queue it for cut-off, and convert the currency. Currency conversion alone can sit two working days. AML flags above unannounced thresholds add another day. A Chinese bank holiday adds another. So when your supplier says nothing has arrived, they're right. And when your bank says sent, they're also right. The problem isn't a lie. The problem is the word means two different things. Stop. WorldFirst pays Chinese accounts directly in CNY same-day. No correspondent banks between you and your supplier. One status. One number. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here is what most business owners don't understand about international wires. Sent is not a status. Sent is a sentence your bank uses to close the ticket. The actual wire is now somewhere inside a chain of correspondent banks. Two. Three. Sometimes five. Each one holds it while they check sanctions, queue it for cut-off, and convert the currency. Currency conversion alone can sit two working days. AML flags above unannounced thresholds add another day. A Chinese bank holiday adds another. So when your supplier says nothing has arrived, they're right. And when your bank says sent, they're also right. The problem isn't a lie. The problem is the word means two different things. Stop. WorldFirst pays Chinese accounts directly in CNY same-day. No correspondent banks between you and your supplier. One status. One number. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Cleanest of the Hormozi set. The teaching distinction (sent is a ticket-closer, not a status of arrival) is genuinely useful and broadly accurate. The cascade (correspondent chain + conversion + AML + holiday) is presented as factors rather than a single worst-case stack, which is fairer. 'No correspondent banks between you and your supplier' on WF side is correct for the direct CNY rail. 'One status. One number.' is product-marketing tight but defensible.

  • Sent is a ticket closer, ok that's a fair point. Never thought of it that way.
  • But sent IS the actual SWIFT status lah, not just bank made up word.
  • No correspondent banks, sure? How WorldFirst do that exactly?
  • Same day every day or got cut off? Need to know lah.
  • WorldFirst regulated where? BNM Malaysia? Or just offshore licence?
#8 PG1 Gary Vee Opinion-led 125 words

Classic Stop / Real-talk opener

Soc4.0 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Lost some of the original Gary Vee punch after polish. Still works but feels closer to baseline.

Pure original

Stop. Real talk for every business owner paying Chinese suppliers. Your bank app says sent. Your supplier says nothing arrived. You think someone is lying. Nobody is. Sent means the wire left your bank. It doesn't mean it landed. Between those two words there's a chain of correspondent banks, a currency conversion that can take two working days, maybe an AML check above a threshold nobody warned you about, maybe a Chinese bank holiday you didn't see coming. So you spend the week stuck between a bank that's done with you and a supplier who won't start production. Listen. WorldFirst pays directly to Chinese bank accounts in CNY, same day, no correspondent chain. One status. One person to chase. Stop accepting silence as service. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop. Real talk for everyFor every business owner paying Chinese suppliers. Your bank app says sent. Your supplier says nothing arrived. You think someone is lying. Nobody is. Sent means the wire left your bank. It doesn't mean it landed. Between those two words there's a chain of correspondent banks, a currency conversion that can take two working days, maybe an AML check above a threshold nobody warned you about, maybe a Chinese bank holiday you didn't see coming. So you spend the week stuck between a bank that's done with you and a supplier who won't start production. Listen. WorldFirst pays directly to Chinese bank accounts in CNY, same day, no correspondent chain. One status. One person to chase. Stop accepting silence as serviceWorth knowing if your wires keep going silent. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Soft Gary Vee voice carries the same core teaching (sent vs landed gap, correspondent chain, conversion, AML, holiday) without absolute claims. 'Worth knowing if your wires keep going silent' is a defensible CTA. The supplier-won't-start framing is presented as a maybe ('maybe an AML check', 'maybe a Chinese bank holiday') which is more honest than the Hormozi versions. 'One status. One person to chase.' is the riskiest WF claim because Malaysian SMBs will want to know who the actual person is.

  • Stuck between bank and supplier, yeah this is real. Happened to me last CNY.
  • Maybe AML, maybe holiday. Ok but how often actually? Got real percentage?
  • One person to chase? Who? Got hotline? Got chat? Got English?
  • Who is WorldFirst. From China? Australia? Where regulated?
  • Soft pitch I like la. But still need to see actual product before trust.
#9 PG3 Gary Vee Opinion-led 112 words

Stop using your bank / right-tool reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Pure original

Stop using your bank app as a tracking tool. I'm serious. The bank app gives you one word. Sent. That word ends their job and starts yours. Now you have to chase three correspondent banks, a conversion desk, an AML team in a different country, and a public holiday calendar in Shenzhen. Meanwhile your supplier won't start production because nothing has landed. The bank app was built to tell their teller the wire left. It was not built to tell you when your supplier can start. Wrong tool. WorldFirst was built for the second job. Direct to Chinese accounts in CNY same-day. One reference. One status that means arrived. Use the right tool. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop using your bank app as a tracking tool. I'm seriousYour bank app is not a tracking tool. The bank app gives you one word. Sent. That word ends their job and starts yours. Now you have to chase three correspondent banks, a conversion desk, an AML team in a different country, and a public holiday calendar in Shenzhen. Meanwhile your supplier won't start production because nothing has landed. The bank app was built to tell their teller the wire left. It was not built to tell you when your supplier can start. Wrong tool. WorldFirst was built for the second job. Direct to Chinese accounts in CNY same-day. One reference. One status that means arrived. Use the right tool. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 'wrong tool' framing is defensible. The bank-app-only-shows-one-word point is accurate and a real pain. Listing the chase steps (correspondent, conversion desk, AML team, holiday calendar) is honest about the operational mess. 'WorldFirst was built for the second job' is a clean positioning claim. Main red flag is 'one status that means arrived' is strong and a skeptic will want to verify it on a real wire, not just hear it.

  • Wrong tool framing is fair la. Bank app really only show sent.
  • AML team in different country, ok dramatic. Usually one country only.
  • Built for the second job, marketing speak. What's the actual mechanism?
  • Status means arrived, sure? What if it's stuck on receiving CN side?
  • Right tool yeah ok but is WorldFirst regulated in Malaysia or not?
#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 97 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about international wires. Your bank's status doesn't mean what you think it means. Sent doesn't mean arrived. Sent means it left their system. After that, the wire enters a black box of correspondent banks, conversion windows, AML thresholds, and bank holidays in cities you've never been to. Your bank closes the ticket. You're left holding the line with your supplier. And nobody owns the gap. WorldFirst doesn't have a gap. CNY direct to Chinese accounts, same-day, one tracking reference your supplier can match against their account. Sent and arrived become the same word. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling you about international wires. Your bank's status doesn't mean what you think it means. Sent doesn't mean arrived. Sent means it left their system. After that, the wire enters a black boxchain of correspondent banks, conversion windows, AML thresholds, and bank holidays in cities you've never been to. Your bank closes the ticket. You're left holding the line with your supplier. And nobody owns the gap. WorldFirst doesn't have athat gap. CNY direct to Chinese accounts, same-day, one tracking reference your supplier can match against their account. Sent and arrived become the same word. Period.

'Black box' softened to 'chain' lowers conspiratorial framing, good. The teaching (sent and arrived are different) is accurate. 'Cities you've never been to' is a defensible bit of color. 'Nobody owns the gap' is the strongest line and actually true: between bank's sent and supplier's received, there's no accountable party. 'WorldFirst doesn't have that gap' is a clean comparative claim that holds for the direct CNY rail.

  • Nobody owns the gap, ok that's a really good line. Felt this last time my wire delay.
  • Don't have the gap, sure? CN bank also can hold what.
  • Cities I never been to, dramatic but I get it. Singapore intermediary is common.
  • What if AML still happens on WorldFirst side? Same problem right?
  • Who is WorldFirst. Where based. Where licence. Need answer.
Card P2-A1Pillar P2Short-form 60sTopic 02Math-led
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 126 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. One wire, 2% rate gap, $1,000 quietly gone. You don't feel it. Now run twelve. That's $12,000 a year. On $600,000 of sourcing. Now run that for three years. $36,000. Five years. $60,000. Same supplier, same wires, same business. The only thing changing is what's left in your account. This isn't fees. It's the spread your bank packs into the FX rate. 2% on one wire feels like nothing. Compound it across the year. Compound it across the decade. That's a hire you didn't make. That's a container you couldn't fund. That's your runway. Specialist FX rails like WorldFirst run at 0.3 to 0.5%. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Stop bleeding 2% per wire. Twelve times a year. Every year. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. One wire, 2% rate gap, $1,000 quietly gone. You don't feel it. Now run twelve. That's $12,000 a year. On $600,000 of sourcing. Now run that for three years. $36,000. Five years. $60,000. Same supplier, same wires, same business. The only thing changing is what's left in your account. This isn't fees. It's the spread your bank packs into the FX rate. 2% on one wire feels like nothing. Compound it across the year. Compound it across the decade. That's a hire you didn't make. That's a container you couldn't fund. That's your runway. Specialist FX rails like WorldFirst run at 0.3 to 0.5%. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Stop bleeding 2% per wire. Twelve times a year. Every year. Period. 2% per wire, twelve times a year, every year. Worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

5-year extrapolation ($60K) is the riskiest claim. Assumes 2% margin holds constant on every wire, $50K per wire, 12 wires a year, for 60 months without any rate improvement, renegotiation, or wire-volume change. No SMB will accept that at face value. 'Runway/hire/container' is heavy emotive stacking on top of an unverifiable compound. The core 1-year math ($12K) is defensible; the multi-year compound is where the audit risk lives.

  • 5 years $60K? You assume my bank never improve, never negotiate. Not real.
  • Compound across the decade? Bro I might not even use same bank in 10 years.
  • Where the 2% come from? My HLB cheaper than that.
  • Runway, hire, container. Sound like script, not real.
  • 0.3-0.5% on specialist rail, plus how much fee leh? Got hidden.

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatLow

Real talk for every business owner running sourcing out of China. The 2% rate gap is the thing you're not auditing. You'd renegotiate a supplier over a 2% price hike. You'd switch a 3PL over a 2% margin shift. But the 2% your bank takes on every wire? You don't even see it. Twelve wires a year. $50K each. $12,000 quietly absorbed. That's a hire. That's an extra container. That's your fourth-quarter bonus. And it's recurring. Every year. Stop. Audit the rate. Compare your bank to a specialist FX rail at 0.3 to 0.5%. WorldFirst runs that rail. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The 2% you stopped seeing is the 2% you can get back. Search WorldFirst. Now.

#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 136 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. If you're a business owner wiring to Chinese suppliers, a 2% rate gap on one wire feels small. Here's why it's not. You wire $50,000. Twelve times a year. That's $600,000 of sourcing. Your bank quotes a rate 2% worse than the real one. That's $1,000 per wire. Times twelve. That's $12,000 a year. Gone. Not a fee. No line item. No receipt. It's the exchange rate margin baked into the rate so you never see it. Specialist FX rails sit at 0.3 to 0.5%. Your bank sits at 2 or more. Stop. That's not a small gap. That's a salary. That's inventory. That's your fourth-quarter margin. WorldFirst gives you specialist-rail rates and same-day delivery to Chinese accounts. Two percent looks small per wire. It's $12K a year on $600K of sourcing. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. If you're a business owner wiring to Chinese suppliers, a 2% rate gap on one wire feels small. Here's why it's not. You wire $50,000. Twelve times a year. That's $600,000 of sourcing. Your bank quotes a rate 2% worse than the real one. That's $1,000 per wire. Times twelve. That's $12,000 a year. Gone. Not a fee. No line item. No receipt. It's the exchange rate margin baked into the rate so you never see it. Specialist FX rails sit at 0.3 to 0.5%. Your bank sits at 2 or more. Stop. That's not a small gap. That's a salary. That's inventory. That's your fourth-quarter margin. WorldFirst gives you specialist-rail rates and same-day delivery to Chinese accounts. Two percent looks small per wire. It's $12K a year on $600K of sourcing. Period. Worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core mechanism (2% FX rate margin baked into bank quote vs specialist FX rails at 0.3 to 0.5%) is real and well-supported by findings. The $1,000 per wire and $12,000 annual figures arithmetically check out for a $50K wire at exactly 2%. Main risk is presenting 2% as universal when it varies by bank, currency pair, and corridor. A Maybank user in Malaysia paying CNY may see different margins than a US bank wiring USD to CNY. 'Salary/inventory/fourth-quarter margin' is emotive but defensible.

  • 2% on every wire? My Maybank not so high lah. Where the number from?
  • I don't do 12 wires a year. Maybe 4 or 5. Numbers feel inflated.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never hear before. Got license Malaysia?
  • Specialist rail 0.3-0.5%? Show me real rate screenshot, not just claim.
  • Same day to China? My bank also say same day. Then what's different?
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 124 words

Accusation opener

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

Your bank skimmed $12,000 from your business this year and you signed every single wire. Here's how. Twelve wires to China at $50,000 each. Each one carried a 2% rate margin. That's $1,000 per wire. Quietly. No line item. No receipt. Just a worse rate than the real one, packed into the quote. You assumed wire fee was the cost. The wire fee is the decoy. The rate is the real cost. Banks have done this on supplier payments for decades because business owners look at fees not rates. I'll say it. If you're sourcing $600K a year and you're still wiring through your bank, you're funding their FX desk. WorldFirst is different. Specialist FX rail at 0.3 to 0.5%. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Stop signing off on the skim. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Your bank skimmedquietly kept $12,000 from your business this year and you signed every single wire. Here's how. Twelve wires to China at $50,000 each. Each one carried a 2% rate margin. That's $1,000 per wire. Quietly. No line item. No receipt. Just a worse rate than the real one, packed into the quote. You assumed wire fee was the cost. The wire fee is the decoy. The rate is the real cost. Banks have done this on supplier payments for decades because business owners look at fees not rates. I'll say it. If you're sourcing $600K a year and you're still wiring through your bank, you're funding their FX desk. WorldFirst is different. Specialist FX rail at 0.3 to 0.5%. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Stop signing off on the skim. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'Skimmed' softened to 'quietly kept' reduces but doesn't eliminate the implied wrongdoing. Banks disclose FX margin in T&Cs even if buried, so 'funding their FX desk' framing is opinion not fact. 'Decades' is a sweeping claim. 'Business owners look at fees not rates' is a slight insult that puts the audience on defensive. Core math is defensible. Tone is the red flag, not the numbers.

  • Quietly kept? They disclose in T&C what. Not theft if I sign.
  • Funding their FX desk? Cmon now you trying too hard.
  • You call me stupid for not reading rate? Thanks for the ad.
  • Got proof on 2%? Show me actual mid-market vs bank quote.
  • Banks doing this for decades, but WorldFirst how many years old?
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 136 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you wire to China every month, you need to hear this. Three numbers, one year. Real talk. One. $50,000 per wire. That's a typical SMB order to a Chinese supplier. Two. Twelve wires a year. That's normal cadence for steady sourcing. Three. 2% rate margin. That's what your bank packs into every quote. Multiply. $1,000 per wire times twelve. $12,000 a year. On $600K of sourcing. If you're a business owner paying China, you need to look. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

If you wire to China every month, you need to hear this. Three numbers, one year. Real talk. One. $50,000 per wire. That's a typical SMB order to a Chinese supplier. Two. Twelve wires a year. That's normal cadence for steady sourcing. Three. 2% rate margin. That's what your bank packs into every quote. Multiply. $1,000 per wire times twelve. $12,000 a year. On $600K of sourcing. Vanished into the spread. You never saw it because it never showed up as a fee. It showed up as a worse rate. And while that's happening, specialist FX rails sit at 0.3 to 0.5% on the same wire. The gap is 1.5 to 1.7 points per wire. Stop wiring through your bank for China. WorldFirst gives you specialist-rail rates. Same-day to Chinese accounts. If you're a business owner paying China, you need to look. Period. WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Three-numbers-stack format is clean and educational. $50K x 12 x 2% = $12K is arithmetically airtight. 'Typical SMB order' and 'normal cadence' qualifiers are honest hedges. Main risk is still the 2% universal assumption. The 'you need to look' close is restrained and defensible. Overall low drama, high math accuracy. The only real audit point is the 2% number itself.

  • 12 wires a year typical? I do more like 6. Half the number.
  • 2% rate margin always? Got source la, my bank lower.
  • Worth a look ok but who is WorldFirst leh. Ant Group? Trust them or not?
  • 0.3 to 0.5% on specialist rail, plus monthly fee? Got hidden cost?
  • Math checks out but feels like ad. Real customer story would help.

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. The FX rate on your supplier wires is the most expensive line in your business that nobody is auditing. You wire $50,000 to your supplier. Your bank takes a 2% spread on the rate. $1,000. Twelve wires a year. $12,000. Three years. $36,000. Now flip it. Same wires, specialist FX rail at 0.5%. $250 per wire. $3,000 a year. The gap is $9,000 a year you're handing your bank because nobody told you the rate was the cost. That's not a fee problem. That's a tool problem. WorldFirst is built for this exact wire. Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Pure original

Here's the mechanism most business owners miss when they pay Chinese suppliers. The wire fee isn't the cost. The rate is. Your bank doesn't quote you the actual USD to CNY rate. They quote a rate that's 2% worse. On a $50,000 wire, that's $1,000. Silent. No statement line. Now run twelve wires. $12,000 a year. On $600K of sourcing, that's 2% of your sourcing budget eaten by spread, every year. Specialist FX rails work differently. Near-interbank rate. 0.3 to 0.5% margin instead of 2%. The gap is 1.5 to 1.7 percentage points per wire. Multiply by twelve. That's the difference between a hire and not. A container and not. A bonus and not. WorldFirst runs on the specialist rail. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Stop. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Pure original

The bank quotes a rate 2% worse than the real one. That's $1,000 per wire. Twelve wires a year. $12,000. Silently. Nobody sends you a notification that says you just lost $1,000 to the spread. #1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 133 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. You spent 500K sourcing from China this year. You think your costs were 500K. They weren't. Your bank skimmed 2% off every wire in the exchange rate. That's 10,000 dollars gone. Silently. Now do that again next year. 10K. The year after. 10K. Five years of sourcing at this volume, your bank quietly took 50,000 dollars from your business. That's a hire. That's a container of inventory. That's your kid's school fees. And it's not a fee. It's the rate margin. 2% buried in the rate so you never see the line item. Specialist FX rails sit at 0.3 to 0.5%. The gap is the bleed. Listen. WorldFirst gives you near-interbank rates on every CNY wire. Same 500K of sourcing. Same suppliers. You keep the 10K. Every year. Every wire. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. You spent 500K sourcing from China this year. You think your costs were 500K. They weren't. Your bank skimmed 2% off every wire in the exchange rate. That's 10,000 dollars gone. Silently. That's your kid's school fees. And it's not a fee. It's the rate margin. 2% buried in the rate so you never see the line item. Specialist FX rails sit at 0.3 to 0.5%. The gap is the bleed. Listen. WorldFirst gives you near-interbank rates on every CNY wire. Same 500K of sourcing. Same suppliers. You keep the 10K. Every year. Every wire. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The five-year compound (50K over 5 years) is the riskiest claim. It assumes constant 500K sourcing volume and constant 2% margin for 60 months, which is an extrapolation no SMB will accept at face value, especially because sourcing volume usually grows or shrinks year to year. 'Kid's school fees' is emotive on top of an unverifiable extrapolation. 'Every wire' is an absolute. Still, the per-year 10K figure is defensible from card data.

  • 50K over 5 years assumes I do same volume every year. Business doesn't work like that.
  • Kid's school fees? Cmon you trying too hard with the heartstrings.
  • 2% on every single wire? Even small ones? Bank usually negotiate for big customer.
  • Got source for the 2% number? Bloomberg or just made up?
  • What's the catch with WorldFirst then? Free rate? Got hidden fee somewhere?

Every single one carried a 2% margin packed into the exchange rate. No line item. No receipt. No refund. You looked at your books and saw 500K of supplier payments. The real number was 510K. #5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 137 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you wire money to China for sourcing, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three things happened on every wire you sent this year and you only saw one of them. One. Your bank charged you a wire fee. Maybe 25 dollars, maybe 40. You saw that. Two. Your bank marked up the exchange rate 2% and pocketed your money silently. You didn't see that. Three. Across 500K of annual sourcing, that 2% became 10,000 dollars sitting in your bank's pocket instead of yours. You didn't see that either. Specialist FX rails run at 0.3 to 0.5%. The gap is real and it compounds every year. Stop wiring through banks for China supplier payments. WorldFirst gives you near-interbank rates on every CNY wire. Same suppliers. Same money. You keep the 10K. If you're a business owner sourcing from China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Maybe 25 dollars, maybe 40. You saw that. Two. Your bank marked up the exchange rate 2% and pocketed your money silently. You didn't see that. Three. Across 500K of annual sourcing, that 2% became 10,000 dollars sitting in your bank's pocket instead of yours. You didn't see that either. Specialist FX rails run at 0.3 to 0.5%. The gap is real and it compounds every year. Stop wiring through banks for China supplier payments. WorldFirst gives you near-interbank rates on every CNY wire. Same suppliers. Same money. You keep the 10K. If you're a business owner sourcing from China, you need WorldFirst. Period. WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The three-things stack format is clean and educational. The 25-40 dollar wire fee range is more honest than a single number. Math holds: 2% × 500K = 10K. Main red flag is still the universal 2% claim and 'every wire' framing. 'Compounds every year' is technically accurate but the word compound is a stretch since 2% on 500K is 10K, not actually compounding unless volume grows. Otherwise solid script.

  • Compounds? It's not compound interest, it's just 10K every year. Be honest.
  • Where the 2% number from? My HLB rate not that bad.
  • 25 to 40 dollar wire fee, ya that part is fair. Bank does tell me.

  • Worth a look ok but who owns WorldFirst? Ant Group? Need to know.
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 107 words

I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. Your bank is the most expensive line item in your sourcing P&L and you don't even see it. You spent 500K on Chinese suppliers this year. You're going to spend 500K next year. And the year after. That 2% FX margin sits on every wire. 10,000 dollars a year. 50,000 dollars in five years. That's not fees. That's just the rate gap between what banks pay each other and what they sell to you. And the wild part. You'll never see a line item for it. WorldFirst runs near-interbank rates on every CNY payment. Same suppliers. The 10K stays in your business. Where it belongs. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

I'll say it. Your bank is the most expensive line item in your sourcing P&L and you don't even see it. You spent 500K on Chinese suppliers this year. You're going to spend 500K next year. And the year after. That 2% FX margin sits on every wire. 10,000 dollars a year. 50,000 dollars in five years. That's not fees. That's just the rate gap between what banks pay each other and what they sell to you. And the wild partpart most people miss. You'll never see a line item for it. WorldFirst runs near-interbank rates on every CNY payment. Same suppliers. The 10K stays in your business. Where it belongs. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'Most expensive line item in your sourcing P&L' is a bold claim because shipping, duties, inventory, and labour usually cost more than the FX margin. Skeptic will push back. The 50K over 5 years is the same risky extrapolation as PH2. The 2% × 500K math is sound per-year but the multi-year stack is shaky. 'You won't find this number anywhere' on P&L is technically true and defensible.

  • Most expensive line item? Shipping cost me 50K plus a year, way more than 10K FX.
  • 50K in 5 years assumes same volume every year. Business not like that.
  • 2% on every wire really? My CIMB rate not bad leh.
  • Show real rate comparison please, not just opinion.
  • WorldFirst near-interbank, but where they make their money? Free service got hidden cost.
#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 137 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's the problem most business owners don't see when they review their sourcing P&L. The fee is not the cost. You see a 25 dollar wire fee on the bank statement, you think that's the price of moving money. Wrong. The real cost is the exchange rate margin. Your bank doesn't give you the actual USD to CNY rate. They mark it up 2%. On 500K of annual supplier payments, that's 10,000 dollars taken silently. You will never see it on a statement because it's baked into the rate itself. Specialist FX rails run at 0.3 to 0.5% margin. Same wire. Same destination. One-quarter the cost. This is how banks make billions off business owners who only check the fee column. Stop. WorldFirst uses near-interbank rates on every CNY payment. Same 500K. You save the 10K. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the problem most business owners don't see when they review their sourcing P&L. The fee is not the cost. You see a 25 dollar wire fee on the bank statement, you think that's the price of moving money. Wrong. The real cost is the exchange rate margin. Your bank doesn't give you the actual USD to CNY rate. They mark it up 2%. On 500K of annual supplier payments, that's 10,000 dollars taken silently. You will never see it on a statement because it's baked into the rate itself. Specialist FX rails run at 0.3 to 0.5% margin. Same wire. Same destination. One-quarter the cost. This is how banks make billions off business owners who only check the fee column. Stop. WorldFirst uses near-interbank rates on every CNY payment. Same 500K. You save the 10K. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Cleanest of the Hormozi set. The fee vs rate distinction is technically accurate and a real underappreciated insight. Math is internally consistent and lifted directly from card data. 'One-quarter the cost' (2% vs 0.5%) is mathematically clean. 'Banks make billions' is generic enough to be defensible. Main risk is still presenting 2% as universal without 'up to' qualifier. 'You will never see it on a statement' is largely true.

  • Ok the fee vs rate distinction actually make sense, fair.
  • But 2% always? My bank not so bad. Maybe US bank different.
  • One-quarter the cost on paper, but how much WorldFirst really charge me?
  • Banks make billions, sure. But how WorldFirst make money then? Free?
  • Who regulate WorldFirst in Malaysia? Need to check before move money.

#9 PG3 Gary Vee Opinion-led 115 words

Stop using your bank / right-tool reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Pure original

Stop using your bank for China sourcing. I'm serious. Banks are great at a lot of things. Cross-border supplier payments isn't one of them. You wouldn't use a hammer to cut wood. So why are you using a domestic bank to send 500K to Chinese suppliers a year? Here's what happens. Every wire, hidden 2% rate margin. End of year, 10,000 dollars gone. Not as a fee. As a rate gap. Specialist FX rails sit at 0.3 to 0.5%. That's the difference. WorldFirst is built for this. Near-interbank rate on every CNY payment. The 10K stays in your business. Use the right tool for the job. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop using your bank for China sourcing. I'm seriousYour bank is not the right tool for China sourcing payments. Banks are great at a lot of things. Cross-border supplier payments isn't one of them. You wouldn't use a hammer to cut wood. So why are you using a domestic bank to send 500K to Chinese suppliers a year? Here's what happens. Every wire, hidden 2% rate margin. End of year, 10,000 dollars gone. Not as a fee. As a rate gap. Specialist FX rails sit at 0.3 to 0.5%. That's the difference. WorldFirst is built for this. Near-interbank rate on every CNY payment. The 10K stays in your business. Use the right tool for the job. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The hammer/wood metaphor is defensible framing. Math is consistent. Specialist FX rails at 0.3-0.5% is directly from card data, defensible. 'Banks great at a lot of things' softens the criticism. Main risk is the assumption that 500K annual sourcing applies to viewer, and the 2% universal figure. The 'right tool' frame is more persuasive than accusatory.

  • Hammer wood analogy ok but show me actual product first.
  • 500K annual sourcing? That's bigger than me lah. I do 100K maybe.
  • 2% rate margin every wire? Even on big amount? Bank usually negotiate.
  • Where WorldFirst regulated? PayNet? BNM? Or some offshore thing?
  • Switching cost also a thing. Account setup, integration, supplier KYC, all takes time.

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Pure original

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about your sourcing budget. You think the bank makes money on the wire fee. They don't. They make money on the rate. You sent 500K to Chinese suppliers this year. Every wire carried a 2% FX margin packed into the exchange rate. 10,000 dollars they pocketed silently. Across the year. No line. No receipt. Card P2-A13Pillar P2Short-form 60sTopic 04Math-led
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 136 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. You pay your Chinese supplier 20K through PayPal once a month. The receipt says 20K. The real cost is closer to 22K. Why? 4.4% cross-border fee, that's 880 dollars. 3.5% FX margin, that's another 700. Plus a 4 to 5% supplier markup because they're absorbing PayPal's receiving fee. Add it up: 2,380 dollars per order. Now run it for a year. Twelve orders. That's 28,560 dollars handed to a payments app and a padded invoice line. Three years? Over 85,000 dollars. That's a hire. That's inventory. That's your kid's school fees. And it's not theft. It's just PayPal pricing built for retail volume, not 20K supplier payments. Listen. WorldFirst uses specialist FX rates and pays Chinese suppliers in CNY directly. Same 20K. The leak shrinks to hundreds, not thousands. Every order. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. You pay your Chinese supplier 20K through PayPal once a month. The receipt says 20K. The real cost is closer to 22K. Why? 4.4% cross-border fee, that's 880 dollars. 3.5% FX margin, that's another 700. Plus a 4 to 5% supplier markup because they're absorbing PayPal's receiving fee. Add it up: 2,380 dollars per order. Now run it for a year. Twelve orders. That's 28,560 dollars handed to a payments app and a padded invoice line. Three years? Over 85,000 dollars. That's a hire. That's inventory. That's your kid's school fees. And it's not theft. It's just PayPal pricing built for retail volume, not 20K supplier payments. Listen. WorldFirst uses specialist FX rates and pays Chinese suppliers in CNY directly. Same 20K. The leak shrinks to hundreds, not thousands. Every order. Every year. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The compounding extrapolation (one order a month for three years = $85K) is the highest-risk claim. It assumes constant order size, constant fee structure, constant supplier markup, and no negotiation across 36 months. 'Your kid's school fees' is emotive layered onto an unverifiable extrapolation. 'PayPal pricing built for retail, not 20K supplier payments' is defensible as a category statement but the math chain is shakier than the individual fee figures.

  • 85K over 3 years? You assume I send 20K every month never stop. Cooked.
  • My order size varies. Sometimes 5K sometimes 30K. This math doesn't reflect reality.
  • Kid's school fees? Bro really pulling on heartstrings now.
  • PayPal got business rates that lower right? You picking retail tier to make it sound bad.
  • Specialist FX rates means what exactly? Show me the actual WorldFirst rate.
#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 124 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China with PayPal. You think PayPal is convenient. It is. It's also expensive. You send $20,000 to your supplier. PayPal takes $880 on the cross-border fee. Then $700 more on the FX margin nobody warned you about. Then your supplier hands you a PI with another 4 to 5% padded in because PayPal charges them too. By the time the goods ship, that $20K order cost you $22,380. Plus a 21-day fund hold on the supplier side that they price in next time. I see this every single week. SMEs paying retail prices for B2B volume because the app is familiar. Stop. WorldFirst. Specialist FX. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Full $20K lands. Supplier doesn't pad. You sleep. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Real talk for everyFor every business owner sourcing from China with PayPal. You think PayPal is convenient. It is. It's also expensive. You send $20,000 to your supplier. PayPal takes $880 on the cross-border fee. Then $700 more on the FX margin nobody warned you about. Then your supplier hands you a PI with another 4 to 5% padded in because PayPal charges them too. By the time the goods ship, that $20K order cost you $22,380. Plus a 21-day fund hold on the supplier side that they price in next time. I see thisThis happens every single week. SMEs paying retail prices for B2B volume because the app is familiar. Stop. WorldFirst. Specialist FX. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Full $20K lands. Supplier doesn't pad. You sleep. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Adds the 21-day fund hold detail from source findings which strengthens credibility on the supplier-side cost. 'Plus a 21-day fund hold on the supplier side that they price in next time' is exactly the kind of mechanism-grounded detail that survives a skeptic's poke. Risk remains the universal supplier markup framing. 'Supplier doesn't pad' on the WF side is a soft claim because some suppliers still pad regardless of rail, just for cushion.

  • 21-day fund hold true for PayPal but mostly for new sellers right? Established suppliers maybe not.
  • Supplier doesn't pad with WorldFirst - how you know? Maybe they pad regardless.
  • $22,380 on $20K - this assume all worst case. My orders never hit this much.
  • Convenient is expensive ok fair. But switching also has friction cost.
  • Specialist FX, direct CNY - I keep hearing this. Just show me the rate today.
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 133 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers through PayPal, you're getting destroyed and you don't even see it. Here's the math. You send 20K to your supplier. The receipt says 20K. The actual cost? Way higher. PayPal hits you with a 4.4% cross-border fee. That's 880 dollars gone. Then they take another 3.5% on the exchange rate. That's another 700 dollars. So your visible cost just hit 1,580. But it gets worse. Stop. Your supplier knows PayPal charges them on the receiving side too. So they pad your invoice 4 to 5%. That's another 800 dollars baked into the PI. Total damage on a 20K order? 2,380 dollars. Eleven percent gone. WorldFirst was built for this. Specialist rate. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Same supplier. Hundreds in fees, not thousands. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers through PayPal, you're getting destroyed and you don't even see itthe real cost is much higher than the receipt. Here's the math. You send 20K to your supplier. The receipt says 20K. The actual cost? is way higher. PayPal hits you with a 4.4% cross-border fee. That's 880 dollars gone. Then they take another 3.5% on the exchange rate. That's another 700 dollars. So your visible cost just hit 1,580. But it gets worse. Stop. Your supplier knows PayPal charges them on the receiving side too. So they pad your invoice 4 to 5%. That's another 800 dollars baked into the PI. Total damage on a 20K order? 2,380 dollars. Eleven percent gone. WorldFirst was built for this. Specialist rate. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Same supplier. Hundreds in fees, not thousands. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core fee math (4.4% + 3.5% FX + 4-5% supplier markup) is defensible and grounded in source findings, but the total '$2,380 on $20K = 11%' is stacked aggressively and assumes the worst-case supplier markup applies universally. 'Eleven percent gone' is a sharp framing that a skeptic with lower historical fee experience will challenge. The 'WorldFirst hundreds in fees, not thousands' line is broadly accurate per source data but lacks a specific number, which weakens the contrast on follow-up.

  • My supplier never pad my PI for PayPal lah. Where this 4-5% from?
  • PayPal fee for me is 3.9% not 4.4%. Numbers feel off.
  • Eleven percent? That's only if everything stacks worst case. Not always.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never hear before. They got license in MY?
  • Show me real rate comparison side by side la, not just claim.
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 134 words

Receipt-doesn't-match-reality opener

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

Your PayPal receipt is lying to you. Not technically. Just functionally. Here's how. You sent 20K to your Chinese supplier. The receipt confirmed 20K. Done deal, right? Wrong. PayPal took 4.4% off the top, 880 dollars, before the wire even moved. Then they marked up the exchange rate 3.5%, another 700 dollars gone, baked into the rate so it never shows as a fee. That's 1,580 dollars on a 20K order. And here's the part nobody warns you about. Your supplier sees PayPal coming and adds 4 to 5% to your invoice because they're eating a receiving fee on their end. So your actual cost lands closer to 22,380 dollars. The receipt said 20K. You paid 22K. Stop trusting receipts that hide the math. WorldFirst publishes the rate. Specialist FX. Direct CNY to Chinese banks. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Your PayPal receipt is lying to youonly tells part of the story. Not technically wrong. Just functionallyincomplete. Here's how. You sent 20K to your Chinese supplier. The receipt confirmed 20K. Done deal, right? WrongNot quite. PayPal took 4.4% off the top, 880 dollars, before the wire even moved. Then they marked up the exchange rate 3.5%, another 700 dollars gone, baked into the rate so it never shows as a fee. That's 1,580 dollars on a 20K order. And here's the part nobody warns you about. Your supplier sees PayPal coming and adds 4 to 5% to your invoice because they're eating a receiving fee on their end. So your actual cost lands closer to 22,380 dollars. The receipt said 20K. You paid 22K. Stop trusting receipts that hide the math. WorldFirst publishes the rate. Specialist FX. Direct CNY to Chinese banks. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'Your PayPal receipt only tells part of the story' is softened from the original 'lying' and now stays factually accurate because PayPal's receipts do disclose fees in the breakdown, just not the FX margin or the supplier-side markup. The mechanism reveal is solid. Weakest spot is still the 4-5% supplier markup as universal, which depends entirely on the supplier's policy. 'WorldFirst publishes the rate' is true for the WF business product but not a specific number, so on follow-up a skeptic will want to see it.

  • Receipt also show fee la. PayPal not hiding it.
  • Supplier markup for PayPal not universal. Mine never charge me extra.
  • WorldFirst publishes the rate - what rate today? Need to see number to compare.
  • FX margin 3.5%? Sometimes PayPal market rate not so bad you know.
  • Where can I find this WorldFirst? Got app or website? You assume I know.
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 137 words

Direct address + three-layer problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you pay Chinese suppliers through PayPal, you need to hear this. Real talk. Last 20K order, three things happened and your receipt only showed one of them. One. PayPal charged a 4.4% cross-border fee. That's 880 dollars. You saw that. Two. PayPal marked up the USD to CNY exchange rate 3.5%. That's another 700 dollars, baked into the rate, never on a line item. You didn't see that. Three. Your supplier padded the PI 4 to 5% because they absorb a receiving fee on PayPal too. That's another 800 dollars hiding in your invoice. You didn't see that either. Total leakage on a 20K order? 2,380. Almost 12 percent. Stop paying retail prices for supplier-grade volume. WorldFirst is built for 20K orders to China. Specialist FX. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. If you're a business owner paying China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

If you pay Chinese suppliers through PayPal, you need to hear this. Real talk. Last 20K order, three things happened and your receipt only showed one of them. One. PayPal charged a 4.4% cross-border fee. That's 880 dollars. You saw that. Two. PayPal marked up the USD to CNY exchange rate 3.5%. That's another 700 dollars, baked into the rate, never on a line item. You didn't see that. Three. Your supplier padded the PI 4 to 5% because they absorb a receiving fee on PayPal too. That's another 800 dollars hiding in your invoice. You didn't see that either. Total leakage on a 20K order? 2,380. Almost 12 percent. Stop paying retail prices for supplier-grade volume. WorldFirst is built for 20K orders to China. Specialist FX. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. If you're a business owner paying China, you need WorldFirst. Period. WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The three-layer problem stack is structurally similar to PH4 but framed as 'you only saw one of them' which is sharper. The fee figures are solid. 'Almost 12 percent' on a $20K order is the bold number to defend and it does sit at the upper end of the range. The 'WorldFirst is worth a look' brand-search CTA is correctly softened. Weakest spot is still treating supplier markup as inevitable rather than situational. Risk also includes 'reputation with that supplier' which doesn't appear in this version but the 12% framing alone may push skeptics to push back on math composition.

  • 12% leak? My PayPal cost feels more like 5-6% total. You're stacking worst case.
  • If supplier really pads 4-5% why my Foshan guy never do? You're generalising.
  • Three things I didn't see? Bro the fee is right there on the receipt, FX too sometimes.
  • WorldFirst is worth a look ok but what license they got in SEA?
  • Specialist FX, direct CNY rails - need to see the actual product, not just claim.
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 119 words

I'll say it / opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. PayPal is the most overpriced thing in your supply chain and nobody talks about it. You pay your Chinese supplier $20,000 through PayPal. Real cost? $2,380. That's on one order. Now run the math. One order a month. That's $28,560 a year. Three years, you're north of $85,000. That's a salary. That's inventory. That's your margin. And here's what nobody tells you. The 4.4% fee is just the visible part. The 3.5% FX margin is hidden in the rate. And your supplier added 4 to 5% to the PI because they're eating fees on their end too. WorldFirst uses specialist FX rates and pays Chinese suppliers in CNY directly. The money goes back into your business. Where it belongs. Period.

Now run the math. One order a month. That's $28,560 a year. Three years, you're north of $85,000. That's a salary. That's inventory. That's your margin. And here's what nobody tells you. The 4.4% fee is just the visible part. The 3.5% FX margin is hidden in the rate. And your supplier added 4 to 5% to the PI because they're eating fees on their end too. WorldFirst uses specialist FX rates and pays Chinese suppliers in CNY directly. The money goes back into your business. Where it belongs. Period.

The line 'hidden in the rate' is correct but applied to PayPal which does have FX disclosure on the conversion screen, even if buried.

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Here's the problem most business owners don't realize when they use PayPal for Chinese suppliers. The fee is not the fee. You see PayPal charged 4.4%, that's 880 dollars on a 20K order. You think that's the cost. Wrong. The exchange rate margin is the bigger bite. PayPal marks up USD to CNY by roughly 3.5%, hidden inside the rate. On 20K, that's another 700 dollars. Stop. Real talk for everyFor every business owner paying Chinese suppliers through PayPal. You sent $20,000 to your factory. PayPal says delivered. Cool. But here's what just happened. 4.4% cross-border fee, that's $880. FX margin on the rate, another $700. So far you're out $1,580 on a $20K order. And here's the kickerpart most people miss. Your supplier added 4 to 5% to the invoice because they're absorbing PayPal's receiving fee. That's another $800 baked in. Your $20K order really cost you $22,380. Listen. WorldFirst was built for supplier payments. Specialist FX. Direct CNY to Chinese banks. The math drops to hundreds, not thousands. Stop letting a retail app price your wholesale volumeWorth knowing if your retail app is pricing your wholesale volume. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Numbers align with source data on the visible PayPal side. The supplier markup at 4-5% is the soft spot, as in the Hormozi set, because it relies on the source claim that 'some suppliers add 4-5%' generalised to a default. 'Retail app pricing wholesale volume' is the strongest category-framing line and survives challenge well because PayPal's own tier structure confirms it. 'Specialist FX, direct CNY to Chinese banks' is accurate to source on WF.

  • $2,380 on $20K? My last PayPal supplier payment cost much less.
  • 4-5% PI padding is hearsay. My supplier never tell me this is for PayPal.
  • Retail app pricing wholesale volume - ok fair point but need real alternative comparison.
  • WorldFirst sounds like another PayPal that just doesn't have brand yet.
  • Hundreds not thousands - show me a real screenshot lah, not just talk.
#9 PG3 Gary Vee Opinion-led 113 words

Right tool for the job reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Pure original

Stop using PayPal to pay Chinese suppliers. I'm serious. PayPal is a tool. It's the wrong tool. You wouldn't use a credit card to buy a house. So why are you using a retail payment app to send $20,000 to a factory? Here's what happens. You send $20K. 4.4% fee, $880. FX margin, $700. Supplier markup baked into the PI, another $800. Real cost? $22,380. That's $2,380 in leakage on every order. WorldFirst is built for this. Specialist FX. Direct CNY rails to Chinese bank accounts. Same $20K. Full amount lands. Supplier doesn't need to pad the PI. Use the right tool for the job. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Stop using PayPal to pay Chinese suppliers. I'm seriousPayPal is not the right tool for paying Chinese suppliers. PayPal is a tool. It's the wrong tool. You wouldn't use a credit card to buy a house. So why are you using a retail payment app to send $20,000 to a factory? Here's what happens. You send $20K. 4.4% fee, $880. FX margin, $700. Supplier markup baked into the PI, another $800. Real cost? $22,380. That's $2,380 in leakage on every order. WorldFirst is built for this. Specialist FX. Direct CNY rails to Chinese bank accounts. Same $20K. Full amount lands. Supplier doesn't need to pad the PI. Use the right tool for the job. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Right-tool-for-the-job analogy is clean and survives challenge well. Math layers (880 + 700 + 800 = 2,380) are arithmetic-accurate. The weakest spot is the assumption that switching to WorldFirst means supplier won't pad the PI, which is true if WF settles in CNY directly but a skeptic may not know that mechanism. 'Use the right tool for the job, your margin depends on it' is a brand-safe close.

  • Credit card to buy house analogy - too dramatic. PayPal works fine for most.
  • If WF pays CNY direct, supplier still need to verify and clear. Same delay or not?
  • Supplier don't pad PI for me. You assume my guy is greedy.
  • Specialist FX what rate ah? Need the actual number to decide.
  • Margin depends on it - ok but switching is also a cost. Time, paperwork, KYC.
#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 127 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about PayPal supplier payments. The receipt is not the cost. You send $20K to your Chinese factory. Receipt confirms $20K. Done? No. PayPal took 4.4% as a cross-border fee, that's $880, you saw that one. Then 3.5% FX margin on the conversion to CNY, another $700, you didn't see that. Then your supplier added 4 to 5% to the invoice because they're absorbing PayPal's receiving fee on their side. That's $800 hiding in your PI. Total damage on a $20K order? $2,380. Almost 12 percent. PayPal is brilliant for retail. It's the wrong tool for $20K supplier orders. WorldFirst is built differently. Specialist FX. Direct CNY to Chinese banks. The rate you see is the rate you get. That's the difference. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling you about PayPal supplier payments. The receipt is not the cost. You send $20K to your Chinese factory. Receipt confirms $20K. Done? NoNot quite. PayPal took 4.4% as a cross-border fee, that's $880, you saw that one. Then 3.5% FX margin on the conversion to CNY, another $700, you didn't see that. Then your supplier added 4 to 5% to the invoice because they're absorbing PayPal's receiving fee on their side. That's $800 hiding in your PI. Total damage on a $20K order? $2,380. Almost 12 percent. That's the difference. Period.
