Three-layer truth structure is the same mechanism as PH4 but in Gary Vee voice. Strongest line is 'PayPal is brilliant for retail. It's the wrong tool for $20K supplier orders' which is defensible category framing. Weakest is still the supplier markup as universal. 'Rate you see is rate you get' is a soft claim that's directionally true for WF business but not exactly literal at the receipt level because there's still a small spread.

  • Rate you see is rate you get - so WorldFirst takes zero margin? Sure or not?
  • Brilliant for retail wrong tool for B2B - actually this part fair.
  • Almost 12% only if supplier really adds 4-5%. Not standard for everyone.
  • WorldFirst direct CNY to Chinese banks - they have license for SAFE compliance?
  • I can audit PayPal cost. How do I audit WorldFirst cost before I switch?
Card P2-A13Pillar P2Short-form 60sTopic 04Comparison
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 133 words

Cold open + scale-mismatch reveal

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatLow

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. PayPal at 200 dollars charges you 9 bucks. You don't even notice. PayPal at 20 thousand charges you 880 dollars. Same app. Same swipe. The product didn't change. The math did. Personal-volume apps are priced for personal volumes. When you scale up from buying a sweater to paying a factory, that percentage doesn't shrink. It grows with every zero. 4.4% on 200 is coffee money. 4.4% on 20K is a flight to Guangzhou. And we haven't even talked about FX. PayPal adds another 3.5% margin on the dollar-to-CNY conversion. That's 700 more dollars gone before your supplier sees a cent. Total cost: roughly 1,580 dollars on a 20K wire. Stop. WorldFirst is priced for business-volume payments. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The bigger the order, the more you save. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. PayPal at 200 dollars charges you 9 bucks. You don't even notice. PayPal at 20 thousand charges you 880 dollars. Same app. Same swipe. The product didn't change. The math did. Personal-volume apps are priced for personal volumes. When you scale up from buying a sweater to paying a factory, that percentage doesn't shrink. It grows with every zero. 4.4% on 200 is coffee money. 4.4% on 20K is a flight to Guangzhou. And we haven't even talked about FX. PayPal adds another 3.5% margin on the dollar-to-CNY conversion. That's 700 more dollars gone before your supplier sees a cent. Total cost: roughly 1,580 dollars on a 20K wire. Stop. WorldFirst is priced for business-volume payments. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The bigger the order, the more you save. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Cleanest math in the Hormozi set. The scale-mismatch teaching (percentage stays the same, pain grows linearly) is genuinely educational and hard to dispute. 'Flight to Guangzhou' is a relatable visual. The 1,580 cumulative figure stacks fewer components than PH1 (transaction + FX only, no PI surcharge), making it more defensible. The 'we haven't even talked about FX' beat is honest sequencing. Main residual risk is the 3.5% FX margin presented as exact when it's a typical range.

  • Flight to Guangzhou ha ok funny. But 4.4% confirmed?
  • Percentage stay same pain grow ok this part actually make sense.
  • FX margin 3.5% always? My PayPal show different number when I check.
  • Bigger order more save? Show me actual WorldFirst rate at 20K vs PayPal please.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Need to verify first.
#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 132 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China through PayPal. You think it's the easy option. It's not. You send 20K. Supplier sees the funds. Then PayPal puts a 21-day hold on the receiver side. Supplier won't start production because the money isn't fully released. You're calling PayPal. PayPal says it's policy. Supplier says they want T/T next time. Meanwhile you're paying 880 in fees and another 700 in FX margin. 1,580 dollars to send a payment that doesn't even fully arrive for three weeks. Your customers are waiting. Your inventory is late. Your margin is bleeding. And you're staring at a hold screen on an app built for refunding sneakers. I see this every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. No hold. Supplier starts. You sleep. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Real talk for everyFor every business owner sourcing from China through PayPal. You think it's the easy option. It's not. You send 20K. Supplier sees the funds. Then PayPal puts a 21-day hold on the receiver side. Supplier won't start production because the money isn't fully released. You're calling PayPal. PayPal says it's policy. Supplier says they want T/T next time. Meanwhile you're paying 880 in fees and another 700 in FX margin. 1,580 dollars to send a payment that doesn't even fully arrive for three weeks. Your customers are waiting. Your inventory is late. Your margin is bleeding. And you're staring at a hold screen on an app built for refunding sneakers. I see thisThis happens every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. No hold. Supplier starts. You sleep. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 21-day hold pain point is supported by findings and is a real PayPal mechanism on receiver side, particularly for new/high-risk accounts. The 'supplier wants T/T next time' beat will resonate with anyone who's been there. Softens 'I see this every week' to 'this happens every week' which is more defensible. 'Hold screen on an app built for refunding sneakers' is sharp and on-brand. Main risk is the 21-day hold framed as universal when it's situational.

  • 21-day hold not always lah. Mostly first transaction or risky pattern.
  • Supplier want T/T next time, ya this is real. Happened to me before.
  • Hold screen sneaker refund app, ok funny but ad-feel high.
  • WorldFirst no hold, sure or not? What if transaction flagged for review?
  • Who is WorldFirst? Need Malaysia license info please.
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 134 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers through PayPal, you're being charged like a tourist. Here's the math. PayPal at 200 dollars feels cheap. 4.4% is 9 bucks. You shrug. PayPal at 20 thousand is 880 dollars. Same app. Same button. Same brand. Different reality. And that's just the transaction fee. FX margin to CNY adds another 3.5%. That's 700 more. Now your supplier adds 4 to 5% to the PI because they're eating a receiving fee too. Your 20K payment just cost you over 2,300 dollars in real money. Stop. PayPal was built for buying t-shirts, not paying factories. WorldFirst is built for business-volume payments. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. If you wouldn't hand a bank teller 880 dollars in cash, don't hand it to an app. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. If you're a business owner paying Chinese suppliers through PayPal, you're being charged like a tourist. Here's the math. PayPal at 200 dollars feels cheap. 4.4% is 9 bucks. You shrug. PayPal at 20 thousand is 880 dollars. Same app. Same button. Same brand. Different reality. And that's just the transaction fee. FX margin to CNY adds another 3.5%. That's 700 more. Now your supplier adds 4 to 5% to the PI because they're eating a receiving fee too. Your 20K payment just cost you over 2,300 dollars in real money. Stop. PayPal was built for buying t-shirts, not paying factories. WorldFirst is built for business-volume payments. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. If you wouldn't hand a bank teller 880 dollars in cash, don't hand it to an app. Period. Worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core math (4.4% on 20K = 880, 3.5% FX margin = 700, 4-5% PI surcharge) is supported by the findings array and is industry-typical for PayPal cross-border B2B. Main risk is the 'PI surcharge' framing as universal when in practice only some suppliers add it openly and others fold it into the quote invisibly. 'Tourist' framing is colorful but defensible. 'Built for t-shirts not factories' is generic enough to land. The cumulative 2,300 figure stacks three separate cost components, which a skeptic may challenge if they only see one of them on a statement.

  • 4.4% sound high lah, my PayPal sometimes show lower fee.
  • Supplier add 4-5% to PI? My one just quote and that's it. How I know if hidden?
  • Who is WorldFirst leh, first time hearing.
  • Sound like ad. Show real PayPal screenshot vs WorldFirst screenshot.
  • Tourist prices ok funny but I just want to know got Malaysia license or not.
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 129 words

Accusation opener

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

PayPal charged you 880 dollars on your last supplier payment and you didn't blink. Here's why. At 200 bucks the fee feels invisible because the fixed-fee portion eats most of the pain. At 20 thousand the percentage takes over. 4.4% kicks in fully. That's 880 dollars on transaction fee alone. Then add 3.5% FX margin on the conversion to CNY. That's another 700. Then your supplier quietly adds 4 to 5% to the PI because PayPal charges them too. By the time the money lands, you've paid roughly 2,300 dollars to use an app that was designed to split a dinner bill. I'll say it. PayPal isn't a B2B tool. It's a consumer wallet. WorldFirst is the business rail. Near-interbank rates. No PI surcharge from suppliers. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Stop paying app-tier fees on business-tier orders. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

PayPal charged you 880 dollars on your last supplier payment and you didn't blink. Here's why. At 200 bucks the fee feels invisible because the fixed-fee portion eats most of the pain. At 20 thousand the percentage takes over. 4.4% kicks in fully. That's 880 dollars on transaction fee alone. Then add 3.5% FX margin on the conversion to CNY. That's another 700. Then your supplier quietly adds 4 to 5% to the PI because PayPal charges them too. By the time the money lands, you've paid roughly 2,300 dollars to use an app that was designed to split a dinner bill. I'll say it. PayPal isn't a B2B tool. It's a consumer wallet. WorldFirst is the business rail. Near-interbank rates. No PI surcharge from suppliers. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Stop paying app-tier fees on business-tier orders. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The cumulative 2,300 dollar claim stacks three layers (880 transaction + 700 FX + ~700 supplier PI surcharge) which is technically supported by findings but lands as steep. 'Designed to split a dinner bill' is fun but reductive. 'PayPal isn't a B2B tool' is true in spirit but PayPal does have a B2B product (Business account) which a skeptic could point to. The 'didn't blink' opener accuses the viewer of inattention, which can put audience on defensive. Strong on findings, weaker on tone.

  • 2,300 dollars on 20K? That's 11.5%. Sound exaggerated.
  • PayPal got Business account what. Not just consumer wallet.
  • Didn't blink? Bro I read my statement, I noticed.
  • Supplier add 4-5% silently, how I check? Got way to verify?
  • Who is WorldFirst? Regulated by BNM or what?
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 154 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you pay Chinese suppliers through PayPal, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three things happen when you scale up and you only feel one of them. One. At 200 bucks you pay 9 dollars in fees and you barely notice. Two. At 20 thousand you pay 880 dollars in transaction fees and it feels like getting hit by a truck. Same app. Same percentage. Bigger number. Three. PayPal adds another 3.5% FX margin on the CNY conversion. That's 700 more. Plus your supplier quietly adds 4 to 5% to the PI because they're also paying PayPal a receiving fee. Your 20K supplier order just cost you roughly 2,300 dollars in real money, plus 21-day hold risk on the supplier side that they're pricing into your quote. Stop using consumer apps for business volumes. WorldFirst is built for this. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. No PI surcharge. If you're a business owner paying China, you need WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

If you pay Chinese suppliers through PayPal, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three things happen when you scale up and you only feel one of them. One. At 200 bucks you pay 9 dollars in fees and you barely notice. Two. At 20 thousand you pay 880 dollars in transaction fees and it feels like getting hit by a truck. Same app. Same percentage. Bigger number. Three. PayPal adds another 3.5% FX margin on the CNY conversion. That's 700 more. Plus your supplier quietly adds 4 to 5% to the PI because they're also paying PayPal a receiving fee. Your 20K supplier order just cost you roughly 2,300 dollars in real money, plus 21-day hold risk on the supplier side that they're pricing into your quote. Stop using consumer apps for business volumes. WorldFirst is built for this. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. No PI surcharge. If you're a business owner paying China, you need WorldFirst. Period. WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Stacks the most claims of any PH script: 880 transaction + 700 FX + 4-5% supplier PI surcharge + 21-day hold pricing. The 21-day hold reference is supported by findings but adds a fifth claim that further compounds. 'Hit by a truck' is vivid but might feel exaggerated. 'You need WorldFirst' (in original) is hard-sell, softened in iteration to 'worth a look'. The total cost claim of 2,300+ requires the viewer to accept all four cost layers simultaneously, which is a tall order.

  • 2,300 on 20K? That's saying 11.5% loss. Hard to believe without proof.
  • 21-day hold? Only on new accounts or risky transactions, not always.
  • Supplier price in 4-5% to PI, how I confirm? They never tell me.
  • Like ad lah. Real talk should mean real proof, not just numbers.
  • Worth a look ok but who is WorldFirst and got Malaysia license?
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 124 words

I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. PayPal is the most expensive rail in your supply chain and nobody talks about it. PayPal at 200 dollars costs 9 bucks. Nobody cares. PayPal at 20K costs 880 dollars. Now you care. Now run the math. Once a month. That's 880 a month. 10K a year just on transaction fees, before FX. Add the 3.5% FX margin and you're at 1,580 per wire. Twelve wires a year. That's nearly 19 grand a year. Disappearing into an app you only signed up for because it was easy. That's a hire. That's inventory. That's your margin. PayPal won't tell you this because they make more money the bigger your orders get. WorldFirst uses near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The percentage goes down, not up. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

I'll say it. PayPal is the most expensive rail in your supply chain and nobody talks about it. PayPal at 200 dollars costs 9 bucks. Nobody cares. PayPal at 20K costs 880 dollars. Now you care. Now run the math. Once a month. That's 880 a month. 10K a year just on transaction fees, before FX. Add the 3.5% FX margin and you're at 1,580 per wire. Twelve wires a year. That's nearly 19 grand a year. Disappearing into an app you only signed up for because it was easy. That's a hire. That's inventory. That's your margin. PayPal won't tell you this because they make more money the bigger your orders get. WorldFirst uses near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The percentage goes down, not up. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'Most expensive rail in your supply chain' is bold and likely false against shipping/freight, which a skeptic will catch immediately. The 19K annual extrapolation assumes 12 monthly 20K wires, which is a strong assumption for the average SMB importer. 'PayPal makes more money the bigger your orders get' is a true mechanism but framed conspiratorially. The compounding annual math will draw the most challenge in this set.

  • Most expensive rail? Shipping cost me 5K per container minimum.
  • 12 wires of 20K a year? That's 240K sourcing. Big SME already.
  • PayPal got richer? They making business by charging. Same as bank.
  • Where 4.4% confirmed? Show me PayPal fee schedule.
  • Percentage goes down on WorldFirst? At what volume? Same rate at 200 and 20K?
#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 138 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's the problem most business owners don't see when they scale a PayPal habit. The fee scales linearly. The pain doesn't. At 200 bucks, the percentage feels like nothing because it's wrapped inside a fixed-fee floor. At 20 thousand, the percentage is the whole story. 4.4% on 20K is 880 dollars. The app didn't get fancier. The app didn't add features. The app just multiplied the same number against a bigger payment. And on top of that, PayPal adds a 3 to 4% FX margin when you convert to CNY. That's another 600 to 800 dollars buried in the rate. So your 20K supplier payment actually costs you closer to 1,580. The mechanism is volume mismatch. PayPal was built for retail, not factory orders. Stop. WorldFirst is built for business volume. Near-interbank rates. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the problem most business owners don't see when they scale a PayPal habit. The fee scales linearly. The pain doesn't. At 200 bucks, the percentage feels like nothing because it's wrapped inside a fixed-fee floor. At 20 thousand, the percentage is the whole story. 4.4% on 20K is 880 dollars. The app didn't get fancier. The app didn't add features. The app just multiplied the same number against a bigger payment. And on top of that, PayPal adds a 3 to 4% FX margin when you convert to CNY. That's another 600 to 800 dollars buried in the rate. So your 20K supplier payment actually costs you closer to 1,580. The mechanism is volume mismatch. PayPal was built for retail, not factory orders. Stop. WorldFirst is built for business volume. Near-interbank rates. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Most defensible of the Hormozi set. The 'fee scales linearly, pain doesn't' framing is a clean teaching moment that doesn't rely on the most aggressive numbers. Uses a 3 to 4% range on FX (more honest than a single figure). Uses 'closer to 1,580' which signals an estimate rather than a precise claim. 'The app didn't get fancier' is a smart point most skeptics will accept. Volume mismatch is the right framing for the angle. Lowest dramatic flourish, highest credibility.

  • Fee scale linearly pain not, ok this teaching I can use.
  • 3 to 4% FX is more honest. At least give a range.
  • 1,580 on 20K is what 7.9%? Still feel high but ok if true.
  • WorldFirst built for business volume, can verify? Got minimum?
  • Who regulate WorldFirst in Malaysia?

Stop using PayPal / right-tool reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Pure original

Stop using PayPal to pay Chinese suppliers. I'm serious. PayPal is a tool. It's the wrong tool. You wouldn't use a calculator app to do your taxes. So why are you using a consumer wallet to send factory-scale payments? Here's what happens. At 200 dollars you pay 9 bucks. Fine. At 20K you pay 880. Painful. Add 3.5% FX margin on the CNY conversion. Another 700. Now your supplier adds 4 to 5% to the PI because they're eating a receiving fee on their side. That's another 800 to 1000 baked into your invoice. Your 20K order really costs you 22 grand. WorldFirst is built for this. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. No PI surcharge from suppliers because they actually want to receive on this rail. Use the right tool for the job. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

At 20K you pay 880. Painful. Add 3.5% FX margin on the CNY conversion. Another 700. Now your supplier adds 4 to 5% to the PI because they're eating a receiving fee on their side. That's another 800 to 1000 baked into your invoice. Your 20K order really costs you 22 grand. WorldFirst is built for this. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. No PI surcharge from suppliers because they actually want to receive on this rail. Use the right tool for the job. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The calculator/taxes analogy works. Math is internally consistent (880 + 700 + 800-1000 surcharge = roughly 2,400 = the 22K total). 'No PI surcharge because suppliers actually want to receive on this rail' is a strong claim that a skeptic would want to verify, but it's not implausible because findings note suppliers reluctant to accept PayPal. 'Right tool for the job' is solid framing. Main risk is the supplier PI surcharge as universal.

  • Calculator vs taxes ok analogy fair. But show real WorldFirst rate first.
  • Supplier want to receive on WorldFirst rail? Why? Got source for this?
  • 22K real cost on 20K order? That's 10%+ which feels stacked.
  • My supplier never quote me surcharge. Don't make assumption please.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Got license in Malaysia?
#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 138 words

Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about PayPal. It's not the fixed fee that gets you. It's the percentage that doesn't shrink as you scale. At 200 dollars you pay 9 bucks. At 20K you pay 880. The app stayed the same. Your payment got bigger. PayPal got richer. Then there's the 3 to 4% FX margin on CNY conversion. That's 600 to 800 more. Silently baked into the rate. Then your supplier prices in their own PayPal receiving fee, quietly adding 4 to 5% to your PI. None of this is on a receipt. None of this is in the app. This is how it works. Every single time you scale up. PayPal doesn't change the percentage. So you better. WorldFirst is built for business-volume payments. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The rate you see is the rate you get. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling you about PayPal. It's not the fixed fee that gets you. It's the percentage that doesn't shrink as you scale. At 200 dollars you pay 9 bucks. At 20K you pay 880. The app stayed the same. Your payment got bigger. PayPal got richer. Then there's the 3 to 4% FX margin on CNY conversion. That's 600 to 800 more. SilentlyQuietly baked into the rate. Then your supplier prices in their own PayPal receiving fee, quietly adding 4 to 5% to your PI. None of this is on a receipt. None of this is in the app. This is how it works. Every single time you scale up. PayPal doesn't change the percentage. So you better. WorldFirst is built for business-volume paymentsis built differently. Near-interbank rate. Same-day to Chinese accounts. The rate you see is the rate you get. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Softened from 'truth nobody's telling you' to 'something nobody's telling you' in iteration, reduces conspiratorial tone. The 'percentage that doesn't shrink as you scale' teaching is genuinely useful. The cascade of claims (transaction + FX + supplier surcharge) is technically supported but stacks. 'Every time you scale up. PayPal doesn't change the percentage' is largely accurate. 'The rate you see is the rate you get' on WF side is a marketing claim worth verifying.

  • Percentage doesn't shrink as you scale, ok this teaching point is fair.
  • Quietly baked into the rate, true for FX margin. Industry standard practice.
  • Supplier price in 4-5%? Some do some don't. Not universal.
  • Rate you see is rate you get on WorldFirst, prove with live screenshot.
  • Need to look up WorldFirst, never heard of them in Malaysia.
Card P2-A14Pillar P2Short-form 60sTopic 04Category
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 140 words

Cold open + mechanism stack

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. You offered your supplier a credit card. They asked for T/T. You thought they were being difficult. They weren't. Here's the actual stack. One. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers have card acceptance set up. Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B. Most factories were never wired for Visa. Two. The 2.95% processing fee comes out of the supplier's margin, not yours. They're not paying that on a 50K order. Three. The 180-day chargeback window means if you dispute, the money gets clawed back after materials are already cut. Four. Alipay and WeChat Pay are the standard. That's what every other buyer uses. Period. Stop blaming the supplier. The card was built for Amazon checkouts, not factory POs. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY on the rails they already use. No 180-day risk. No 3% fee on their side. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. You offered your supplier a credit card. They asked for T/T. You thought they were being difficult. They weren't. Here's the actual stack. One. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers have card acceptance set up. Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B. Most factories were never wired for Visa. Two. The 2.95% processing fee comes out of the supplier's margin, not yours. They're not paying that on a 50K order. Three. The 180-day chargeback window means if you dispute, the money gets clawed back after materials are already cut. Four. Alipay and WeChat Pay are the standard. That's what every other buyer uses. Period. Stop blaming the supplier. TheThe card was built for Amazon checkouts, not factory POs. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY on the rails they already use. No 180-day risk. No 3% fee on their side. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Four-point stack is structurally clean and each point maps to a real mechanism (acceptance rate, fee incidence, chargeback window, prevailing rails). The 2.95% fee figure matches Alibaba published rates. 'Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B' is accurate. Main risk: '50K order' as a hypothetical is fine but the supplier-bears-fee framing oversimplifies. On Trade Assurance, Alibaba absorbs some risk via escrow. Skeptic with Alipay experience may push back on 'rails they already use' as vague.

  • Supplier pays the 2.95% not me? Sure or not? On my Alibaba card transactions buyer side also charged.
  • Trade Assurance card payment got escrow lah. Not the same as direct card.
  • Where the 3 to 5% acceptance number come from? Show me the study.
  • Most factories never wired for Visa, true. But that's why I use Trade Assurance.
  • WorldFirst on Alipay rails? They got partnership or just messaging?
#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 123 words

Real talk + supplier's view

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatLow

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk for every business owner sourcing from China. You're treating the credit card like a payment method. Your supplier is treating it like a liability. You see 2.95% as a fee you'll happily eat. Your supplier sees 2.95% coming out of their margin because the merchant pays card fees, not the cardholder. You see chargeback protection. Your supplier sees 180 days of their bank reversing the money on goods they already shipped. You see Visa. Your supplier sees a system 95% of their factory peers don't even use. The card isn't bad. It's just not for this. I see this every week. Every single week. Stop. WorldFirst pays Chinese suppliers in CNY on Alipay, WeChat, and local bank rails. The systems they actually use. Search WorldFirst. Now.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Real talk for everyFor every business owner sourcing from China. You're treating the credit card like a payment method. Your supplier is treating it like a liability. You see 2.95% as a fee you'll happily eat. Your supplier sees 2.95% coming out of their margin because the merchant pays card fees, not the cardholder. You see chargeback protection. Your supplier sees 180 days of their bank reversing the money on goods they already shipped. You see Visa. Your supplier sees a system 95% of their factory peers don't even use. The card isn't bad. Cleanest script in the set. The buyer-vs-supplier perspective contrast is empathetic and educational. 'Merchant pays card fees, not the cardholder' is technically accurate and a genuine teaching moment most buyers miss. 'A system 95% of their factory peers don't even use' is the only mild overreach. 'I see this every week' was softened to 'This happens every week' which removes the consultant-claim. Soft on specific numbers but rich on mechanism. Most defensible script in the GV set.

  • Merchant pays card fees not cardholder, ok this part I actually didn't know. Useful.
  • 95% of factory peers don't use Visa, this is direct card right? Trade Assurance different.
  • Buyer vs supplier perspective, this is the best take in the set actually. Empathy wins.
  • This happens every week, bit much. Maybe every few months for me.
  • WorldFirst on Alipay WeChat local bank rails, which exactly? Partnership?
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 144 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. If you're a business owner trying to pay Chinese suppliers with a credit card, you're going to get rejected. Here's why. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers accept cards directly. The other 95% run on Alipay or WeChat Pay. So when you offer Visa, your supplier hears 'I want to pay you with a tool you can't accept.' Then it gets worse. A credit card gives you 180 days to dispute the charge. On goods that already shipped. To a factory that already spent the money on materials. So for the supplier, your card isn't a payment. It's a loan with a return policy. Stop. The card isn't broken. The category was built for retail, not factory orders. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese suppliers on the same rails they already use. No 180-day chargeback risk. No 3% fee on their side. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. If you're a business owner trying to pay Chinese suppliers with a credit card, you're going to get rejected. Here's why. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese suppliers accept cards directly. The other 95% run on Alipay or WeChat Pay. So when you offer Visa, your supplier hears 'I want to pay you with a tool you can't accept.' Then it gets worse. A credit card gives you 180 days to dispute the charge. On goods that already shipped. To a factory that already spent the money on materials. So for the supplier, your card isn't a payment. It's a loan with a return policy. Stop. The card isn't broken. The category was built for retail, not factory orders. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese suppliers on the same rails they already use. No 180-day chargeback risk. No 3% fee on their side. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core mechanism is real and well-sourced. The 3 to 5% direct card acceptance figure is consistent with industry estimates for Chinese B2B. The 180-day chargeback window is accurate for most major card schemes. 'Loan with a return policy' is metaphor, defensible. Main risk: the WF claim is broad ('CNY direct on the same rails') without naming the exact rail. A skeptic with card experience on Alibaba Trade Assurance will note that escrowed card payments DO work in some cases, so the 'you're going to get rejected' opener is slightly absolute.

  • I paid by card on Alibaba Trade Assurance lah. Worked fine. So 'going to get rejected' is wrong what.
  • 3 to 5% where you get this number? Got source?
  • 180 day chargeback for B2B? My card company never explain like that.
  • Who is WorldFirst? First time hearing. Got Malaysia license?
  • Same rails as Alipay? Means you also tier with Ant or what?
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 142 words

Accusation flip / it's not the supplier

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

You thought your supplier was being unreasonable. They weren't. You were using the wrong tool. You offered a credit card. Your supplier said T/T. You assumed it was about fees. It wasn't. Here's what your supplier actually saw. A card payment with a 180-day chargeback window. On a 50K order. Where they already paid for raw materials in week one. If you dispute in month five, they lose the order, lose the inventory, and lose the materials cost. That's not payment. That's a loan with a return policy. Plus 95% of Chinese factories don't even have card terminals. They run on Alipay and WeChat. I'll say it. The supplier wasn't being difficult. The category was built for retail, not factory orders. WorldFirst sends CNY directly to your supplier on the rails they use every day. No chargeback. No 3% fee on their margin. Stop blaming the supplier. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

You thought your supplier was being unreasonable. They weren't. You were using the wrong tool. You offered a credit card. Your supplier said T/T. You assumed it was about fees. It wasn't. Here's what your supplier actually saw. A card payment with a 180-day chargeback window. On a 50K order. Where they already paid for raw materials in week one. If you dispute in month five, they lose the order, lose the inventory, and lose the materials cost. That's not payment. That's a loan with a return policy. Plus 95% of Chinese factories don't even have card terminals. They run on Alipay and WeChat. I'll say it. The supplier wasn't being difficult. The category was built for retail, not factory orders. WorldFirst sends CNY directly to your supplier on the rails they use every day. No chargeback. No 3% fee on their margin. Stop blaming the supplier. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The flip (it's not the supplier, it's the category) is the strongest creative angle and defensible. Materials timeline (raw materials week one, dispute month five) is a real and underappreciated risk for suppliers. Main risk: '95% of Chinese factories don't even have card terminals' is a strong claim that overstates the source finding (the actual finding is bank card terminals are rare in B2B). Card acceptance via Alibaba escrow exists for many of those same factories indirectly. Skeptic may also push back on 'they run on Alipay and WeChat' for B2B factory wires, where T/T to corporate bank account is also very common.

  • My factory pays salary and ops via bank transfer lah, not WeChat. WeChat for retail mostly.
  • 95% no terminal, where this come from? My supplier in Yiwu got QR code everywhere.
  • Material lose in month 5 chargeback ok this part interesting. Got real case?
  • Trade Assurance escrow handle the chargeback risk lah. Not all card payment same.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Why I trust you over Alibaba?
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 149 words

Direct address + 3-thing stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you've ever offered a credit card to a Chinese supplier and got told no, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three things are happening at once. One. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories accept Visa or Mastercard. Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B. The infrastructure isn't there. Two. The 2.95% processing fee gets eaten by the supplier, not you. On a 12K order that's 360 bucks coming out of their margin. They're not absorbing that. Three. The 180-day chargeback window means if you dispute next quarter, they lose everything. Materials gone. Inventory gone. Their bank claws the money back. So your supplier isn't being stubborn. They're protecting their business. Stop trying to force the card. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to your supplier on Alipay, WeChat, and bank rails. The systems they actually use. No chargeback. No 3% fee on their side. If you're paying China, WorldFirst is the tool. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

If you've ever offered a credit card to a Chinese supplier and got told no, you need to hear this. Real talk. Three things are happening at once. One. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories accept Visa or Mastercard. Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B. The infrastructure isn't there. Two. The 2.95% processing fee gets eaten by the supplier, not you. On a 12K order that's 360 bucks coming out of their margin. They're not absorbing that. Three. The 180-day chargeback window means if you dispute next quarter, they lose everything. Materials gone. Inventory gone. Their bank claws the money back. So your supplier isn't being stubborn. They're protecting their business. Stop trying to force the card. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to your supplier on Alipay, WeChat, and bank rails. The systems they actually use. No chargeback. No 3% fee on their side. If you're paying China, WorldFirst is the tool. Period. WorldFirst is worth a look. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The 3-thing stack is teachable and the math on a 12K order (360 fee) is internally consistent. 'Bank card terminals are rare in Chinese B2B' is accurate. The 'protecting their business' framing is empathetic and lands well. Main red flag: 'supplier eats the 2.95% fee' is technically true for direct card acceptance but ignores Trade Assurance scenarios where Alibaba absorbs the dispute risk. A skeptic who's done Trade Assurance card payments will push back. 'They claw the money back' is dramatic but factually accurate for chargebacks.

  • 12K order 360 fee, math check out. But still on Alibaba I never pay 360 extra leh.
  • 3 to 5% acceptance is direct card right? Trade Assurance got different model.
  • Supplier eat the 2.95%, where the source? My Alibaba transaction showed buyer fee.
  • Worth a look ok but who is WorldFirst? Owned by Ant Group? Trust factor.
  • Protecting their business, this empathy line is good. First time I see content explain supplier side.
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 134 words

I'll say it opinion-as-truth

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. The credit card is the most overrated tool in cross-border supplier payments and nobody wants to admit it. You offered Visa. Your supplier said T/T. You blamed the supplier. The supplier wasn't the problem. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card acceptance. 95% have run on Alipay or WeChat for a decade. The card isn't part of their world. Plus the supplier eats the 2.95% fee. Plus they carry 180 days of chargeback risk. On goods they already shipped. So when you push the card, you're asking them to take less money and more risk for the same order. WorldFirst pays them in CNY on the rails they actually use. No fee on their side. No 180-day risk. The card category was built for retail. Factory orders are a different game. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

I'll say it. The credit card is the most overrated tool in cross-border supplier payments and nobody wants to admit it. You offered Visa. Your supplier said T/T. You blamed the supplier. The supplier wasn't the problem. Only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card acceptance. 95% have run on Alipay or WeChat for a decade. The card isn't part of their world. Plus the supplier eats the 2.95% fee. Plus they carry 180 days of chargeback risk. On goods they already shipped. So when you push the card, you're asking them to take less money and more risk for the same order. WorldFirst pays them in CNY on the rails they actually use. No fee on their side. No 180-day risk. The card category was built for retail. Factory orders are a different game. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'Most overrated tool in cross-border supplier payments' is a bold opinion framed as opinion, which is defensible Gary Vee voice. The supplier-side framing (less money + more risk for same order) is the sharpest insight in the set. Math holds: supplier eats 2.95%, takes 180-day chargeback risk. Main red flag: 'never been part of their world' is rhetorical and easy to challenge with one counter-example. Trade Assurance card workflow is a real exception.

  • Most overrated tool? Shipping cost overrate me lah, not card fee.
  • Trade Assurance card on Alibaba is fine for me. So 'not part of their world' is too strong.
  • Less money more risk same order, ok this framing actually clean.

#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 164 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's what most business owners don't understand about credit cards and Chinese suppliers. The card isn't the problem. The category is. Credit cards were built for retail. You buy a sweater, you don't like it, you dispute, you get your money back. Beautiful. Now apply that to a factory order. You commission 10,000 units. The factory buys raw materials in week one. Cuts fabric in week three. Ships in week eight. You receive in week twelve. And the card gives you 180 days to dispute the whole thing. After materials are gone. After production is finished. After the ship has left port. No supplier on earth is going to accept that risk for a 2.95% fee they have to absorb. Stop. 95% of Chinese suppliers don't even have card terminals. They run on Alipay, WeChat Pay, and bank transfers. That's the category. WorldFirst sends CNY direct on the same rails. No chargeback risk for the supplier. No 3% fee they have to swallow. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's what most business owners don't understand about credit cards and Chinese suppliers. The card isn't the problem. The category is. Credit cards were built for retail. You buy a sweater, you don't like it, you dispute, you get your money back. Beautiful. Now apply that to a factory order. You commission 10,000 units. The factory buys raw materials in week one. Cuts fabric in week three. Ships in week eight. You receive in week twelve. And the card gives you 180 days to dispute the whole thing. After materials are gone. After production is finished. After the ship has left port. No supplier on earth is going to accept that risk for a 2.95% fee they have to absorb. Stop. 95% of Chinese suppliers don't even have card terminals. They run on Alipay, WeChat Pay, and bank transfers. That's the category. WorldFirst sends CNY direct on the same rails. No chargeback risk for the supplier. No 3% fee they have to swallow. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Cleanest of the Hormozi set. The retail-vs-wholesale category argument is genuinely good teaching and defensible. The sweater-dispute analogy reduces jargon. Timeline (week one materials, week eight ship, week twelve receive) is realistic for typical orders. The 2.95% supplier-side burden is consistent with Alibaba's published merchant fees. 'No supplier on earth' is the only overreach since some larger factories with international setup DO take cards. Main red flag remaining: 'CNY direct on the same rails' is still vague on the WF side.

  • Card built for retail, this part actually make sense. Good teaching.
  • No supplier on earth take card? My larger supplier in Shenzhen accept card no problem.
  • Week 12 still inside the 180 day chargeback, ok now I understand why supplier scared.
  • WorldFirst CNY direct on same rails, what rails exactly? Show me.
  • If supplier eat 2.95% how come some Alibaba sellers still happy with card?

Right-tool reframe

Soc4.0 Aud4.2 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatMedium

Hammer/wood analogy is plain English and lands. 'Use the right tool for the job' is generic but clean.

Pure original

Stop trying to pay Chinese suppliers with a credit card. I'm serious. The card is a tool. It's the wrong tool. It was built for retail checkouts. Sweater doesn't fit, dispute, money back. Beautiful. Useless for factory orders. Here's what your supplier sees. 3 to 5% of Chinese factories accept cards. The other 95% run on Alipay and WeChat. The supplier eats the 2.95% fee. The factory floor isn't a retail counter. Search WorldFirst. Now.

The other 95% run on Alipay and WeChat. The supplier eats the 2.95% fee. The supplier carries 180 days of dispute risk on goods they already shipped.   • 10K order 300 supplier margin, math check. But my supplier still accept on Trade Assurance.
  • Five months cash exposed, this is good reframe. Never thought from supplier POV.
  • WorldFirst no fee on supplier side, what about my side? Got top up min? Subscription?

#10 PG4 Gary Vee Opinion-led 129 words

Truth nobody's telling you

Soc4.2 Aud4.1 Sales4.0 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'Your bank doesn't make money on the wire fee. They make money on the exchange rate.' = the kind of line a peer would quote. CTA soft — no explicit 'Search WorldFirst'.

Pure original

Here's the truth nobody's telling you about credit cards and Chinese suppliers. The card was never meant for this. The card was built for B2C. Coffee shops. Online stores. Hotel rooms. Places where the buyer walks away with the product on day one and disputes if it's broken. Now look at a factory order. 90-day production. 30-day shipping. 180-day chargeback window that outlasts both. Your supplier ships first, then waits five months to know if the money is real. Meanwhile only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card terminals. The other 95% are on Alipay and WeChat. So when you push the card, you're forcing a retail tool onto a wholesale workflow. WorldFirst is built for the wholesale workflow. CNY direct to the supplier. Rails they already use. No chargeback drama. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the truthsomething nobody's telling you about credit cards and Chinese suppliers. The card was never meant for this. The card was built for B2C. Coffee shops. Online stores. Hotel rooms. Places where the buyer walks away with the product on day one and disputes if it's broken. Now look at a factory order. 90-day production. 30-day shipping. 180-day chargeback window that outlasts both. Your supplier ships first, then waits five months to know if the money is real. Meanwhile only 3 to 5% of Chinese factories even have card terminals. The other 95% are on Alipay and WeChat. So when you push the card, you're forcing a retail tool onto a wholesale workflow. WorldFirst is built for the wholesale workflowbuilt differently. CNY direct to the supplier. Rails they already use. No chargeback drama. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Strongest concept in the GV set. The B2C-vs-B2B category argument is genuinely good teaching. The timeline math (90-day production + 30-day shipping = 120 days, well inside the 180-day chargeback window) is accurate and defensible. Coffee shops/online stores/hotel examples make the retail framing concrete. Main risks: 'never meant for this' is rhetorical and a skeptic with Trade Assurance experience will note that Alibaba designed a card-with-escrow workflow that explicitly addresses this. 'Forcing a retail tool onto a wholesale workflow' is sharp.

  • 90-day production 30-day shipping 180-day chargeback, math check. Good visual.
  • Card never meant for this, ok but Alibaba Trade Assurance designed card workflow for this exact case.
  • Coffee shops hotel rooms example, good. Made it click.
  • WorldFirst built differently, fine but show me how on a real transaction.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Need to know regulator before I trust.
Card P2-A18Pillar P2Short-form 60sTopic 04Math-led
#1 PH2 Alex Hormozi PAS 126 words

Cold open + compounding math

Soc4.5 Aud4.2 Sales4.3 Overall4.3 HeatHigh

Compounding maths (1.2K → 28.8K/yr → 144K/5yr) lands hard. 'A salary. A warehouse. Your kid's college fund.' = concrete stakes.

Pure original

Watch this. You're paying Chinese suppliers 50,000 dollars a month. You're using a personal-tier transfer tool because it was easy to set up. Your spread is around 1.5 percent above what a business platform charges. 1.5 percent of 50,000 is 750 dollars. Per month. 750 dollars. Multiply by twelve months. That's 9,000 dollars a year. Disappearing inside the rate. Never appearing on a single line item. Never showing up as a fee. Just a slightly worse exchange rate every single transfer for an entire year. Over three years, 27,000 dollars. Over five, 45,000. That's a whole hire. That's a container of stock. That's your margin on an entire product line. Stop. The fix is one tier change. Business-tier FX quotes on a business-tier platform. WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Watch this. You're paying Chinese suppliers 50,000 dollars a month. You're using a personal-tier transfer tool because it was easy to set up. Your spread is around 1.5 percent above what a business platform charges. 1.5 percent of 50,000 is 750 dollars. Per month. 750 dollars. Multiply by twelve months. That's 9,000 dollars a year. Disappearing inside the rate. Never appearing on a single line item. Never showing up as a fee. Just a slightly worse exchange rate every single transfer for an entire year. Over three years, 27,000 dollars. Over five, 45,000. That's a whole hire. That's a container of stock. That's your margin on an entire product line. Stop. The fix is one tier change. Business-tier FX quotes on a business-tier platform. WorldFirst. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The compounding math (1.5% of 50K = 750/mo, then 9K/yr, then 27K/3yr, 45K/5yr) is presented as universal when it's a stacked hypothetical. The 1.5% gap is the midpoint of the spread range from the findings but treating it as flat-rate every transfer ignores corridor and volume variation. 'WorldFirst' as the fix is product-true but the magnitude of savings claim is the trust risk. 5-year extrapolation always smells like ad copy to a skeptical viewer.

  • 45k over 5 years? Math too perfect lah, my business not even sure to last 5 years.
  • 1.5% gap every single transfer? My OCBC business account give me 0.7% on big wire. Where this 1.5% come from?
  • WorldFirst got Malaysia license or not? Important. Wise was blocked before, scared to switch again.
  • 9k a year sound a lot but my supplier payments not that big also. Number not relate to my size.
  • One hire? One container? Dramatic la. Just show me rate side by side.
#2 PG5 Gary Vee Opinion-led 143 words

Real talk + escalating personal energy

Soc4.4 Aud4.3 Sales4.2 Overall4.3 HeatMedium

Call-the-bank scenario is the most relatable in the batch. 'You sleep' close is a strong payoff line.

Pure original

Real talk for every business owner who's ever stared at a transfer receipt and thought the math felt off. You're not crazy. The rate you saw on Google before you signed off, that's not the rate you got. The rate your bank app showed you, that already had a 2 to 4 percent margin baked in. You don't see that margin anywhere on the receipt. It's not a fee. It's not a charge. It's just a slightly worse rate quote than the one banks use among themselves. Run that 2 percent across a year of supplier payments. On 50,000 a month, the gap between a personal-tier spread and a business-tier spread is 9,000 dollars. Per year. Vanishing inside the rate quote. I see this every single week with business owners who didn't even know there was a second tier. Stop accepting retail spreads on business volume. WorldFirst quotes business-tier by default. Search WorldFirst. Now.

You don't see that margin anywhere on the receipt. It's not a fee. It's not a charge. It's just a slightly worse rate quote than the one banks use among themselves. Trust risk mainly from the 'every single week' line which is mild exaggeration.

  • Stared at receipt and math felt off, this is real. Felt this before.
  • Second tier exists, useful idea. But how I qualify for business tier with small volume?
  • Who is WorldFirst? Got license in Malaysia? Important question.
  • 9k a year disappearing in rate quote, ok dramatic. But my volume smaller, save how much?
  • Every single week, mild over-claim but the relatable frame work better than conspiracy tone.
#3 PH1 Alex Hormozi PAS 130 words

Classic PAS opener

Soc4.2 Aud4.0 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

'This is not a fee problem. This is a hidden margin problem.' is a strong reframe line. Clean PAS rhythm intact after polish.

Pure original

Listen. Open Google right now. Type THB to CNY. Whatever number it shows you, that rate does not exist for your wallet. That's the mid-market rate. It's the rate banks use among themselves. The rate you actually get is a different number, a worse number, and the difference is called the spread. On a personal tool, that spread is usually 2 to 4 percent. On a business platform, it's 0.3 to 1 percent. Same currency pair. Same day. Same minute. Two different rates. On a 20,000 dollar supplier wire, 2 percent is 400 dollars. 0.5 percent is 100 dollars. That's 300 dollars that disappeared inside the rate. No line item. No fee notification. Just a worse number than Google showed you. Stop. WorldFirst quotes business-tier rates direct to business owners. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Listen. Open Google right now. Type THB to CNY. Whatever number it shows you, that rate does not exist for your wallet. That's the mid-market rate. It's the rate banks use among themselves. The rate you actually get is a different number, a worse number, and the difference is called the spread. On a personal tool, that spread is usually 2 to 4 percent. On a business platform, it's 0.3 to 1 percent. Same currency pair. Same day. Same minute. Two different rates. On a 20,000 dollar supplier wire, 2 percent is 400 dollars. 0.5 percent is 100 dollars. That's 300 dollars that disappeared inside the rate. No line item. No fee notification. Just a worse number than Google showed you. Stop. WorldFirst quotes business-tier rates direct to business owners. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Core mechanism (mid-market vs retail rate, two tiers running in parallel) is technically accurate and matches the findings: 2-4% consumer spread, 0.3-1% business spread. The 'on 20K wire' math (400 vs 100 = 300 saved) is consistent with the findings note. Weakest beat is 'WorldFirst quotes business-tier rates direct to business owners' being slightly absolute; actual rate depends on volume tier and corridor. The 'rate does not exist for your wallet' line is rhetorically strong but technically right.

  • 300 dollars saved on 20k? My last wire from Maybank was not that bad lah, maybe 0.8% only.
  • Who is WorldFirst? Never see this brand in Malaysia ads or in my forwarder's recommendation.
  • Mid-market rate concept ok but where the proof the retail spread really 2-4%? Show one screenshot.
  • Business-tier rate, but how big my volume need to be? My monthly only 8k USD, qualify or not?
  • Confusing lah, two rates same time, sound like a magic trick. Real or marketing?
#4 PH3 Alex Hormozi PAS 138 words

Accusation opener + brutal specificity

Soc4.4 Aud3.9 Sales4.3 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

Accusation opener is visceral. 'You signed off on it' might still feel slightly attacking — close to the line for SEA register.

Pure original

Your bank stole 300 dollars from your last supplier wire and you can't even find it on the receipt. Here's why. You sent 20,000 dollars to your factory in CNY. Your bank's app showed you a rate. You signed off. The wire went through. What you didn't see is that the rate they used was 2 percent worse than the mid-market rate Google would have shown you. 2 percent of 20,000 is 400 dollars. A business-tier platform on the same wire would have charged 0.5 percent. That's 100 dollars. The difference, 300 dollars, sat inside the spread. Not labelled. Not itemised. Not flagged. Just baked into the rate quote on screen. They didn't lie. They just quoted you the retail tier when you should have been on the business tier. Stop signing off on personal-tier wires for business volume. WorldFirst is built for business-tier spreads. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Your bank stolepocketed 300 dollars from your last supplier wire and you can't even find it on the receipt. Here's why. You sent 20,000 dollars to your factory in CNY. Your bank's app showed you a rate. You signed off. The wire went through. What you didn't see is that the rate they used was 2 percent worse than the mid-market rate Google would have shown you. 2 percent of 20,000 is 400 dollars. A business-tier platform on the same wire would have charged 0.5 percent. That's 100 dollars. The difference, 300 dollars, sat inside the spread. Not labelled. Not itemised. Not flagged. Just baked into the rate quote on screen. They didn't lie. They just quoted you the retail tier when you should have been on the business tier. Stop signing off on personal-tier wires for business volume. WorldFirst is built for business-tier spreads. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

'Your bank pocketed 300 dollars' framed as fact when it's a specific hypothetical wire. The 2% personal vs 0.5% business comparison is within the findings range but presenting them as the exact numbers any viewer paid creates trust risk if their real spread was different. 'They quoted you the retail tier when you should have been on the business tier' is fair commentary but assumes the viewer's bank actually has a business tier they qualify for.

  • Pocketed 300? My last 20k wire only shown 0.6% spread, that's 120, not 300. Number very different.
  • Business tier on my bank requires minimum 100k monthly volume, I dont hit. So advice not for me?
  • Never heard WorldFirst before. Cobi or who present this? No face also weird.
  • Built for business-tier spreads, ok but what is the actual WorldFirst rate? Just give number la.
  • Bank didnt lie they just quoted retail tier, fair point. But where the proof of WF being cheaper?
#5 PH5 Alex Hormozi PAS 151 words

Direct address + problem stack

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatMedium

1-2-3 structural reveal works well. Listing what you saw vs didn't see is clean teaching.

Pure original

If you pay Chinese suppliers, you need to hear this. Real talk. The rate on Google is not the rate you get. The rate on your bank's app is not the rate you get either. There are two FX rates running side by side every minute of every day. The mid-market rate, which banks trade among themselves. And the retail rate, which is what gets quoted to you. The retail rate has a margin built in. On personal tier, 2 to 4 percent. On business tier, 0.3 to 1 percent. That gap is the spread. It's never a line item. Never a fee notification. Just a slightly worse number than what Google showed you. On a 20,000 dollar supplier wire that gap is 300 dollars. On 50,000 a month for a year, it's 9,000 dollars. Stop running business volume through personal-tier spreads. WorldFirst quotes business-tier rates by default. If you pay suppliers in China, WorldFirst is worth checking. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

If you pay Chinese suppliers, you need to hear this. Real talk. The rate on Google is not the rate you get. The rate on your bank's app is not the rate you get either. There are two FX rates running side by side every minute of every day. The mid-market rate, which banks trade among themselves. And the retail rate, which is what gets quoted to you. The retail rate has a margin built in. On personal tier, 2 to 4 percent. On business tier, 0.3 to 1 percent. That gap is the spread. It's never a line item. Never a fee notification. Just a slightly worse number than what Google showed you. On a 20,000 dollar supplier wire that gap is 300 dollars. On 50,000 a month for a year, it's 9,000 dollars. Stop running business volume through personal-tier spreads. WorldFirst quotes business-tier rates by default. If you pay suppliers in China, WorldFirst is worth checking. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

Combines mechanism (two rates, the spread) with specific numbers ($300 on 20K, $9K/yr on 50K/mo) which is consistent with the findings. 'Worth checking' softens the CTA appropriately. Weakest beat is generalising the 2-4% / 0.3-1% to all banks and all corridors as if it applies to every Malaysian importer the same way. SEA bank rates vary considerably by corridor (CNH especially has wider spreads).

  • 9k a year on 50k a month, ok the math add up. But my monthly is 12k only, save how much for me?
  • Worth checking is a softer ask, ok I respect. But still who is WorldFirst, never see in Malaysia.
  • Spread concept makes sense but my Maybank wire say 'no FX fee'. So got spread or not?
  • Two FX rates running side by side, this part useful actually. Wish my bank told me earlier.
  • Worth a look better than push-to-download. Less aggressive, more trustable.
#6 PG2 Gary Vee Opinion-led 130 words

I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math

Soc4.3 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.2 HeatHigh

'Your bank is the most expensive part of your supply chain' is the sharpest single line in this batch. Tight at 97 words.

Pure original

I'll say it. The exchange rate you see is not real for you. The rate on Google is the wholesale rate. The rate banks use when they trade with each other. You're not in that club. You see retail. Retail has a margin built in. On consumer transfer tools, that margin is 2 to 4 percent. On business-grade platforms, 0.3 to 1 percent. Same pair. Same second. Two different prices. If you're sending 50,000 a month to suppliers and you're on a consumer tool, you're eating roughly 1.5 percent more than a business tool would charge. 1.5 percent of 50,000 is 750. Per month. 9,000 a year. Disappearing inside the rate. Never showing up as a fee. WorldFirst quotes business-tier rates. That 9,000 a year goes back into your business. Where it belongs. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

I'll say it. The exchange rate you see is not real for you. The rate on Google is the wholesale rate. The rate banks use when they trade with each other. You're not in that club. You see retail. Retail has a margin built in. On consumer transfer tools, that margin is 2 to 4 percent. On business-grade platforms, 0.3 to 1 percent. Same pair. Same second. Two different prices. If you're sending 50,000 a month to suppliers and you're on a consumer tool, you're eating roughly 1.5 percent more than a business tool would charge. 1.5 percent of 50,000 is 750. Per month. 9,000 a year. Disappearing inside the rate. Never showing up as a fee. WorldFirst quotes business-tier rates. That 9,000 a year goes back into your business. Where it belongs. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The compound claim (1.5% diff on 50K/mo = 750/mo = 9K/yr) is from the findings but presented as guaranteed savings every business owner will see. Reality is the gap varies by corridor and volume. 'You're not in that club' is rhetorically strong but presumptive about the viewer. 'WorldFirst quotes business-tier rates' is broadly accurate but conflates default-tier behaviour with personalised quote.

  • 9k a year guaranteed savings? Sound too good. Got case study or just calculator math?
  • Not in that club, who decide which club I'm in? My company is registered, why am I personal-tier?
  • WorldFirst quote business-tier by default means what? Even small business volume also business-tier?
  • 1.5 percent gap on every transfer feel exaggerated. My OCBC quote 0.8% on bigger volume.
  • Where the money goes back in my business part, dramatic but real. 9k is real money.
#7 PH4 Alex Hormozi PAS 140 words

Mechanism-first reveal

Soc4.1 Aud4.1 Sales4.2 Overall4.1 HeatLow

'The fee is not the fee' = sharp reframe. 'Banks make billions' is a broad claim that could draw pushback.

Pure original

Here's the part of currency exchange most business owners don't understand. There are actually two rates running in parallel at the same moment. Rate one. The mid-market rate. The rate banks quote to each other when they trade currency wholesale. That's the rate Google shows you. Rate two. The retail rate. The rate quoted to anyone outside the wholesale system. That includes you. The retail rate already has a margin baked into it. On personal-tier products that margin is 2 to 4 percent. On business-tier products it's 0.3 to 1 percent. Same currency pair. Same exact second. Two different prices depending on which tier you're on. The retail margin is called the spread. It's how providers make money on FX. They quote you a slightly worse rate than what they're actually paying. Stop accepting retail-tier spreads on business volume. WorldFirst quotes business-tier on every transfer. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Brand-polish iteration deleted / added

Here's the part of currency exchange most business owners don't understand. There are actually two rates running in parallel at the same moment. Rate one. The mid-market rate. The rate banks quote to each other when they trade currency wholesale. That's the rate Google shows you. Rate two. The retail rate. The rate quoted to anyone outside the wholesale system. That includes you. The retail rate already has a margin baked into it. On personal-tier products that margin is 2 to 4 percent. On business-tier products it's 0.3 to 1 percent. Same currency pair. Same exact second. Two different prices depending on which tier you're on. The retail margin is called the spread. It's how providers make money on FX. They quote you a slightly worse rate than what they're actually paying. Stop accepting retail-tier spreads on business volume. WorldFirst quotes business-tier on every transfer. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Skeptic test · what a Malaysian SMB might push back on

The strongest fact-check profile of the Hormozi set. Mechanism explanation (mid-market = wholesale rate banks use among themselves; retail = with margin built in) is correctly explained. Range presentation (2-4% personal, 0.3-1% business) honours the findings without overclaiming a single number. 'WorldFirst quotes business-tier on every transfer' is broadly product-accurate. No specific dollar figure means no single number to challenge.

  • Two rates running parallel, ok now I understand. But how do I check what tier I'm on?
  • Range 2-4% sound more honest than fixed 2%. Still want to see WF rate before I switch.
  • Who is WorldFirst tho. First time hear. Got Bank Negara license here in MY?
  • Wholesale rate concept never explained to me by my bank also. Useful info actually.
  • Business-tier on every transfer means what minimum? Need volume? Need company registration?
